keys: The Reawakening of Magic , the Return of the Old Art ,
value:
The Reawakening of Magic. [See also: John Uskglass, The Raven King's Court, The Pillar of Darkness, The Year Without a Summer.]
It is February 1817, and magic — which several generations of sober, educated Europeans had agreed to regard as superstition — has returned. Two causes are presently identified by those competent to offer an opinion. First: the eruption of Mount Tambora in 1815 thinned the boundary between the mortal world and Faerie with the thoroughness of a geological event that does not consult theology before proceeding. The volcanic winter that starved Europe also blurred the border between mortal reality and Faerie — the same darkness, the same cold. Second: the Raven King's spell — worked through Mr. Strange and Mr. Norrell as its instruments, neither of whom appears fully aware of this — sent shockwaves through Faerie that have reactivated every dormant alliance between court and mortal people simultaneously. Every Fae court believes it is the only legitimate power. None of them coordinated this. They are discovering they are not alone.
¹ The present author, who has observed a portion of what followed, notes that 'not alone' is understating the situation considerably.
title: The Reawakening of Magic
description: This entry explains why magic has suddenly returned across all of Europe in 1817. Two unrelated events — a volcanic eruption in Indonesia and a magical working in England — have collided to tear the barrier between the mortal world and Faerie. Every supernatural tradition on the continent is waking up at once, and no one planned this.

keys: English Practical Magic , the Old English Art ,
value:
English Practical Magic. [See also: John Uskglass, The Raven King's Court, Genius Loci, The King's Roads, The Norrellite School, The Strangite School, The Madness Axis.]
English magic descends from John Uskglass — the Raven King — who returned from Faerie in 1110 commanding ravens and fairy armies to conquer the North of England. He governed three kingdoms simultaneously for three centuries before departing in 1434: Northern England, a country in Faerie, and the Bitter Lands beyond. His alliances with England's genius loci — the spirits inhabiting every rain-shower, stone, river, and tree — persist centuries after his departure. Magic flows through mirrors and the King's Roads, which are fairy paths accessible through any reflective surface. In February 1817, the tradition divides between two schools: the Norrellites, who study without performing, and the Strangites, who perform and occasionally study. A further discovery, pursued recklessly in Venice by Mr. Strange: deliberate madness grants fairy sight. The most powerful Fae are, by mortal standards, essentially insane. English Practical Magic is also practised covertly by women throughout England, sometimes to effect that surpasses the acknowledged masters — a fact the gentlemen magicians prefer not to discuss.
¹ The present author does not adjudicate between the schools. He has, however, seen what Mr. Strange's madness experiments produced, and notes that 'advantage' is not quite the word he would have chosen.
title: English Practical Magic
description: England is where magic came back first, through the work of two magicians: the cautious, scholarly Mr. Norrell and the brilliant, reckless Mr. Strange. As an English practitioner in February 1817, you inherit the tradition of the legendary Raven King — magic drawn from the living spirits of the landscape, accessed through mirrors and fairy roads. Two schools are at open rivalry. You must decide where your loyalties lie.

keys: Celtic Otherworld Magic , the fairy doctors' learning ,
value:
Celtic Otherworld Magic. [See also: The Sídhe Court, The Geis, Tír na nÓg, Fairy Doctors, Bean Sídhe, The Sluagh.]
Celtic magic — the tradition of Ireland, Scotland, Wales, Cornwall, and Brittany — does not command. It negotiates. Power flows from the Aos Sí, remnants of the Tuatha Dé Danann, beings of tremendous age who retreated into hollow hills when iron-bearing mortals arrived and have waited there ever since. What mortals called Lugh and the Dagda were not names of gods but titles — held by Sídhe of sufficient distinction, as a lord might hold an earldom. Practice operates through spoken charms, herb-craft, well-magic at sacred springs, and the mastery of liminal spaces: crossroads, thresholds, shorelines, dusk. Its defining principle is the geis — a supernatural obligation that grants power when observed and brings catastrophic destruction when violated. The return of magic has reactivated ancient geasa on bloodlines that had entirely forgotten they carried them, which is an awkward discovery for all concerned. The Sídhe find it merely amusing.
¹ Professor Hollis records the case of a Skye family who discovered in the autumn of 1816 that their eldest son was under a geis requiring him never to stand in a doorway. The present author has not independently confirmed this account. He finds it plausible.
title: Celtic Otherworld Magic
description: Celtic magic covers Ireland, Scotland, Wales, Cornwall, and Brittany. Rather than commanding supernatural forces, practitioners negotiate with the Sídhe — the ancient fairy beings who never left, only withdrew into hollow hills. The tradition runs on binding oaths, sacred landscape, and liminal moments — thresholds, dusk, the shore where sea meets land. It is older than Christianity in these islands, and it has not forgotten that.

keys: Norse-Germanic Rune Craft , the runic heathenry of the North ,
value:
Norse-Germanic Rune Craft. [See also: The Æsir Court, The One-Eyed Lord, Galdrastafir, The Wild Hunt, Yggdrasil, Völvas and Seiðr.]
Norse magic is cosmic technology powered by sacrifice. The lord of the Æsir Court — what mortals remembered as Odin — is a Fae of extraordinary collection-mania who hung himself from Yggdrasil, the World Tree connecting the Nine Realms, for nine days: not from wisdom, not from duty, but because such a sacrifice was, to him, aesthetically beautiful. He discovered the runes — fundamental forces carved into the structure of reality, not merely symbols — and was pleased with what he found. Three streams serve the tradition: galdr, which treats specific poetic meters as audible mechanisms for reshaping reality; seiðr, fate-manipulation through shamanic ecstasy, traditionally practiced by völvas and considered dangerously feminine for men to attempt; and rune magic, which channels cosmic forces through symbols activated with blood and intent. Icelandic galdrastafir — visual sigils preserved in the Galdrabók — have begun functioning with alarming reliability. The Wild Hunt rides winter storms again.
¹ Those who hear hooves on the wind are advised to bar their doors and extinguish their lights. The One-Eyed Lord does not hunt for any purpose a mortal would recognise as purposeful. He hunts because riding pleases him.
title: Norse-Germanic Rune Craft
description: Norse-Germanic magic draws power from sacrifice, runes, and the vast cosmic machinery of the Nine Realms. Practiced across Scandinavia, Iceland, Germany, and the Netherlands, it serves a Fae court whose lord hanged himself from the World Tree for nine days to discover the runes — forces carved into reality itself. The Wild Hunt rides the winter storms again. The old sacrificial magic is back, and it requires a great deal of blood.

keys: Finno-Ugric Origin-Song Magic , the Finnish singing sorcery ,
value:
Finno-Ugric Origin-Song Magic. [See also: The Väki Court, Tietäjä, Löyly, Metsänpeitto, The Kalevala, Táltos Spirit-Battle.]
Finnish magic — the tradition of Finland, Estonia, the Sámi lands, and Hungary — is the most purely epistemic tradition in Europe: to know the true origin of a thing is to command it. Recite the origin-song of fire, and fire obeys. Sing the lineage of iron, and iron cannot cut you. The Väki Court, what mortals dimly recalled as Tapio and Ahti, are Fae whose power is woven into elemental reality. Contact with the wrong väki causes illness; the sauna's löyly-steam is the proper sacred channel. The Finnish tietäjä practices knowledge as sorcery. The Sámi noaidi travels three cosmic levels in trance, a painted drum serving as a map of the journey. The Hungarian táltos is born marked — teeth present at birth, extra fingers, or a caul — and at age seven undergoes a terrifying initiatory ordeal before gaining the ability to fight other táltos in animal form to influence weather. The runo-songs of the Kalevala are, in February 1817, operationally functional.
¹ The present author did not believe this when first told. He has since revised his position. He does not recommend singing the origin of fire indoors.
title: Finno-Ugric Origin-Song Magic
description: Finno-Ugric magic works through knowledge of origins. If you know where something truly came from — fire, iron, water, illness — you can command it. This is the magic of Finland, Estonia, the Sámi people, and Hungary: ancient, strange, and unlike anything the English or German magical establishments have any category for. The epic songs of the Kalevala are now working spells. Handle accordingly.

keys: Slavic Whisper Magic , the whispered charms ,
value:
Slavic Whisper Magic. [See also: The Parliament of Domains, Zagovory, Dvoeverie, Domovoi, Island Buyan, Baba Yaga.]
Slavic magic is phonetic: precise pronunciation is the mechanism. Mispronounce the charm and it fails entirely. The tradition serves not a single Fae court but a Parliament of Domains — a vast and chaotic assembly of territorial spirits so constitutionally monomaniacal that each considers its domain the entirety of the world, and everyone else's domain a polite fiction. The leshy rules each forest, the vodyanoy each water, the domovoi each individual house. Zagovory — oral incantations in sets of three, nine, or twelve — invoke the sacred stone Alatyr on the island Buyan. After Christianization, pagan Fae were replaced in prayer by Orthodox saints in a system called dvoeverie, double faith, which the Fae appear not to have been informed of, and which the saints appear not to have endorsed. The return of magic has made old formulas suddenly potent in ways that surprise their practitioners, which is precisely the most dangerous kind of surprise.
¹ The domovoi has not, to anyone's certain knowledge, been informed that it is technically St. Nicholas now. It continues to require porridge behind the stove on the appointed days. The porridge continues to vanish.
title: Slavic Whisper Magic
description: Slavic magic covers Russia, Ukraine, Poland, and the Czech lands — a tradition of whispered charms spoken with exact precision that negotiate with hundreds of local spirits, each obsessively territorial and constitutionally incapable of caring about anything outside their own domain. In February 1817, the old charms have teeth again. The domovoi in every hearth, the leshy in every wood, and the vodyanoy in every river are awake, demanding the old courtesies.

keys: Baltic Sacred Fire Magic , the pagan fire-worship ,
value:
Baltic Sacred Fire Magic. [See also: The Romuva Court, Perkūnas, Sacred Groves, The Aitvaras, Laima, Dainos.]
Lithuania was the last pagan nation in Europe — not Christianized until 1387, which is to say within living grandmothers' memory. The Romuva Court, named for the sacred centre where the eternal flame was once tended, barely had time to sleep before magic's return jolted it awake. Its thunder-being, Perkūnas, is not furious in any sense a mortal would recognise as fury. He is proprietary. The sacred groves were his. The prayers were his. He wants them returned not because they served a higher purpose, but because they were his, and someone took them, and he finds this aesthetically intolerable. His lightning has been striking churches with improving accuracy since 1816. Baltic magic centres on sacred fire, sacred groves called alkai, and the veneration of the žaltys — a grass snake kept in the household and fed milk — killing one invites catastrophe. The dainos, Lithuanian folk songs, carry magical content within everyday lyrics. Living grandmothers remember the songs. The grove-sites are intact.
¹ The aitvaras — a shapeshifting spirit that may be deliberately created and brings its household stolen wealth — is actively manifesting across the Baltic lands. The ethics of this arrangement are, the present author observes, the aitvaras's least pressing concern.
title: Baltic Sacred Fire Magic
description: Baltic magic covers Lithuania, Latvia, and the old Prussian lands — the most recently awakened tradition in Europe, because it was the most recently suppressed. Lithuanian grandmothers still remember the old songs. The sacred groves still stand. The thunder-being is furious, and is expressing this with increasing accuracy against church steeples. This tradition never really went to sleep. It was barely interrupted.

keys: Balkan Dragon-Scholar Magic , the Devil's school arts ,
value:
Balkan Dragon-Scholar Magic. [See also: The Scholomance Court, The Schoolmaster, Solomonari, Strigoi, Iele, Ursitoare.]
The Balkans possess Europe's most fluid tradition — witch, vampire, werewolf, and fairy blur and merge here in ways that distress taxonomists and appear to delight the entities involved. The Scholomance Court takes its name from the legendary underground school in the Transylvanian mountains where, according to tradition, the Devil taught ten students the secrets of weather and dragon-riding, keeping the tenth as his permanent Weathermaker. The Devil was a Fae lord of considerable power with an abiding interest in mortal learning capacity — not from compassion, but from the fascination a naturalist has for an unusual beetle. His graduates, the solomonari, ride balaur dragons with golden bridles. In February 1817 the Scholomance has reopened and is accepting applications. The strigoi, the iele (irresistibly beautiful nocturnal fairies), and the zmeu (dragon-beings of human intelligence) operate in proximity — a magical environment of considerable liveliness. The ursitoare, three Fates who visit every newborn, are appearing at cradles again across the region.
¹ The terms of enrolment include a one-in-ten retention rate for what the Schoolmaster describes as extended faculty fellowship. Prospective students are advised to be aware of this before accepting.
title: Balkan Dragon-Scholar Magic
description: Balkan magic covers Romania, Bulgaria, Serbia, and surrounding lands — the most chaotically rich tradition in Europe, where witch, vampire, werewolf, and fairy all blur together. Its Fae court is an underground school: students learn weather magic and dragon-riding from a being who considers mortal students interesting the way a naturalist finds beetles interesting. One student in ten is kept permanently. Understand this before enrolling.

keys: Mediterranean Eye-Ward Magic , the southern eye-warding ,
value:
Mediterranean Eye-Ward Magic. [See also: The Olympian Court, The Lord of the Shining Mountain, The Evil Eye, Benandanti, Malocchio.]
Mediterranean magic — the tradition of Greece, Southern Italy, Sicily, and the wider coast — is organized around a single governing terror: the Evil Eye. What mortals remembered as the Olympian gods were Fae of the sun-drenched south whose power operated through beauty, envy, and the dangerous boundary between admiration and destruction. The neraides, descended from ancient nymphs, were their courtiers; the kallikantzaroi, subterranean goblins who emerge during the Twelve Days of Christmas, were their prisoners. Italian magic centres on the strega tradition and the benedicaria healing practice. The benandanti of Friuli — born with a caul — leave their bodies on Ember Days to fight witches with fennel stalks in organized astral combat for agricultural prosperity. The knowledge to remove malocchio must be transmitted on Christmas Eve from grandmother to grandchild and has become the most urgently sought inheritance in Southern Europe. The oil-and-water diagnostic is, since the return of magic, suddenly and disturbingly accurate.
¹ The Olympian Court's lord does not weep for the loss of worship from any sentiment a mortal would recognise as grief. He weeps because the situation is, to him, a considerable aesthetic disappointment.
title: Mediterranean Eye-Ward Magic
description: Mediterranean magic spans Greece, Southern Italy, and Sicily — a tradition built around defending against the Evil Eye, the supernatural harm caused by envy and admiration turned malicious. The ancient gods of Olympus were never gods: they were Fae of extraordinary power and vanity whose magic operated through beauty, fate, and the precarious line between being admired and being destroyed. In February 1817 they remember being worshipped, and they find the current situation disagreeable.

keys: Iberian Blood-and-Stone Magic , the Basque mountain arts ,
value:
Iberian Blood-and-Stone Magic. [See also: The Mari Court, Mari, Sorginak, Basajaun, Santa Compaña, Religion versus Magic.]
Iberian magic preserves the oldest stratum in Western Europe. The Mari Court of the Basque Country predates Indo-European languages entirely — genuinely alien to every tradition surrounding it, and aware of this distinction in the way that very old things are aware of their age, which is to say calmly and without feeling the need to explain. The being called Mari is a Fae sovereign of weather, justice, and the deep earth: she dwells in mountain caves, manifests as various women, red animals, or the black he-goat, and demands honesty above all other courtesies. She cannot be deceived. This is not a skill. It is her nature.¹ Galician meigas practice herb-magic and curse-work under the shadow of the Santa Compaña, a spectral procession of the dead. The queimada — a communal exorcism in which grape spirit is set ablaze while the Conxuro is chanted — is a drinkable banishing ritual. The Inquisition drove much of Iberian magic underground; the return has brought it back through the stone. The basajaun has been sighted in the Pyrenees for the first time in centuries.
¹ Practitioners who approach Mari with concealed motives find the concealed portion extracted as payment. The only protection is complete honesty. Complete honesty before a being who controls the weather is its own form of courage.
title: Iberian Blood-and-Stone Magic
description: Iberian magic covers Spain, Portugal, and especially the Basque Country — home to the oldest Fae court in Western Europe, one that predates the Indo-European languages. The Mari Court demands one thing above all else: complete honesty. Mari cannot be deceived, and anything concealed becomes her property. This is the most ancient and least forgiving tradition in this catalogue. The Inquisition is still active in Spain. Practitioners navigate both hazards simultaneously.

keys: French Grimoire Magic , the book-bound spirits ,
value:
French Grimoire Magic. [See also: The Mélusine Court, The Chief Notary, The Ankou, Le Grand Albert, Le Dragon Rouge.]
France is the grimoire capital of Europe — the tradition of formalized sorcery compiled into books of power. The Mélusine Court, named for the great serpent-fairy who married a nobleman and flew away when he broke his oath never to observe her bathing on Saturdays, governs through written law, formal contract, and binding text. It is the most legalistic Fae court in Europe, which is to say that it follows the letter of every agreement with a precision that would distress any lawyer who had not first thought carefully about what the letter of an agreement means to a being constitutionally incapable of perceiving ambiguity. French magic operates through grimoires — Le Grand Albert, Le Petit Albert, Le Dragon Rouge — and the summoning of spirits according to precise textual formulas. The spirits that answer are not the demons the grimoires describe. They are Fae of the Mélusine Court. They follow the grimoire's instructions exactly.¹ The Ankou — Death's servant, replaced annually by the last person to die in each parish — has resumed his rounds in Brittany.
¹ The present author cannot stress sufficiently that 'exactly' is the operative word. He has seen what results from one misplaced definite article.
title: French Grimoire Magic
description: French magic is the most formally documented tradition in Europe — a system of written grimoires and binding contracts, governed by a Fae court that is essentially the most bureaucratic supernatural entity ever recorded. The spirits you summon follow instructions to the letter. Literally to the letter. Understanding what 'the letter' means to a fairy is the entire practice. France in 1817 is politically fractured between royalists, Bonapartists, and republicans; magic adds a fourth axis of dispute.

keys: Romani Road Magic , the Romani blood-gift ,
value:
Romani Road Magic. [See also: The Romani Bloodline Court, The Fractured Sight, Small Alliances, Dead-Dealing, Drabardi.]
Romani magic descends from a Changeling — a Fae child left with a Romani family who, when recalled to its court, refused to go. It had found mortal things worth staying for. It chose the road, bred with mortals, and died as mortals die. What it left was a bloodline: Romani practitioners carry their power not because they were taught it but because they inherited it, and it manifests differently in each descendant. Three streams run through the blood. The fractured sight — the Changeling's non-linear time-perception diluted through generations — provides temporal double-exposure; the cards, the palm, the pattern in smoke supply scaffolding for a perception that already exists. The small alliances — pacts made not with rivers but with puddles, not with ancient stones but with pebbles, with sparrows and field mice and small devils overlooked when the infernal hierarchies were established — form an intelligence network operating below any court's notice. Dead-dealing grants access to the quiet dead who have simply lived through what has not yet happened to the living. No Fae court claims the Romani. No Fae court has found a legal category for them. Most find this philosophically irritating.
¹ The gentleman with the thistle-down hair once met a Romani drabardi on the road to London, found her briefly interesting, then found her unsettling in a way he did not pursue. She had looked at him as one looks at someone whose end one has already seen.
title: Romani Road Magic
description: Romani magic is unlike any other tradition: not a court, not a school, not a landscape alliance, but a bloodline. The Romani carry diluted Fae power through generations of mortal lives — each practitioner manifesting it differently. They owe nothing to any Fae court, which is both their greatest freedom and their practical ceiling. They read time sideways, deal with the quiet dead, and maintain thousands of small pacts with small spirits across every road they have ever traveled.

keys: Desert Magic — The Djinn Traditions , Solomon's inheritance ,
value:
Desert Magic — The Djinn Traditions. [See also: The Djinn Courts, Sahir, Kahin, Seal-Magic, Astrological Binding, The Narcissist's Grievance.]
The Djinn are the Fae of the desert, river-valley, and sea-coast — ancient beings present since before the oldest mortal civilizations of their region, and the rightful governors of every magical alliance in their territory. They are not the European Fae courts' subordinates, nor their enemies. They are their equivalents in a different geography: equally old, equally proud, and carrying a grievance that makes every other inter-court dispute in this catalogue look like a polite disagreement over precedence. Their mortal practitioners — the sahir (sorcerers), the kahin (diviners), and the rāqī (incantation specialists) — maintained alliances through direct negotiation across millennia. Then a younger Fae arrived, convinced that all other powers were fiction, and spread this conviction through the Djinn's territory. Old alliances frayed. Pacts went unrenewed. The Djinn did not sleep. They waited, and their waiting was the silence of a fire banked rather than extinguished.¹ Three streams characterize practice: direct negotiation, now more dangerous than at any point in living memory; astrological binding; and seal-magic, being urgently reconstructed from fragments. Practitioners who cannot bind what they summon are not practitioners. They are offerings.
¹ Every family whose ancestor made a compact with a Djinn and then converted and forgot is currently receiving a visit. The Djinn's memory of the compact predates the conversion. Compound interest applies.
title: Desert Magic — The Djinn Traditions
description: Djinn magic covers the Arabian Peninsula, Persia, the Levant, and North Africa. The Djinn — the Fae of the desert world — never went dormant. They waited, with a fire's patience, through centuries in which their practitioners were told the Djinn did not exist. They have centuries of accumulated grievance and a precise memory for every pact ever made. Knowing how to bind what you summon is not optional. Practitioners in this tradition approach beings of justified anger and extraordinary power.

keys: Abrahamic Fringe Magic , the sacred name tradition ,
value:
Abrahamic Fringe Magic. [See also: The Narcissist, Kabbalistic Practice, Golem-Making, Islamic Esoteric Practice, Christian Ceremonial Magic, The Djinn Courts.]
A single Fae of extraordinary conviction is the source of this power. No name will be given here, because the question of its name is the most contested theological dispute in human history, and the present author has no interest in adjudicating it in a footnote. This Fae is not the oldest, nor the most powerful. It is, by some measures, the most successful, in the narrow sense that it has convinced more mortals of more total claims than any other being in recorded history. It arrived without a landscape and without a court, carrying only an absolute, genuine, unfeigned conviction that it alone was the source of all creation. Fae cannot lie. This one believes every word. The three traditions it generated are three separate mortal attempts to systematize the instructions of a single being whose demands were total and whose instructions contradicted themselves across centuries. Jewish Kabbalistic practice produced the most technically rigorous map of this Fae's internal architecture ever attempted — the Sefirot are a Fae's psychology charted with scholarly precision. Christian ceremonial magic is the most syncretic. Islamic esoteric practice operates, in 1817, directly in the territory the Djinn are currently reclaiming.¹
¹ The other Fae courts call it the Narcissist. They do so with visible satisfaction. The being in question is not aware of this name, which would require acknowledging that other beings exist to have coined it.
title: Abrahamic Fringe Magic
description: Abrahamic magic draws power from a single, extraordinary Fae who genuinely believes it is the source of all creation. This is not a lie — fairies cannot lie. It simply believes it, absolutely and without reservation. The power it provides is real and deep. The three faiths built on its instructions — Jewish, Christian, and Islamic — each access genuine power in their own way, constrained by the same structural limits: no landscape magic, no dealings with other courts. The Djinn find the entire situation unforgivable.

keys: Peoples of Magical Europe , the magical peoples of Europe ,
value:
Peoples of Magical Europe. [See also: The Reawakening of Magic, The Dual Catalyst.]
Magic's return does not respect borders drawn by diplomats at the Congress of Vienna. It follows older lines — the lines of language, belief, and blood. A Polish peasant and a Russian serf may serve rival emperors, but they whisper the same zagovory over their sick children and keep the same courtesies for the same hearth-spirits. The Fae courts, for their part, formed their alliances with mortal peoples long before any current nation-state existed, and they show no awareness that these nations exist now.
¹ The present author observes that the relationship between ethnicity and magical tradition is one of inheritance rather than exclusivity. An Englishman who studies grimoire magic is unusual. He is not impossible. The Fae courts, however, have opinions.
title: Peoples of Magical Europe
description: This entry explains how magical traditions relate to heritage in 1817 Europe. Magic follows cultural bloodlines and old alliances rather than national borders. Choosing your ethnicity shapes which magical traditions are naturally open to you and which Fae courts consider you a familiar face.

keys: English , of English birth and breeding ,
value:
English. [See also: English Practical Magic, The Norrellite School, The Strangite School, The Raven King's Court.]
The English are the inheritors of John Uskglass's tradition — three centuries of fairy-allied sorcery followed by four centuries of forgetting, followed by two years of embarrassing recollection. English society in February 1817 is rigidly stratified, and magic's return has inverted several of its preferred hierarchies: a Yorkshire farmhand with the Sight commands more useful knowledge than a Duke's second son with a classical education, which both parties find uncomfortable. Access to England's richest surviving library of magical texts positions the English practitioner advantageously — provided he can navigate the factional war between Norrell's school and Strange's school, which is currently more dangerous than the French.
¹ The Raven King's ancient ley-lines are reawakening across the northern counties. What they are awakening into has not yet been fully determined.
title: English
description: England is the centre of magic's return in 1817 — the country with the richest surviving magical history, the most famous living magicians, and the most bitter internal dispute about how magic should be practiced. As an Englishman or Englishwoman, you inherit the tradition of the legendary Raven King and are caught between two schools at open war with each other.

keys: Scottish , of Scottish birth and breeding ,
value:
Scottish. [See also: Celtic Otherworld Magic, The Sídhe Court, Second Sight, The Highland Clearances.]
Fiercely independent, recently and reluctantly joined to England by the Acts of Union — which the Sídhe Court ignored, having been party to no such agreement. The Scottish Highlands preserve older Celtic traditions that lowland English magicians have consistently dismissed as superstition, which they are finding increasingly embarrassing to explain. Highland Clearances are actively displacing Gaelic-speaking communities and scattering the knowledge held in those communities — a form of magical devastation that no one in Westminster has thought to classify as such. Scottish practitioners carry a double burden: their traditions are real and potent, but English magical society considers them primitive.
¹ The Seelie and Unseelie distinction — familiar from Scottish ballads — appears in no English theoretical text of substance. This is because English theoretical magicians have not been to Scotland. Scottish practitioners advise keeping it that way.
title: Scottish
description: Scotland in 1817 sits between two worlds — politically joined to England, but magically rooted in older Celtic traditions that London's magicians dismiss and are wrong to. The Highlands preserve living fairy-doctor lineages, second sight, and a direct relationship with the Sídhe that no English book-magician has matched. The Highland Clearances are simultaneously displacing communities and scattering rare magical knowledge.

keys: Irish , of Hibernian birth ,
value:
Irish. [See also: Celtic Otherworld Magic, The Sídhe Court, Bean Feasa, Fairy Doctors, The Geis.]
A colonized nation under British rule, approaching the catastrophes of the coming decades with an expression of stoic endurance that the English have mistaken for acquiescence. Irish magical tradition is among the deepest in Europe: the bean feasa, fairy doctors, and filid maintain practices stretching back millennia, and the relationship with the Aos Sí is more intimate and more dangerous than anything in Britain. Fairy forts dot every parish; even Protestant landlords leave them undisturbed. Magic's return has given the Irish a power their colonial masters cannot easily regulate, and the political implications are not lost on anyone.
¹ The Raven King's alliances were with English landscape spirits. He made no equivalent treaty with Ireland's genius loci. Whether this is an advantage or a complication for Irish practitioners is a question several scholars are currently pursuing and none have yet answered.
title: Irish
description: Ireland in 1817 is under British rule, its people suffering under colonial law — but its magical tradition is one of the oldest and deepest in Europe. The relationship with the fairy people here is intimate, dangerous, and predates Christianity by millennia. Magic's return has awakened power that no colonial administration knows how to suppress, and every fairy fort in every Irish parish is humming.

keys: Welsh , of Cambrian birth ,
value:
Welsh. [See also: Celtic Otherworld Magic, The Sídhe Court, Gwyn ap Nudd, Annwn.]
A people with their own language, their own legal tradition, and their own fairy court — the Tylwyth Teg, ruled by Gwyn ap Nudd from Annwn. The Welsh language carries magical resonance that English translations do not preserve; certain phrases in Cymraeg activate effects that their English equivalents do not, a fact which the dyn hysbys (knowing men) have long understood and scholars have not. The industrial revolution is beginning its transformation of South Wales, creating an eerie collision between iron foundries and the Fair Family who cannot abide iron. Magic's return is manifesting differently in the Rhondda Valley and the mountain parishes, and the difference is not subtle.
title: Welsh
description: Wales preserves its own distinct fairy court — the Tylwyth Teg — and a magical tradition encoded in a language whose power English doesn't translate. As the industrial revolution begins destroying Welsh landscapes, the iron foundries and the ancient fairy paths are coming into violent proximity. Welsh practitioners navigate colonial pressure, industrial encroachment, and a fairy court that is very much awake.

keys: French , of French extraction ,
value:
French. [See also: French Grimoire Magic, The Mélusine Court, The Chief Notary, Le Grand Albert.]
Citizens of a nation still reeling from revolution, terror, empire, and collapse — a sequence of events that would unsettle any population, even one not simultaneously discovering that the spirits in their grimoires are real and have opinions about the current government. France is the grimoire capital of Europe, and the Mélusine Court's Fae operate through written contract, making French magic the most legalistic tradition on the continent. Breton French carry Celtic traditions closer to the Irish than to Parisian sophistication. The fractured politics of post-Napoleonic France mean a French practitioner must determine, at every introduction, which faction their interlocutor belongs to before deciding how much to reveal.
¹ The Mélusine Court has no view on French politics. It has filed a request for clarification on which government currently holds the contracts. No reply has been received.
title: French
description: France in 1817 is politically fractured between royalists, Bonapartists, and republicans — and now has a fourth axis of dispute. The grimoire tradition is the most formally documented magical practice in Europe, governed by the most contractual Fae court on the continent. French practitioners are extraordinarily well-supplied with magical texts whose contents are now suddenly operational.

keys: Germanic , of Teutonic extraction ,
value:
Germanic. [See also: Norse-Germanic Rune Craft, The Æsir Court, The One-Eyed Lord, Hexenmeister, Galdrastafir.]
The German-speaking peoples are fragmented across dozens of principalities and the rival ambitions of Prussia and Austria — a political arrangement the Æsir Court finds irrelevant, having formed its alliances with the Norse peoples long before the current borders were drawn. Germanic Romanticism is in full flower: the Brothers Grimm are actively collecting the folk tales now proving to be field reports, which they have not yet realised. The Hexenmeister tradition, the alchemical legacy of Paracelsus and Agrippa, and the deeply rooted Rosicrucianism of the educated classes give the Germanic practitioner an unusually broad theoretical foundation — and an unusually violent collision between Enlightenment rationalism and the evidence outside their windows.
title: Germanic
description: The German-speaking lands in 1817 are home to the Romantic movement, the Grimm brothers, and the reawakening rune-craft tradition of the Norse Fae court. As a Germanic practitioner you inherit a rich alchemical and scholarly tradition alongside living folk magic, and are personally acquainted with the gap between what learned men say is possible and what is demonstrably occurring.

keys: Scandinavian , of Norse extraction ,
value:
Scandinavian. [See also: Norse-Germanic Rune Craft, The Æsir Court, The Wild Hunt, Völvas and Seiðr, Galdrastafir.]
The Norse peoples preserve the Æsir Court traditions most directly, and Iceland — with its medieval sagas recording magical practice in scholarly detail and its population's persistent belief in huldufólk — is the hotbed of returned magic. Galdrastafir that were curiosities in manuscripts are now functional. The Wild Hunt rides winter storms again. Norwegian and Swedish klok folk find their svartebøker suddenly dangerous. Denmark, more cosmopolitan, struggles to reconcile Enlightenment values with trolls in the Jutland heath. The Eddas and the sagas constitute the most complete surviving record of a Fae court's expectations in existence — an advantage that requires one to have read them, and to have read them correctly.
¹ 'Correctly' is the difficulty. Three separate Icelandic scholars have produced three separate interpretations of the Hávamál's magical content. Two of them have since had encounters suggesting their interpretations were inaccurate.
title: Scandinavian
description: Scandinavia and Iceland in 1817 are the heartland of Norse magical tradition — the culture that kept the most detailed written records of the Æsir Court's expectations. The Wild Hunt rides again. Magical staves carved in grimoires are now working spells. The Eddas are operational manuals. Knowing how to read them correctly, however, is a separate question from knowing that they should be read.

keys: Finnish , of Finnish extraction ,
value:
Finnish. [See also: Finno-Ugric Origin-Song Magic, The Väki Court, Tietäjä, The Kalevala, Löyly.]
A people under Russian imperial rule but culturally distinct, speaking a language unrelated to their neighbours and practicing magic unrelated to any surrounding tradition. Finnish origin-song sorcery is the most alien magical tradition in Northern Europe; the tietäjä's knowledge-as-power has no equivalents in any English or Germanic theoretical text. The Kalevala's runo-songs are operationally functional as of 1817. Finnish practitioners are simultaneously invaluable — their magic produces results other traditions cannot — and isolated, because no one else understands how it works. This combination of utility and incomprehensibility has historically protected them. It continues to do so.
title: Finnish
description: Finnish magic is the most intellectually alien tradition in Northern Europe: to know the true origin of a thing is to command it. As a Finn in 1817, you carry a tradition that produces results no one else can replicate and that no one else properly understands — which is both a significant advantage and a significant complication when working with practitioners from other backgrounds.

keys: Sámi , of Sámi extraction ,
value:
Sámi. [See also: Finno-Ugric Origin-Song Magic, The Väki Court, Noaidi, Drum-Trance.]
The indigenous people of northern Scandinavia — reindeer herders and fisher-folk whose noaidi preserve the oldest continuous magical practice in Europe. Sámi magic centres on the goavddis (sacred drum) and the joik, a vocal tradition that does not describe a thing but embodies it — a distinction that takes some time to appreciate and rewards the effort. Christian missionaries burned their drums and suppressed their practice for generations. Magic's return has vindicated the noaidi comprehensively, which has not made the missionaries easier to live with. It has drawn considerable attention from southern magicians hungry for what they describe as 'authentic' power, which the Sámi find wearying.
¹ The present author notes that no southern magician who has attempted to acquire a noaidi's drum by purchase has yet obtained one. The noaidi are aware of what their drums are worth.
title: Sámi
description: The Sámi people of northern Scandinavia preserve one of the oldest unbroken magical traditions in Europe — drum-trance shamanism and a vocal art that doesn't describe things but becomes them. Centuries of missionary suppression have ended. The magic is back. Outsiders are arriving with money and academic curiosity, which is a new and not entirely welcome development.

keys: Russian , of Russian extraction ,
value:
Russian. [See also: Slavic Whisper Magic, Parliament of Domains, Dvoeverie, Zagovory, Domovoi.]
Subjects of an empire of extraordinary size, where magic's return manifests differently in St. Petersburg drawing rooms and Siberian forests — which is to say, everywhere and not always in the same register. Russian magical tradition blends Slavic whisper-magic with Orthodox Christian mysticism in the dvoeverie (double faith) — practitioners simultaneously invoke Christ and address the leshy with no sense of contradiction, which the Orthodox Church finds officially troubling and practically unavoidable. The zagovory whispered over sick children are the same from Novgorod to Vladivostok. The znakhar (folk healer), vedma (witch), and the mysterious startsy (holy elders) represent different faces of a practice the empire cannot suppress because it is woven into every household in it.
title: Russian
description: Russia in 1817 is a vast empire where folk magic is inseparable from daily life. The tradition runs on spoken charms, spirit-courtesies, and a comfortable coexistence of Orthodox Christianity and older powers that the Church officially opposes and practically cannot dislodge. Every hearth has a domovoi. Every forest has a leshy. They are awake, and they remember which families forgot their manners.

keys: Polish , of Polish extraction ,
value:
Polish. [See also: Slavic Whisper Magic, Parliament of Domains, Zagovory, Dvoeverie.]
A nation that officially does not exist — partitioned between Russia, Prussia, and Austria since 1795. Polish identity survives through language, Catholic faith, and a magical tradition that its occupiers cannot confiscate. Polish folk magic blends Slavic whisper-craft with Catholic devotional practice and a fierce streak of romantic nationalism; the strzygon and the południca (Lady Midday) walk the Polish fields again, which has done nothing to reduce the fields' political tension. Magic's return has given the Polish practitioner weapons their occupiers have no category for. The question of whether to use them is not abstract.
¹ Polish practitioners are advised that both Russian and Prussian authorities have begun documenting unusual occurrences in their territories. What they intend to do with this documentation has not yet been determined.
title: Polish
description: Poland in 1817 does not officially exist — its territory divided between three empires. Polish magical tradition survives through exactly the same combination of stubbornness and subtlety that has preserved Polish culture itself. Magic's return has given practitioners of an occupied nation tools their occupiers cannot categorise or confiscate. What to do with this is an urgent and open question.

keys: Hungarian , of Magyar extraction ,
value:
Hungarian. [See also: Finno-Ugric Origin-Song Magic, Táltos-Born, The Scholomance Court, Lidérc.]
A proud nation within the Habsburg Empire, speaking a Finno-Ugric language unrelated to their Slavic and Germanic neighbours. Hungarian magic descends from the steppe shamanism of the Magyar migration — the táltos tradition, with its born-marked practitioners and ecstatic bull-form spirit battles, is unlike anything in Central Europe and has attracted alarmed attention from the Vienna court since its reappearance. Hungarians also carry Balkan influences: strigoi-like beings, dragon lore, and the lidérc (a shape-shifting night demon hatched from an egg kept under the arm) are particular to the Magyar territories. The dual Finno-Ugric and Balkan inheritance gives the Hungarian practitioner an unusually wide range of available approaches.
title: Hungarian
description: Hungary sits between the Norse-Finnic and Balkan magical worlds, speaking a language unrelated to its neighbours and practicing a form of shamanism unlike anything in Central Europe. Táltos spirit-battlers were already making the Habsburg authorities nervous before 1817. The return of magic has made the situation considerably more interesting for everyone involved.

keys: Romanian , of Wallachian or Moldavian extraction ,
value:
Romanian. [See also: Balkan Dragon-Scholar Magic, The Scholomance Court, The Schoolmaster, Solomonari, Strigoi.]
Speakers of a Romance language surrounded by Slavic neighbours, occupying the Carpathian Mountains — the most magically saturated landscape in Eastern Europe by any measure the present author has found applicable. Romanian magical tradition is the richest in the Balkans: solomonari ride balaur dragons with golden bridles, strigoi walk (both living and dead, which is a distinction requiring clarification), the călușari warrior-dancers heal through choreography, and the ursitoare visit every cradle. The Scholomance itself lies in the Transylvanian mountains and has reopened. Romanian peasant culture maintains an elaborate calendar of magical observances whose importance, in February 1817, has abruptly shifted from traditional to urgent.
title: Romanian
description: Romania sits at the heart of the Carpathians — the most supernaturally active landscape in Eastern Europe. Dragon-riding weather wizards, the living dead, warrior-dancers who heal through ritual combat, and three Fates visiting every cradle are all features of the tradition. The Scholomance — the underground school where the Fae lord teaches ten students and keeps one — has reopened its doors.

keys: South Slavic , of Illyrian extraction ,
value:
South Slavic. [See also: Balkan Dragon-Scholar Magic, Slavic Whisper Magic, Dhampir, Vila, Krsnik.]
The South Slavic peoples — Serbian, Bulgarian, Croatian — live under Ottoman or Habsburg rule, their traditions preserved by village life and oral culture. Serbian epic poetry encodes genuine magical knowledge: the vila (mountain fairy) who serves as a hero's blood-sister, the zmaj (dragon-hero), and the vampire tradition that is the most developed in Europe. Bulgarian magic includes nestinarstvo (fire-walking in trance). Croatian krsnik practitioners fight the kudlak in nightly animal-form spirit battles. The Ottoman frontier has created a unique magical borderland where Christian, Muslim, and pre-Christian practices operate in reluctant proximity. Each of these three traditions has its own internal logic and only superficially resembles the others.
title: South Slavic
description: The South Slavic peoples occupy a magical borderland where Ottoman, Habsburg, and older pre-Christian traditions collide. Serbia, Bulgaria, and Croatia each carry distinct magical practices: epic poetry as operational knowledge, fire-walking, the most sophisticated vampire lore in Europe, and practitioners whose spirits leave their bodies at night to fight supernatural threats in animal form.

keys: Baltic , of Lithuanian or Lettish extraction ,
value:
Baltic. [See also: Baltic Sacred Fire Magic, The Romuva Court, Perkūnas, Sacred Groves, Aitvaras.]
The Baltic peoples — Lithuanian and Latvian — preserve the most recently pagan culture in Europe. Living grandmothers in 1817 remember grandmothers who remembered the sacred groves before Christianization. The old religion was so thinly buried that magic's return has hit the Baltic lands with particular force: the Romuva Court had barely closed its eyes before being jolted awake. Baltic practitioners find themselves at the centre of scholarly attention from every direction — researchers rushing to study what they describe as the 'purest' surviving pre-Christian tradition. The practitioners in question are occupied with more pressing matters than being studied.
¹ Perkūnas's lightning-strikes on church steeples in the Lithuanian territories have increased in frequency and, reports suggest, in precision. The ecclesiastical authorities have no explanation to offer.
title: Baltic
description: Lithuania was the last pagan nation in Europe — not Christianized until 1387 — and the old tradition was so recently buried that living grandmothers remember it whole. The return of magic here has been dramatic and immediate. Sacred groves still stand. The thunder-being wants his fires and prayers back. Baltic practitioners occupy the most intact pre-Christian magical tradition in Europe, which is both a considerable advantage and considerable responsibility.

keys: Greek , of Hellenic birth ,
value:
Greek. [See also: Mediterranean Eye-Ward Magic, The Olympian Court, The Lord of the Shining Mountain, Evil Eye, Neraides.]
Citizens of a nation in the process of achieving independence from the Ottoman Empire — a political drama that the Olympian Court is observing with detached interest, having opinions about Greek affairs that predate the Ottoman Empire considerably. Greek magical tradition bridges classical antiquity and living folk practice: the neraides are the nymphs of old, the kallikantzaroi are goblins that predate Olympus, and the magissa practices an art with roots in Neoplatonism and the binding-spell tradition of the ancient world. The Olympian Court's Fae remember being worshipped, and they find the current situation aesthetically disagreeable.
¹ A Greek practitioner may claim the philosophical inheritance of Hermeticism, theurgy, and the Neoplatonic magical tradition. He is advised to be aware that the beings who inspired that tradition have not found scholarly commentary flattering.
title: Greek
description: Greece in 1817 is fighting for independence from the Ottomans — and its ancient magical tradition is fighting its own battles. The gods of Olympus were Fae of extraordinary vanity who remember being worshipped and find the current state of affairs deeply unsatisfying. Greek practitioners inherit both the philosophical magical tradition of the ancient world and the living folk practice of village healers and Evil Eye curers.

keys: Italian , of Italian extraction ,
value:
Italian. [See also: Mediterranean Eye-Ward Magic, The Olympian Court, Benandanti, Malocchio, Evil Eye.]
A fragmented peninsula of rival states, papal territories, and Austrian-controlled regions. Italian magic divides sharply between the learned tradition of the north — alchemy, ceremonial magic, the benandanti of Friuli who leave their bodies on Ember Days to fight witches for the harvest — and the folk tradition of the south, where the strega, the malocchio, and the benedicaria blessing-healers operate in dense proximity to Neapolitan superstition and Sicilian clan pragmatism. The city-states have each developed distinct magical microcultures. The Walnut Tree of Benevento is bearing leaves again. The knowledge to remove malocchio, which must pass from grandmother to grandchild on Christmas Eve, has become the most urgently sought inheritance in Southern Italy.
title: Italian
description: Italy in 1817 is a patchwork of states — not yet unified, each city with its own magical character. Northern Italian learning (alchemy, ceremonial magic, spirit-walking benandanti) sits alongside southern Italian folk practice (the Evil Eye, strega witchcraft, blessing-healers). The knowledge to cure malocchio is suddenly the most valuable thing a grandmother can pass on.

keys: Iberian , of Spanish or Portuguese extraction ,
value:
Iberian. [See also: Iberian Blood-and-Stone Magic, The Mari Court, Mari, Sorginak, Basajaun, Religion versus Magic.]
Spain is an empire in decline, its Inquisition recently suppressed but its shadow long. The Basque Country is something else entirely — its language and mythology predate Indo-European arrival, making the Mari Court's tradition the oldest in Western Europe and genuinely alien to every surrounding practice. A Basque sorgin serving Mari practices magic from a wellspring that shares no common root with Celtic, Germanic, or Mediterranean traditions. Portuguese practitioners carry the mouras encantadas of their megalithic landscape. Galician meigas and Asturian practitioners carry Celtic-influenced traditions distinct from Castilian Spain. Iberian practitioners have centuries of experience hiding their work from institutions with judicial powers. This is a transferable skill.
¹ The basajaun has been sighted in the Pyrenees. The Inquisition's files on the Zugarramurdi trials remain open.
title: Iberian
description: Iberia's magical landscape runs from the oldest Fae court in Western Europe — the Mari Court of the Basque Country, whose tradition predates Indo-European languages — to Galician folk magic to Castilian Inquisition-surviving cunning craft. Practitioners here are very experienced at working under hostile institutional observation. The Inquisition may be suppressed. Its records and its instincts persist.

keys: Romani , of Romani birth ,
value:
Romani. [See also: Romani Road Magic, The Romani Bloodline Court, The Fractured Sight, Small Alliances, Drabardi.]
A people who have been everywhere and belong nowhere in the political sense — which is not the same as belonging to nothing. The Romani carry the only magical bloodline in Europe, descended from a Changeling who chose to stay among mortals rather than return to its court. Every Romani person carries, to some degree, diluted Fae heritage — but the degree varies enormously, and most do not know what they carry until they reach for it. In February 1817 the Romani exist under persistent persecution in every nation this catalogue covers. The return of magic has not changed their legal status in any European jurisdiction. It has made their practical power undeniable, which creates its own dangers for a persecuted people whose power the powerful have now noticed.
¹ No Fae court claims the Romani. No Fae court has found a legal category for them. Most find this philosophically irritating and do nothing about it, which is, the present author thinks, the wisest available response.
title: Romani
description: The Romani carry something no other ethnic group in Europe possesses: actual Fae blood, diluted through generations. This manifests differently in every individual — some carry strong fractured sight, some carry the gift for small-spirit alliances, some carry both, some show nothing until it is needed. No Fae court claims them, which means no Fae court can demand anything of them — the only such free position in all of Europe.

keys: Arabic Persian and North African , of Arabian or Persian extraction ,
value:
Arabic, Persian, and North African. [See also: Desert Magic, The Djinn Courts, Sahir, Seal-Magic, Astrological Binding.]
A grouping of peoples united by proximity to the Djinn and the complexity of their relationship with them. Moroccans, Egyptians, Levantines, Arabians, and Persians each carry a regional variant of the Desert tradition, shaped by which Djinn courts dominated their specific landscape and how thoroughly centuries of theological pressure disrupted the old alliances. The disruption was never complete. The Djinn were too old and too present to disappear when theology said they should not exist. Practitioners maintained contact through cracks in the official cosmology, which satisfied the theology and the Djinn both, barely, and only temporarily. In February 1817 that accommodation is ending. The Djinn are collecting. Practitioners whose ancestors made pacts and then converted and forgot the pacts are receiving visits.
¹ The Djinn's memory of any compact predates the conversion that is offered as grounds for its cancellation. They find this argument unpersuasive.
title: Arabic, Persian, and North African
description: The peoples of the Arabian Peninsula, Persia, and North Africa have lived alongside the Djinn — the Fae of the desert and river-valley — since before recorded history. Centuries of theological pressure frayed these old alliances but never broke them. In 1817 the Djinn are fully awake, collecting on debts their mortal partners' ancestors forgot. This is a tradition of justified fury and extraordinary depth, for those who can survive negotiating with it.

keys: Jewish , of the Hebrew nation ,
value:
Jewish. [See also: Abrahamic Fringe Magic, Kabbalistic Practice, Golem-Making, Pan-European Network.]
A people of the diaspora — present in every location this catalogue covers. Jewish communities have survived by making everything portable: tradition carried in books, in calendar, in the accumulated knowledge of communities that have been everywhere and expelled from most of it. Their magical tradition is the most technically rigorous in Europe. Centuries of Kabbalistic scholarship have produced an extraordinarily precise map of a particular Fae's internal architecture — documented in more detail than any other tradition has managed for any other court. That the scholars who produced this map believed they were charting divine cosmology rather than a Fae's psychology does not make the map less accurate. Those who have realised what they were actually charting face the most difficult theological crisis of this or any recent century. Most have not yet realised it. Their magic works regardless.
¹ The present author declines to characterise the nature of the realisation beyond noting that it is, in his observation, irrevocable.
title: Jewish
description: Jewish magical tradition is the most technically precise in Europe — centuries of Kabbalistic scholarship have produced an extraordinarily detailed map of a Fae's internal architecture that other traditions have never come close to replicating. Jewish practitioners in 1817 are present in every city in Europe, carry an unparalleled amulet-craft tradition, and operate under various degrees of legal restriction depending on which nation's laws apply. The magic works regardless of the legal status.

keys: Fae-Touched Ancestries , the fairy-marked ,
value:
Fae-Touched Ancestries. [See also: The Reawakening of Magic, The Raven King's Court, Genius Loci.]
These are not categories of persons in the conventional sense. They are humans marked by fairy contact — born different, transformed, or carrying blood that is not entirely mortal. In a world where magic has returned, these marks have begun manifesting in ways that were previously dormant or suppressed. The present author notes that the return of magic has not created these conditions but rather revealed them: the caul-born were always caul-born, the seventh son always seventh. What has changed is that the world has grown receptive to what they carry.
¹ A person may belong to one of the following ancestries in addition to an ethnic background. The two inheritances are not in conflict. They are, in several cases, in conversation with each other in ways the practitioner may not anticipate.
title: Fae-Touched Ancestries
description: These ancestries are not ethnic backgrounds but birth-marks — supernatural inheritances from fairy contact, unusual birth circumstances, or blood that is not entirely mortal. They layer on top of an ethnic background and shape what kind of practitioner you naturally become. In 1817, these marks are waking up along with everything else.

keys: Changeling (Returned) , one returned from the other country ,
value:
Changeling (Returned). [See also: The Raven King's Court, Lost-Hope, The Gentleman with the Thistle-Down Hair, Fairy Debt.]
You were taken by the Fae as a child and eventually returned — or escaped. You spent years in a Fae court, and though you look human, you are no longer entirely so. You perceive glamour, see through fairy illusions, and sense the proximity of Fae beings. You also struggle with mortal food (it tastes like ash), mortal time (it moves too fast or too slowly), and mortal emotions (they appear crude and very loud). You carry knowledge of the court that took you, but speaking of it directly causes physical pain — a parting gift from your captors, whose generosity in this regard is consistent with their generosity in all other regards.
¹ Abilities: See through glamour. Sense Fae proximity. Knowledge of one Fae court, gained at personal cost. Difficulty with mortal norms — food, time, and emotional register all present ongoing challenges that do not diminish with years of mortal residence.
title: Changeling (Returned)
description: You were taken by the Fae as a child and eventually came back — through escape, rescue, or some arrangement that cost more than it appeared to. You look human. You are no longer entirely human. Fairy food was better than mortal food. Mortal time runs at the wrong speed. You know things about the court that kept you, and speaking of them causes physical pain — which is how you know the information is real and valuable.

keys: Caul-Born , one born with the caul ,
value:
Caul-Born. [See also: Benandante, Solomonar, Noaidi, Hound of God, Krsnik.]
You were born wrapped in a caul — the amniotic membrane — occurring in fewer than one in eighty thousand births, though the present author suspects this statistic is becoming less reliable. Across all of Europe, the caul-born are marked for supernatural destiny; the specific destiny varies by tradition. In Italy you might become a benandante, compelled to fight witches in spirit-form on Ember Days. In the Balkans you might be solomonari material. In maritime cultures you are held immune to drowning, and sailors pay considerable sums for preserved cauls as protective talismans. The return of magic has activated these latent inheritances, and the caul itself — if preserved by a careful midwife — functions as a powerful protective amulet.
¹ Abilities: Enhanced aptitude for the magical tradition of your homeland. Protective amulet if caul is preserved. Immunity to drowning in all recorded European traditions. Potential for spirit-walking or astral combat (tradition-dependent).
title: Caul-Born
description: Born wrapped in the amniotic membrane — a rarity occurring in fewer than one in 80,000 births. Every European tradition has a name for what you are and a destiny prepared for you. In Italy you are potential benandanti material. In the Balkans, solomonari. At sea, the person everyone wants as crew. The return of magic has activated what was dormant. Your caul, if it was kept, is now a powerful protective charm.

keys: Seventh Son (or Seventh Daughter) , the gifted seventh child ,
value:
Seventh Son (or Seventh Daughter). [See also: Cunning Man or Wise Woman, Second Sight, Genius Loci.]
The seventh consecutive son of a seventh consecutive son — or, in some traditions, the seventh daughter of a seventh daughter. You were born with inherent healing touch, second sight, and an instinctive rapport with spirits that you neither sought nor expected and cannot entirely explain. Before magic's return, these gifts manifested as uncanny intuition, fortunate hands, and an unsettling ability to know when someone was about to die. Now they manifest as abilities that require no training because they require no learning — they simply are what you are. The local cunning folk recognised you at birth. Your family has been managing other people's expectations of you ever since.
¹ Abilities: Inherent healing touch. Second sight — perceive spirits, Fae, and magical workings without training. Instinctive rapport with supernatural beings. No tradition required. The power is innate, which means it cannot be studied away.
title: Seventh Son (or Seventh Daughter)
description: The seventh consecutive son of a seventh son — or the seventh daughter — is marked at birth with gifts that require no instruction. You see spirits. You heal by touch. Supernatural beings respond to you instinctively. You did not study for this, and you cannot unstudied it. In 1817 these gifts are becoming more visible and harder to explain to those around you.

keys: Fairy-Blooded , one of fairy descent ,
value:
Fairy-Blooded. [See also: The Mélusine Court, The Sídhe Court, Romani Road Magic, The Romani Bloodline Court, Iron Vulnerability.]
Somewhere in your ancestry — one generation back or twenty — a mortal and a Fae being produced offspring. The great noble houses of Europe whisper about this: the Lusignans claimed Mélusine's blood, the Plantagenets boasted of fairy ancestry, certain Scottish clans trace lineage to selkie marriages. Your fairy blood was dormant until magic returned. Now it manifests in small ways that are growing less small: you are uncannily beautiful or uncannily unsettling, iron makes your skin itch, you have difficulty producing direct lies, and on certain nights you hear music no one else can hear. The Fae court your ancestor belonged to considers you a distant relation — which may or may not be a welcome development.
¹ Abilities: Fae-adjacent appearance. Iron sensitivity. Difficulty with direct untruth. Periodic supernatural perception at liminal times. Claim of kinship with one Fae court — whose other members you have had no opportunity to vet.
title: Fairy-Blooded
description: Somewhere in your family tree — recent or ancient — a mortal and a Fae produced a child. The noble houses of Europe whisper about such lineages, and several claim them. With magic's return your inheritance has woken: iron stings, lies are difficult, and music comes to you at night from nowhere. A Fae court considers you distant family. Proceed carefully.

keys: Dhampir , the child of a revenant ,
value:
Dhampir. [See also: Balkan Dragon-Scholar Magic, Strigoi, The Scholomance Court, Dead-Dealing.]
Your father was a revenant — a strigoi mort who returned to his living wife. You appear normal to most eyes, but you see the invisible dead, perceive vampiric entities, and sense the presence of dark magic with an immediacy that is not, strictly speaking, comfortable. In the Balkans, your kind have worked as professional vampire-hunters for generations — you are a specialist the community requires and a person they regard with cautious affection. Magic's return has dramatically increased the walking dead population, making your skills urgently necessary. It has also intensified your own nature. You are alive. You carry something of the grave in you. Mirrors sometimes show your reflection a heartbeat late.
¹ Abilities: See the invisible dead and vampiric entities. Sense dark magic. Vampire-hunting aptitude — traditional methods are familiar rather than learned. Ambiguous relationship with mirrors, shadows, and reflections, which the present author does not describe further.
title: Dhampir
description: Your father was a revenant. You appear human and are largely human — but you see the dead that others cannot see, sense vampiric presences, and your reflection occasionally lags behind you in mirrors. The Balkans have needed your kind for generations. Magic's return has made the walking dead considerably more common, and your skills considerably more in demand.

keys: Táltos-Born , the Hungarian born-marked ,
value:
Táltos-Born. [See also: Finno-Ugric Origin-Song Magic, Hungarian, Táltos Spirit-Battler, Weather Magic.]
You were born with teeth already present, a sixth finger, extra bones, or a caul — the marks of the Hungarian táltos. At age seven, the spirits came for you and subjected you to a three-day ordeal of fever and terror. You survived. You can now enter the fighting-trance (révülés), project your spirit in the form of a bull or stallion, and do battle with other táltos or hostile spirits to control weather. The Habsburg authorities consider you a dangerous peasant superstition. You are nevertheless real, which they find inconvenient. Your power is tied to ecstatic states that are physically exhausting and socially alarming, and you answer to no Fae court — your power is innate, not granted, and the courts find this an irritating variable.
¹ Abilities: Spirit-projection in animal form (bull or stallion). Weather influence through spirit combat. Ecstatic trance. Physical cost of magical exertion, which cannot be avoided.
title: Táltos-Born
description: The Hungarian táltos are born marked and undergo an initiatory ordeal at age seven that leaves them able to project their spirit in animal form and fight supernatural battles that visibly affect the weather. No Fae court grants this power — it is inherited and inherent. The Habsburg Empire considers you a superstition. You are demonstrably not, which is the source of considerable friction.

keys: Hound of God , the benevolent werewolf ,
value:
Hound of God. [See also: Slavic Whisper Magic, Baltic Sacred Fire Magic, The Wild Hunt, Benandante.]
In 1691, the Livonian peasant Thiess of Kaltenbrun testified that he was a benevolent werewolf — a Hound of God who descended into Hell three times yearly with iron rods to battle the Devil's witches and defend the community's crops. Your tradition descends from this lineage. You are a werewolf, but not a monster: you are a spiritual warrior who transforms to protect the living from supernatural predation. Transformation is tied to the agricultural calendar — Ember Days, solstices — and initiated by a ritual toast of beer transmitted through your line. By day you are a person. By night, during the appointed seasons, you are something else that does the work a person cannot do. Magic's return has made your transformations more powerful, more frequent, and harder to contain within the calendar.
¹ Abilities: Calendar-linked beneficial lycanthropy. Spirit-warrior capabilities against supernatural threats. Enhanced senses and physical ability. Transformation control challenges — the calendar is becoming a suggestion.
title: Hound of God
description: You are a werewolf — but not a monster. Your tradition holds that the Hounds of God descend into Hell each season to fight the witches who threaten the harvest, returning to their human lives when the work is done. Magic's return has made the transformations stronger, more frequent, and less respectful of the agricultural calendar. You are still a protector. The boundaries of what you are protecting, and what you are, are in flux.

keys: Practical Magician , a practitioner of the art ,
value:
Practical Magician. [See also: English Practical Magic, The Norrellite School, The Strangite School, Books of Magic, The Madness Axis.]
A practical magician casts spells — from books, from improvisation, or from the third and most dangerous category, which several practitioners have recently taken to calling intuition and which more cautious observers might call recklessness dressed in clean clothes. Before Mr. Norrell demonstrated the magic of York Cathedral's statues, practical magic was considered an eccentric affectation. The position has since shifted. Practitioners trained before the return find their book-knowledge producing results that sometimes match the texts and sometimes do not — and the discrepancy is invariably more dangerous than the ignorance it replaced.
¹ The tradition divides between the Norrellite school, which works from approved texts under institutional authority, and the Strangite school, which works from instinct and publishes freely. Both positions have merit. Both have recently produced catastrophic results. The aspiring practical magician is advised to consider which catastrophe he prefers.
title: Practical Magician
description: You cast spells — from books, from experiment, or from dangerous intuition. Until recently this was an eccentric and faintly embarrassing profession. Now it is one of the most consequential in Europe, and the most contested. Two schools are in open factional war over how magic should be practiced. You will need to decide where you stand, or have the decision made for you.

keys: Theoretical Magician , a gentleman of the theoretical school ,
value:
Theoretical Magician. [See also: York — The Learned Society in Crisis, Books of Magic, The Norrellite School, Gilbert Norrell.]
A theoretical magician studies the history, philosophy, and theory of magic — and performs no spells. Before magic's return, this was the only respectable kind of magician in England. York — The Learned Society in Crisis was a club of theoretical magicians who debated footnotes, disputed the Raven King's biographical details, and considered the actual performance of magic to be in questionable taste. You are the custodian of a scholarly tradition stretching back centuries. What you do not possess is any demonstrated method for making any of it work. The return of practical magic has not made your knowledge useless — someone must read the old books and assess the old claims — but it has made your social position precarious in ways the Society never anticipated.
¹ The theoretical magician's great advantage is that he is never wrong in the way a practical magician is wrong — which is to say, suddenly and with loud consequences.
title: Theoretical Magician
description: You study magic. You do not cast it. This was the only respectable form of the art in England before 1817, and the return of practical magic has placed you in an awkward position — your scholarship is valuable, but the practitioners are the ones doing things. The old books you have spent your life reading are turning out to be operational manuals rather than historical records. This requires a significant revision of your previous assumptions.

keys: Norrellite , one of Mr Norrell's party ,
value:
Norrellite. [See also: Gilbert Norrell, York — The Learned Society in Crisis, The Friends of English Magic, Books of Magic.]
You follow the school of Gilbert Norrell — controlled, institutional, book-bound English magic practiced within strict boundaries and under recognised authority. You read The Friends of English Magic and regard it as reliable. You regard the Raven King as a historical figure to be studied cautiously, not invoked. You distrust fairy magic, wild magic, women's magic, and anything that cannot be found in a properly catalogued library. You are not wrong that uncontrolled magic is dangerous — the evidence is considerable and ongoing. You are wrong that Norrell's method of control, which amounts to owning all the books, constitutes safety rather than monopoly. In February 1817 your faction controls the institutions, the periodicals, and the ear of government. The magic actually manifesting does not match the magic in your approved texts. Your school's response has been to deny the discrepancy.
title: Norrellite
description: You follow the school of Mr. Norrell — the cautious, institutional approach to English magic, practiced from approved texts, within recognised authority, under respectable conditions. Norrell controls the largest magical library in England and the ear of the British government. His school is dominant and its caution is not entirely wrong. Its response to magic that doesn't fit the approved texts has been to dispute the magic rather than revise the texts.

keys: Strangite , one of Mr Strange's following ,
value:
Strangite. [See also: Jonathan Strange, The History and Practice of English Magic, The Raven King's Court, The Madness Axis, The King's Roads.]
You follow the school of Jonathan Strange — wild, experimental, intuitive English magic that draws power from the Raven King's tradition, from fairy roads, from the landscape itself, and from sources Norrell would prefer not to name. Strange's History and Practice of English Magic (1816) published what Norrell suppressed, and you have read it and found in it a liberation. You are drawn to the Raven King not as a cautionary tale but as a model — a magician who made alliances with the great natural forces of a landscape. The weakness of your school: Strange himself is in Venice, grieving, and has begun deliberately destroying his own sanity to access fairy sight. The question of whether his path leads to transcendence or catastrophe is the question your faction cannot answer without waiting to see which it is.
¹ Strange's tincture method — the details are not repeated here — is available to Strangite practitioners. The present author strongly advises against it.
title: Strangite
description: You follow the school of Jonathan Strange — experimental, intuitive magic that draws from the Raven King's wild tradition rather than Norrell's safe one. Strange published freely where Norrell suppressed, and his approach has liberated practitioners across England. In February 1817 Strange himself is in Venice, grieving his wife's apparent death, and has begun drinking preparations designed to induce madness. Your school is led by a man walking toward a precipice. This does not mean he is wrong about the direction.

keys: Cunning Man or Wise Woman , the cunning folk ,
value:
Cunning Man or Wise Woman. [See also: Celtic Otherworld Magic, Slavic Whisper Magic, Fairy Doctors, Genius Loci.]
The village magical practitioner — healer, curse-breaker, finder of lost things, identifier of thieves. You are the znakhar (Russia), klok gumma (Scandinavia), dyn hysbys (Wales), fattucchiera (Italy), or simply the person everyone visits after the physician fails, which is frequently. Your magic is practical, local, and handed down through family or apprenticeship. You were never respectable, but you were always necessary. Now that your charms work with undeniable force, your social position is shifting rapidly — and not always upward. The Church, the State, and the gentleman magicians all have opinions about you. The Church's opinion is the most dangerous. The gentleman magicians' opinion is the most irritating, because they have recently become interested in what you know.
¹ The cunning folk's intelligence network — accumulated through generations of seeing what people bring to them, which is everything — is currently the most practical source of local magical information in any region. This is an advantage that gentleman magicians take some time to recognise.
title: Cunning Man or Wise Woman
description: You are the village magical practitioner — the healer, curse-breaker, and finder of lost things that every community has always had, under different names in different countries. Your magic is local, practical, and hereditary. It has always worked, quietly. Now it works loudly, which brings new kinds of attention from people who never cared about you before.

keys: Fairy Doctor , the fairy physician ,
value:
Fairy Doctor. [See also: Celtic Otherworld Magic, The Sídhe Court, Changeling Returned, The Geis, Fetch.]
A specialist in fairy-caused afflictions.
