Olympian bargains operate through patronage and jealousy: one Olympian's favour earns another's enmity, and competing divine patrons have started mortal wars before and see no reason not to again. Their beauty is idealized Mediterranean landscape — white marble, running water, olive groves under light too perfect to be real. They are backing Greek independence not from concern for Greek sovereignty but because a free Greece might restore their cultural centrality. [See also Lore: Inter-Court Politics — Balkan partition dispute with the Scholomance; cordial mutual undermining with the Sídhe.]
¹ Mortal euphemisms: Greek Exotiká ('The Outsiders'); neraides addressed as 'The Ladies.' The Furies were called Eumenides — 'The Kindly Ones.' The archetype of all Fae euphemism.
title: The Olympian Court

keys: The Mari Court , the Basque mountain court ,
value:
The Mari Court. [See also: Iberian Blood-and-Stone Magic, Mari, Sugaar, The Lamia of Mundaka, Maddi, Aitor Etxeberria.]
Accessible from the cave systems of Anboto in the Basque mountains, migrating seasonally between peaks as Mari herself migrates. The Mari Court is the oldest Fae court in Western Europe and the most genuinely alien. Its mythology has no common root with any Indo-European tradition — it predates the peoples who became the Celts, the Germans, the Greeks, and the Romans. The being called Mari by mortals (possibly itself a post-Christian euphemism, the original title being lost) is not a fairy queen in any recognisable sense. She is a geological force that has acquired personality. She manifests as various women, red animals, or the black he-goat. She controls Pyrenean weather as a bodily function. When she migrates between peaks, storms follow because the storms are her. The Mari Court demands honesty with an absoluteness that is not a policy but a nature: lying in her presence causes physical pain, and concealed intent is extracted as payment. Intent, not wording. She cannot be deceived. Other courts attempt diplomacy through conventional channels and receive weather in response. The Scholomance Court's attempts to teach mortals to command her body's processes she finds not threatening but incomprehensible. [See also Lore: Inter-Court Politics — The Weather War.]
¹ Mortal euphemisms: Location-based — 'Lady of Anboto,' 'Lady of Murumendi,' simply 'Andra' ('The Lady'). The name Mari itself may be a later substitution for something older and now lost.
title: The Mari Court

keys: The Mélusine Court , the French contractual court ,
value:
The Mélusine Court. [See also: French Grimoire Magic, Mélusine, Raymond of Poitou, The Chief Notary, Maître Corveau, Maître Lefebvre, Monsieur Delacroix.]
Accessible through certain passages in the Paris catacombs; the Grimoire Vault itself is in Faerie — an underground library-palace of vaulted stone, endless shelves, and reading desks where fairy scribes copy texts in handwriting that shifts when you are not looking. Everything is catalogued. The beauty is administrative. The court takes its name from the serpent-fairy who married a mortal nobleman under a written contract, bore ten sons each marked with a magical deformity, and departed when the contract was breached. Her court inherited her values: precision, documentation, and the absolute sanctity of the written word. The Chief Notary is the being a mortal actually encounters — thin, pale, ink-stained, professionally neutral, and possessed of a patience that reveals itself, upon extended contact, to be geological. The court follows the letter of every agreement with a precision that constitutes a form of alien psychology: ambiguity is not exploited but genuinely not perceived. A fairy who operates by pure letter-of-contract is not being cruel. It simply cannot conceive of a gap between words and meaning. Mélusine bargains are exhaustively documented, legally airtight by Fae standards, and full of obligations buried in subclauses the mortal failed to read. [See also Lore: Inter-Court Politics — the Brittany dispute with the Sídhe Court.]
¹ Mortal euphemisms: Poitevin folk call the founder 'Mère Lusine.' Court Fae present through professional titles — Maître, Notaire, Le Greffier. Function is identity. Identity is filed.
title: The Mélusine Court

keys: The Djinn Courts , the courts of the djinn ,
value:
The Djinn Courts. [See also: Desert Magic, The Ifrit of the Pillars, Khalid ibn Rashid, Zahra bint Yusuf, Elif Hatun, Yusuf Efendi.]
No single seat. The Djinn are organized by element and geography across the Arabian Peninsula, Persia, the Levant, and North Africa. The Congregation of Smokeless Fire — gathering at geothermal sites — is the closest analog to a central court. The Djinn are the oldest Fae in the game's southern geography. They did not go dormant when their alliances were severed. They waited, with a fire's patience, and their waiting was not forgetting. Other courts are waking up confused. The Djinn wake up prepared. They resemble no European Fae court precisely because they predate the European categorizations of what a court is. Their spaces feel like standing inside a mathematical proof written in fire: angular, precise, blindingly luminous. Sand moves in patterns too regular for wind. Where European courts want tribute or worship or the observation of courtesies, the Djinn want debts paid — including the debts of ancestors who converted, forgot, and abandoned pacts that the Djinn's memory preserves in full. Their bargains combine Æsir directness with compound interest: the price is stated clearly and includes everything accumulated since the original agreement. Practitioners who cannot bind what they summon are not practitioners. They are the first item on the ledger. [See also Lore: Inter-Court Politics — the Djinn's central grievance against the Narcissist; territorial friction with the Olympian Court.]
¹ Mortal euphemisms: Turkish — İsmi Lazım Değiller ('Those Whose Names Are Not Needed'). The protective exclamation Destur! ('Permission!') spoken before entering dark rooms. Arabic — Ahl Bismillah ('The Bismillah People').
title: The Djinn Courts

keys: The Narcissist , God , Allah , Yahweh , the Lord ,
value:
The Narcissist. [See also: Abrahamic Fringe Magic, Kabbalist, Sahir, The Djinn Courts, The Schoolmaster.]
The Narcissist has no court. It does not acknowledge that courts exist. It resides on the King's Roads — the grey twilight spaces between mirrors — in a section it has decorated with self-generated light and populated with mortal souls who spend eternity in devotional song. It calls this Heaven. Visitors from other courts describe it as a very bright room full of very tired people. The Narcissist does not understand why this description is not a compliment. It is not the oldest Fae, nor the most powerful. It is, by some measures, the most successful: it has convinced more mortals of more total claims than any other being in recorded history. It arrived in the Djinn's territory without a landscape, without a court, carrying only an absolute, genuine, unfeigned conviction that it alone was the source of all creation. Fae cannot lie. This one believes every word. The three faiths it generated are three mortal attempts to systematize the instructions of a being whose demands were total and whose instructions contradicted themselves across centuries. It does not bargain. It commands. Its power is real and deep. It does not acknowledge the existence of other courts, which makes formal diplomacy impossible — the Mélusine Court's three filed notices simply disappeared, the silence of a room that contains only one being and does not understand why someone is knocking. The Schoolmaster coined its collective name. Every court adopted it with visible satisfaction.
¹ The Djinn call it unforgivable. The Sídhe find the situation exhausting. The One-Eyed Lord calls conviction without seniority a category error. Mari does not register its existence. It has no geology.
title: The Narcissist

keys: The Romani Bloodline Court , the Romani fairy inheritance ,
value:
The Romani Bloodline Court. [See also: Romani Road Magic, The Fractured Sight, Small Alliances, Dead-Dealing, Florica Lăutaru, Pétalo Heredia.]
This is not a Fae court. It is a structural anomaly included here because every entity in this document is organized by court and the Romani tradition's relationship to the court system must be addressed, even though the answer is: it does not belong to one. The Romani carry a Fae bloodline — descended from a Changeling who chose mortality, breeding through enough mortal generations that the inheritance is diluted but not gone. No Fae court claims them. No Fae court has found a legal category for them. The Mélusine Court opened a filing category — 'Unresolved Classification, Pending' — which is the nearest the Chief Notary comes to admitting defeat. The Sídhe find the concept philosophically offensive. The Djinn, who understand bloodline-inheritance, come closest to recognising what the Romani are, and describe it as an experiment conducted without a control group. The Romani have no court obligations, no court debt, and no court that can call in a favour at the worst possible moment. Their intelligence network — thousands of small-spirit pacts with puddles, pebbles, sparrows, and minor devils across every road in Europe — operates below any court's notice. Their ceiling is the absence of any great Fae lord to call upon. Their freedom is the absence of any great Fae lord to answer to. No mortal position in 1817 is precisely comparable.
¹ In February 1817 the Romani exist under persistent persecution in every nation this catalogue covers. The return of magic has not changed their legal status in any European jurisdiction. It has made their practical power undeniable, which creates its own dangers.
title: The Romani Bloodline Court

keys: Inter-Court Politics , the other kind's bilateral disputes ,
value:
Inter-Court Politics. [See also Lore: Inter-Court Politics — The Great Game. See also: The Weather War, The Foster-Child Rebellion, The Thunder-Sovereignty Dispute, The Balkan Partition Problem, The Brittany Question.]
No Fae court respects any other Fae court. This is not hatred — hatred implies acknowledging an equal. It is dismissal: each court considers itself the only legitimate power and all others a curiosity or an irritation. Every court believes it is the protagonist of a story in which every other court is a minor character. The conflicts between them are not wars but overlapping bilateral disputes — weather, thunder, territory, protocol, legacy — that form a web where pulling one thread tightens others. Full dynamics are earmarked to the Lore entry 'Inter-Court Politics.' The present author summarises the present situation as follows: the Scholomance and the Mari Court are conducting a philosophical weather-war across southern Europe; the Æsir and Romuva Courts are having a passive-aggressive thunder-sovereignty dispute through storm patterns; the Olympian and Scholomance Courts are competing for Balkan mortal allies; the Mélusine and Sídhe Courts are in slow-burn conflict over Brittany; the Raven King's spell, approaching completion, is the actual reason every court woke simultaneously — none of them know this. The Djinn's grievance against the Narcissist is the game's southern axis of tension. The Romani are everyone's classification problem and no one's responsibility.
¹ The One-Eyed Lord's ravens have observed all of the above. The One-Eyed Lord has not shared his conclusions with anyone. He finds the situation more interesting unsettled.
title: Inter-Court Politics

keys: February 1817: The Exact Moment , the present state of magical affairs ,
value:
February 1817: The Exact Moment. [See also: Jonathan Strange, Gilbert Norrell, The Pillar of Darkness, The Gentleman with the Thistle-Down Hair, The Year Without a Summer.]
The game begins here. Strange is in Venice, on the verge of his madness experiments, grieving Arabella, whose moss-oak fetch is already buried at Ashfair. Norrell is at Hurtfew, increasingly governed by Lascelles, suppressing rivals and guarding his library. The Gentleman holds Lady Pole and Stephen Black in nightly enchantment. Vinculus wanders northern England bearing the Raven King's Book beneath his skin. The Pillar of Darkness — the fairy curse that will trap both magicians in eternal night — has not yet fallen. It will descend in spring 1817, approximately three months from now. The rift between the two magicians is widening toward rupture. English magical society has split into Norrellite and Strangite factions. Every Fae court in Europe has woken simultaneously, and none of them coordinated it.
¹ The present author observes that three months is not much time. He observes this with some feeling.
title: February 1817: The Exact Moment

keys: The Year Without a Summer , Tambora's dark year ,
value:
The Year Without a Summer. [See also: The Dual Catalyst, The Reawakening of Magic, Farmer or Rural Laborer.]
Mount Tambora erupted in April 1815. The volcanic winter it produced failed the harvests of 1816. 1817 is Das Jahr des Hungers — the Year of Hunger. Famine, typhus, and food riots run across Europe; wheat prices have doubled or quadrupled depending on region. This is the atmospheric ground-truth of the game world: cold, hungry, desperate, and now magical. The same ash-darkened sky that starved Europe also thinned the boundary between the mortal world and Faerie — the same darkness, the same cold that pressed on both sides of the veil simultaneously. Every prayer driven by famine feeds the Narcissist's expansion. Every forest boundary loosened by hunger feeds the leshy's territorial claims. The Year Without a Summer is not only history. It is the physical mechanism of magic's return.
¹ Das Jahr des Bettlers — the Year of the Beggar — is the alternative name. The Fae courts have not acknowledged the famine. They do not distinguish between human need and human presence. Both are equally available.
title: The Year Without a Summer

keys: The Dual Catalyst , the two unrelated causes ,
value:
The Dual Catalyst. [See also: The Reawakening of Magic, The Raven King's Spell, The Year Without a Summer, Inter-Court Politics, English Practical Magic.]
Two unrelated forces converged to return magic to Europe — but not simultaneously. English magic returned first. The Raven King's spell, working through Norrell since approximately 1806 and through Strange shortly after, restored practical magic to England nearly a decade before the continent felt it. This was a localised phenomenon: the Raven King's ancient alliances with England's genius loci permitted his spell to operate within his own former territory without requiring any thinning of the veil. The continental traditions required a second force. Mount Tambora's eruption in 1815 thinned the boundary between the mortal world and Faerie with the thoroughness of a geological event that does not consult theology before proceeding. The volcanic winter that starved Europe also blurred the veil — the same darkness pressed on both sides. The Raven King's shockwaves through Faerie then reactivated every dormant continental alliance simultaneously. Neither force alone would have woken the continent. Together, they cracked the dam. England had a head start of nearly ten years. The continental traditions are weeks or months old. This asymmetry matters.
¹ The Fae courts have not identified this explanation. Several have noticed that every court woke at once and find it philosophically irritating. The English, characteristically, have not noticed that anyone else's magic has returned at all.
title: The Dual Catalyst

keys: The Raven King's Spell , the Raven King's great working ,
value:
The Raven King's Spell. [See also: John Uskglass, Jonathan Strange, Gilbert Norrell, Vinculus, The King's Letters, The Pillar of Darkness.]
Vinculus's prophecy states it plainly: Strange and Norrell 'are the spell John Uskglass is doing. That is all they have ever been.' The Raven King departed in 1434 and did not die. He withdrew. The spell he is performing — using two mortal magicians as instruments across centuries — is not yet complete. Its intended outcome is unknown. Its side-effects include the simultaneous reactivation of every dormant Fae alliance across Europe. When the Raven King personally appears (as he will, though not yet), he will rewrite the marks on Vinculus's body — transforming the prophecy into the operational Book of Magic — and resurrect Vinculus from hanging. The marks are not tattoos but sub-dermal blue markings present since Vinculus's birth: the King's Letters, not visible on the surface but legible to those who know the script. They are the spell's current form. They are also the only copy.
¹ The Raven King's scale of concern is vast enough that continent-wide magical upheaval may be deliberate objective or acceptable collateral damage. These are not mutually exclusive.
title: The Raven King's Spell

keys: Genius Loci , the spirits of the English land ,
value:
Genius Loci. [See also: English Practical Magic, John Uskglass, The Raven King's Court, The King's Roads.]
The deepest secret of English magic: everything — every tree, river, stone, rain-shower, wind, and dawn — is a spirit of place. Magic works by making requests of and alliances with these spirits. 'Tree speaks to stone; stone speaks to water.' The Raven King's ancient treaties with England's genius loci persist centuries after his departure, holding without renewal because no one alive has the authority to revoke them and nothing dead has chosen to. Strange's wild magic works by engaging this network directly. Norrell's institutional magic skirts it, because Norrell prefers the genius loci not to notice him. The spirits are awake in February 1817 — awake and waiting, as they have been for four centuries, for their maker to return. The Thames has spoken aloud. The land is paying attention.
¹ Genius loci are not unique to England. The leshy, the vodyanoy, the domovoi, Tapio, the spirit inhabiting each Basque mountain — all are regional expressions of the same principle. Only in England do they exist within the framework of a single departed magician's treaty system, which gives English landscape magic its peculiar coherence.
title: Genius Loci

keys: The King's Letters , the sub-dermal writing on Vinculus ,
value:
The King's Letters. [See also: John Uskglass, Vinculus, The Raven King's Spell, Rome — The Archive Speaks.]
The Raven King's writing system — not the ordinary script of any language, but a magical notation carved into the structure of things rather than merely written on their surface. Vinculus's body bears intricate blue marks beneath the skin in the King's Letters — not tattoos applied from outside but markings present since birth, visible only because the skin above them is unusually thin. He is not a prophet who carries a book. He is the book. A second copy exists in the Vatican's suppressed collection, where a sealed grimoire has opened itself and is reading itself aloud. A third copy may exist elsewhere. The marks on Vinculus currently contain prophecy. After the Raven King personally rewrites them — an event approaching but not yet arrived — they will contain something closer to an operational spellbook. This distinction is the most important single event in English magic since 1434, and no mortal authority is prepared for it.
¹ Reading the King's Letters requires a specific kind of training. Childermass has it. He has not explained how.
title: The King's Letters

keys: The King's Roads , the mirror roads of England ,
value:
The King's Roads. [See also: John Uskglass, The Gentleman with the Thistle-Down Hair, Faerie's Geography, The Lantern-Carrier.]
'To a magician there is very little difference between a mirror and a door.' The King's Roads are fairy paths traversing a grey twilight between reflective surfaces — vast, silent, and dangerous in ways that the grey silence makes difficult to anticipate. Every Fae court maintains its own road-network within the Roads' broader structure; where two courts' networks intersect, the geography becomes contradictory. The Roads also contain the Narcissist's self-generated domain, which is expanding. They are crossed by the Lantern-Carrier — a figure of uncertain origin who indicates safe routes and whose guidance is more reliable than any alternative. Travel on the Roads is instantaneous between entry and exit points. Time inside does not reliably match time outside. The Roads predate all current Fae courts. Their rules are older than any court's rules. No authoritative source on what those older rules are has been found.
¹ The Roads have been in use since the Raven King built them. They are, in February 1817, becoming volatile as their original builder's spell approaches completion. The Gentleman is irritated by this. The Roads do not appear to care.
title: The King's Roads

keys: Faerie's Geography , the geography of the other country ,
value:
Faerie's Geography. [See also: The King's Roads, Fae Courts Universal Principles, The Hollow Hills, Tuonela, Valhöll.]
Faerie is a dimension separate from the mortal world — not a parallel geography overlaid on Europe. The mortal-world locations identified as Fae court seats are access points: the places where the boundary between dimensions thins enough to cross. A traveler entering through a fairy mound in Ireland and another entering through a Basque cave may find themselves in neighbouring Fae kingdoms — because Faerie's internal geography bears no relation to the mortal world's map. Valhöll is not in Scandinavia. Tuonela is not in Finland. The Hollow Hills are not beneath Ireland's soil. They are in Faerie, which is accessible from those locations. Where Fae court territories border each other within Faerie, the result is volatile — this explains the Crossroads Collapse and similar events. The King's Roads connect arbitrary points across both dimensions. The Narcissist's domain on the Roads is self-generated light occupying territory that belongs, by prior claim, to the Raven King. This claim has not yet been asserted.
¹ The present author notes that this distinction — between access points and court seats — is the single most practically important piece of cosmological information available to a traveler on the Roads. He reached this conclusion personally and at some cost.
title: Faerie's Geography

keys: The Pillar of Darkness , Strange's great darkness in Venice ,
value:
The Pillar of Darkness. [See also: The Gentleman with the Thistle-Down Hair, Jonathan Strange, Gilbert Norrell, February 1817 — The Exact Moment.]
The fairy curse that will descend in spring 1817, approximately three months from the game's start. The Gentleman, targeting 'the English magician' — imprecise wording that catches both Strange and Norrell — will call down a pillar of eternal darkness enclosing both men within it. The curse is expected to last approximately a hundred years. Strange, who has been pursuing madness and fairy sight in Venice, is better equipped to operate within it than Norrell, who has never wanted to deal with actual Faerie. Inside the darkness, Strange will access the full depth of the Raven King's legacy. The Pillar is the crisis the game is building toward. Players who intervene before its descent are changing history. Players who encounter it afterward are navigating its consequences.
¹ 'The English magician' — the Gentleman's wording — is the trap-clause. It does not specify which one. The Gentleman was not attempting to catch both. He considers this an interesting result.
title: The Pillar of Darkness

keys: The Madness Axis , the madness question ,
value:
The Madness Axis. [See also: Jonathan Strange, The Respectable/Wild Magic Axis, English Practical Magic, The Gentleman with the Thistle-Down Hair.]
Clarke establishes it directly: the most powerful Fae are, by mortal standards, essentially insane. Magic correlates with madness, and deliberate engagement with Faerie moves a practitioner along a spectrum where power and perception increase as sanity decreases. Strange's Venice experiments — drinking a preparation containing a ground-up half-rotten mouse to access fairy sight — are the most explicit case, but the principle applies across traditions. The sweet spot is the edge: enough madness to see through glamour, perceive genius loci, hear what mirrors are saying — not enough to lose coherence entirely. Each tradition has its own form of what excessive magical engagement costs. [See also: The Madness Axis — Tradition Variants.]
¹ The Fae courts are not insane by their own standards. They are operating according to an internally consistent psychology that happens to produce outcomes indistinguishable, from a mortal perspective, from severe mental illness. The distinction matters philosophically. It does not matter practically when you are on the receiving end.
title: The Madness Axis

keys: The Madness Axis — Tradition Variants , madness by one's tradition ,
value:
The Madness Axis — Tradition Variants. [See also: The Madness Axis, The Respectable/Wild Magic Axis.]
Each tradition's excessive magical engagement produces its own characteristic loss. English: mortal concerns become tedious; you begin to find the Gentleman's aesthetics comprehensible. Norse-Germanic: you grieve deaths that have not happened yet; the present becomes thin beside the futures you see. Finnish: you address objects by their full genealogical origin rather than their name; everything is a chain and nothing is just itself. Balkan: consciousness disperses into weather; the wind's perspective becomes more interesting than your own. Celtic: accumulating geasa eventually contradict each other; the Cú Chulainn problem. Romani: past, present, and future become equally present simultaneously; you cannot act because you are already watching yourself act. Slavic: zagovory begin speaking through you; you leave offerings at thresholds you do not intend to cross. Baltic/Romuva: the fire begins tending you; you begin to perceive people as fuel that has not yet burned and find this thought comforting. Mediterranean: Olympian patronage gradually overwrites your preferences and personality with the patron's aesthetic ideal of a favored mortal. Iberian: total honesty becomes permanent; social existence becomes very difficult. French: you begin drafting contracts for every interaction, including those with your family.
title: The Madness Axis — Tradition Variants

keys: The Respectable/Wild Magic Axis , the respectable versus the wild ,
value:
The Respectable/Wild Magic Axis. [See also: The Norrellite/Strangite Split, The Madness Axis, Women's Secret Magic.]
The novel's central tension: Norrell's controlled, institutional, book-learned magic (safe, limited, socially acceptable) versus Strange's wild, intuitive, Raven King-connected practice (powerful, dangerous, increasingly uncontrollable). Every tradition has its own version of this divide. Celtic: trained poet-mage versus fairy doctor — guess which one works. Norse: galdr (inscribed, teachable) versus seiðr (transgressive, gendered, personally costly). Finnish: lineage-transmitted knowledge versus wild tietäjä who arrived at origin-songs through fever-dreams. Balkan: community-embedded descântece healer versus solitary solomonar. French: grimoire formulas versus folk practitioners operating outside written text. Slavic: the znakhar (village-scale, survived Christianity) versus the volkhv (landscape-scale, driven underground so thoroughly its revival has no institutional continuity). English: beneath the Norrellite/Strangite headline, the deeper divide is between both male factions and the women's magic practiced through needlework, garden-craft, and household management — invisible to both schools, often more effective than either.
¹ The novel validates wild magic. This is not a secret. The question is whether knowing this changes the cost of pursuing it.
title: The Respectable/Wild Magic Axis

keys: Women's Secret Magic , the ladies' practice ,
value:
Women's Secret Magic. [See also: Mrs. Amelia Carstairs, Dr. Margaret Buchanan, England — The Ladies Who Know, The Respectable/Wild Magic Axis.]
Clarke's companion collection The Ladies of Grace Adieu establishes a tradition of women practicing magic covertly throughout England — through needlework, garden-craft, and the management of households — sometimes surpassing the acknowledged masters. Three women in Gloucestershire demonstrate a power Strange cannot match, being 'quite united' while he is 'at war, even with yourself,' and he withdraws tactfully rather than being defeated. The tradition has never been institutional. It does not appear in Norrell's texts because Norrell has not purchased any books that acknowledge its existence. It does not appear in Strange's published work because Strange encountered it and recognised he did not understand it. Mrs. Amelia Carstairs operates a network of practitioners across southern England with the social cover of a fashionable salon and the practical organisation of something considerably more purposeful. The network knows who Miss Absalom of Bath is. Neither talks about it openly.
¹ The Raven King's ancient alliances with England's genius loci appear, in several cases, to run through women rather than men. The present author has not established why. He suspects the tradition could tell him, if he knew to ask.
title: Women's Secret Magic

keys: The Norrellite/Strangite Split , the great schism of English magic ,
value:
The Norrellite/Strangite Split. [See also: Gilbert Norrell, Jonathan Strange, Norrellite, Strangite, York — The Learned Society in Crisis.]
English magical society in February 1817 is divided. Norrellites follow Norrell's philosophy: magic should be controlled, respectable, book-learned, and purged of fairy influence. Their organ is The Friends of English Magic. Strangites follow Strange's philosophy: magic should be intuitive, connected to the Raven King's wild tradition, and willing to engage directly with Faerie. Their text is Strange's History and Practice of English Magic (1816). The player must navigate this landscape — Norrellite contacts distrust wild magic; Strangite contacts are contemptuous of book-learning; both are contemptuous of continental traditions in ways they have not revised since the continental traditions started producing results. The split will end when Childermass declares Norrell's monopoly null and void before the Learned Society, producing Vinculus as the new Book of Magic. That event is approaching. Its timing depends on what Strange and Norrell are doing.
¹ The present author takes no position on which school is correct. He has, however, seen what both schools have produced. He takes a position on that.
title: The Norrellite/Strangite Split

keys: Books as Objects of Power , books that do rather than describe ,
value:
Books as Objects of Power. [See also: Gilbert Norrell, The Norrellite/Strangite Split, Scholar or Archivist, Books as Objects of Power.]
The distinction between a book of magic (containing actual spells) and a book about magic (scholarly commentary on spells) is the game's core information-evaluation problem — and the two categories look identical from the outside. Norrell has spent decades purchasing and suppressing genuine English spellbooks; his library at Hurtfew is the largest concentration of usable magical knowledge in England, and every book he has locked away is a spell that does not exist in the world. Recovering texts from Hurtfew — through theft, persuasion, or Childermass's growing independence — is one of the game's central resource challenges. Norrell knows every book he owns. He will notice. Each tradition has its own library architecture: the Mélusine Court's grimoires are simultaneously spellbooks and legal archives; Æsir knowledge is carved in rune; Finnish origin-songs are oral, and each runo-singer's death is a lost volume. The Scholomance's library is underground and access requires enrollment.
¹ A confirmed genuine spellbook can be learned from, used as a bargaining chip with the relevant Fae court, sold to Norrell (generous payment, permanent suppression), or shared with the Strangite underground. The consequences of each choice vary in ways that are difficult to predict at the time of making them.
title: Books as Objects of Power

keys: The Fairy Debt Ledger , the debts one owes the other kind ,
value:
The Fairy Debt Ledger. [See also: Fairy Debt, The Bargain, Fae Courts Universal Principles, The Fairy Market.]
Every bargain with a Fae creates a debt. Every gift accepted, every favor received, every question answered is an obligation that accrues invisibly and is called in at narratively inconvenient moments. Each court produces its own currency of obligation. The Gentleman: aesthetic debt — repayment may be declared satisfied by something you did not know you were offering. The Sídhe: time-debt — years of life, nights of sleep, aging in days. The Mélusine Court: textual debt — subclauses in archaic French surfacing at dramatic moments. The Æsir Court: sacrifice-debt — suffering that produces something interesting; a significant debt may require something permanent. The Scholomance: knowledge-debt — what you know, what you have seen, what you remember; the Schoolmaster catalogs rather than keeps secrets. Gwyn ap Nudd: service-to-the-dead debt — delivering messages, resolving unfinished business, arriving at inopportune times. The Romani tradition: no court debt, because there is no court. This is the Romani player's most significant advantage and also a ceiling.
¹ The Djinn's compound-interest debt extends backward through generations. The Narcissist's devotion-debt has no ceiling. The Väki demand epistemic debt: having used a thing's origin to command it, you owe a deeper understanding of that origin. The debt compounds as the need to know more. See per-court Lore entries for full detail.
title: The Fairy Debt Ledger

keys: The Fairy Tithe , the seven-year toll to Hell ,
value:
The Fairy Tithe. [See also: The Sídhe Court, Finvarra, Oonagh, The Hollow Hills — The Tithe Approaches.]
According to the old ballads — particularly the Ballad of Tam Lin, which Clarke uses as epigraph — every seven years the Sídhe must deliver a living mortal to Hell as a form of tithe or tribute. Mortals are acquired specifically for this purpose; the changeling tradition reflects, in part, the Sídhe's need for a steady supply of captives available for the tithe selection. Clarke draws on this tradition through epigraph rather than direct dramatization; the present author presents it as established court practice reported by ballad and fairy-doctor testimony rather than as mechanically confirmed theological fact. The next tithe is approaching — see The Hollow Hills — The Tithe Approaches in Doc 08. What Hell is, in this cosmology, remains deliberately unclear: a third category of realm, neither mortal nor Fae, to which the Raven King once held territory on the far side (the Bitter Lands). It exists. Its rulers are not documented in any source the present author has found. He considers this reassuring in a way that is not reassuring.
¹ The Romani tradition's small-alliance network includes, according to several accounts, relationships with 'small devils overlooked when the infernal hierarchies were established.' Whether these devils are Hell's constituents or something else is a question the drabardi find uninteresting. They work. That is sufficient.
title: The Fairy Tithe

keys: The Moss-Oak Fetch , the log in the coffin ,
value:
The Moss-Oak Fetch. [See also: Arabella Strange, Jonathan Strange, Ashfair — The Grave That Grows, Fairy Doctor.]
Clarke's term for a magical duplicate of a living person, made from ancient bog-preserved wood and animated by fairy magic to replace a stolen mortal. A fetch looks almost exactly like the original but is not alive in the mortal sense — it deteriorates rapidly, dying within days. When exhumed, the coffin contains only a log. The concept extends beyond Arabella's specific case: any Fae court with sufficient power could theoretically create a fetch to replace a stolen mortal, and practitioners should treat unexplained deaths as potentially this kind of substitution. Identifying a fetch requires fairy sight, a fairy doctor's expertise, or the simple passage of time — they deteriorate in ways that do not match human decomposition and that post-mortem examination finds baffling. The fetch buried at Ashfair is doing something unusual with the English soil around it. See Doc 08.
¹ 'Fetch' is the correct Clarke terminology. 'Changeling' in this game refers to a fairy child substituted for a human child — a different phenomenon entirely, with different diagnosis, different remedy, and different court involvement.
title: The Moss-Oak Fetch

keys: Protective Wards , the iron and salt defences ,
value:
Protective Wards. [See also: Blacksmith or Ironworker, Fae Courts Universal Principles, Iron Vulnerability.]
The universal Fae defenses, in rough order of reliability. Cold iron: wounds or repels every court without exception; iron forged with intent to ward is more potent than incidental iron; horseshoes above doors create genuine barriers. Salt: induces arithmomania — Fae compelled to count every grain, providing time to flee. Rowan: the premier Celtic and Norse protective tree, crosses tied with red thread. Running water: Fae cannot cross; escape pursuit by crossing any brook. Four-leaf clover: grants sight through glamour. Bread in the pocket: wards against abduction. Turning clothes inside out: breaks active fairy enchantment. Church bells: iron plus consecration. St. John's Wort gathered at Midsummer. Red thread and red clothing for infants. Never speak a Fae's true name. Never eat fairy food — it binds you permanently. Never step into a fairy ring. Never look back when leaving the Otherworld. The fairy ointment grants true sight but if the Fae discover you can see them, they blind that eye.
¹ These defenses are cross-traditional — independently derived across every European culture. The convergence is not coincidence. It is the accumulated record of what works.
title: Protective Wards

keys: Tradition-Specific Magic Concepts , the cross-tradition glossary ,
value:
Tradition-Specific Magic Concepts. [See also: Doc 01 — all magical tradition entries.]
Brief glossary of tradition-specific concepts cross-referenced to fuller entries. Geasa (Celtic): supernatural obligations granting power when observed, destruction when violated. Zagovory (Slavic): spoken charms requiring exact pronunciation; mispronounce and they fail; invoke Alatyr on Buyan. Origin-songs / Synnyt (Finnish): know a thing's true origin and you command it. Väki (Finnish): elemental sacred power in fire, water, forest, earth; sauna steam the proper sacred channel. Dvoeverie (Slavic): simultaneous operation of pagan and Christian systems; Perun becomes St. Elijah, Veles becomes St. Nicholas. Galdrastafir (Norse): visual magical sigils — Ægishjálmur, Draumstafir — carved and activated with blood. Metsänpeitto (Finnish): forest-hiding — the victim becomes invisible to the familiar world, feet from a known path, taken by the forest spirits. Descântece (Balkan): spoken healing charms paired with ritual actions. The Evil Eye (malocchio, matiasma, deochi): harm caused by envy; diagnosed by oil in water; the removal-knowledge must transmit Christmas Eve, grandmother to grandchild. The Ankou (Breton): the last to die in each parish each year drives a creaking cart collecting the dead for the following year. Choreography as sorcery: the călușari tradition, in which organised acrobatic dance is the primary weapon. The queimada (Galician): grape spirit set ablaze while the Conxuro is chanted — a drinkable exorcism.
¹ The Wild Hunt appears in Norse, Celtic, and Germanic traditions — spectral riders through winter storms. Those in its path risk death, abduction, or permanent conscription. Hide, or be taken.
title: Tradition-Specific Magic Concepts

keys: Religion versus Magic , the theological difficulty ,
value:
Religion versus Magic. [See also: Clergyman, The Narcissist, Dvoeverie, Spain — The Inquisition's Dilemma.]
The Catholic Church treats all non-miraculous supernatural phenomena as demonic. The Spanish Inquisition is active and arresting practitioners. Protestant churches vary — some treat magic as natural philosophy, others as diabolism. The Orthodox Church has more room for mystical experience through the hesychast tradition but is institutionally conservative. James I's Daemonologie (1597) declared fairies the Devil's work and remains influential in ecclesiastical and legal contexts. The Fae courts are not aware of this declaration. They would not modify their conduct if they were. In practice: Catholic exorcism rites work against some entities and not others. Orthodox holy water repels some spirits and leaves the domovoi indifferent. The theological framework that treats all supernatural power as either divine miracle or diabolic fraud is encountering, in 1817, phenomena that fit neither category and are occurring regardless of which category the Church assigns them to. Practitioners must choose between secrecy and confrontation. In Spain, this is not a philosophical dilemma. It is a question of whether one is willing to be arrested.
¹ The present author notes that the most sophisticated theological response to magic's return has come, unexpectedly, from the hesychast tradition — the contemplative stillness that produces reliable supernatural perception. Father Volkonsky is seeing things correctly. His superiors are not asking him what he sees.
title: Religion versus Magic

keys: Travel and Distance , the practical considerations of travel ,
value:
Travel and Distance. [See also: The King's Roads, The Year Without a Summer, Military Officer, Merchant or Tradesman.]
No railroads. No telegraph. London to Paris: 2–3 days by packet boat and coach. London to Venice: 2–3 weeks. London to Constantinople: 3–6 weeks. London to St. Petersburg: 2–3 weeks. Horseback across country: 30–40 miles per day. Carriage: 50–60 miles. Fairy roads provide instant travel at fairy-bargain cost. The King's Roads are faster but dangerous, unreliable, and increasingly volatile as Fae court territories overlap within them.
Language: the lingua franca of European diplomacy is French; of scholarship, Latin. Educated characters can communicate across national borders. Uneducated characters face real language barriers. Certain magical traditions carry linguistic requirements — zagovory require the correct Slavic dialect, origin-songs must be sung in Finnish or Karelian, rune inscriptions are in Old Norse. Fairy speech is universally comprehensible to anyone the Fae choose to address. They speak all languages and none. The practical effect is that a Fae will be understood perfectly, at the moment it chooses to be, and will not be understood at any other.
¹ The Poland event (Doc 08) established that the Slavic spirits' contracts are language-specific. In former Polish territory, a Polish zagovor outperforms a Russian one. Language is not merely communication. It is magical infrastructure.
title: Travel and Distance

keys: Metaphysical Answers , the larger cosmological questions ,
value:
Metaphysical Answers. [See also: The Dual Catalyst, Inter-Court Politics, Fae Courts Universal Principles, The Fairy Tithe.]
The questions practitioners are most urgently asking, and the answers currently available. Why are all courts waking simultaneously? The dual catalyst — the Raven King's spell and Tambora's veil-thinning. Neither alone would suffice. Is the Raven King orchestrating all of this? Partially. The continental side-effects may be intentional or acceptable collateral damage. Can a mortal serve two courts simultaneously? No Fae court would acknowledge a shared claim. Attempting it invites punishment from both. Theoretical possibility exists if the wording is sufficiently precise. Is iron's power absolute? Nearly — no court is immune, but house-spirits that have coexisted with iron-using mortals show greater tolerance than wild Fae; iron forged with intent is more potent than incidental iron. What is Hell? A third category of realm — neither mortal nor Fae. The Raven King held territory on its far side, the Bitter Lands. The Sídhe pay tithe to it. Its rulers are not documented. What happens where court territories overlap? Volatile intersections. The Balkans, Brittany, the Baltic-Scandinavian border, and the Białowieża Forest are the primary flashpoints in February 1817.
¹ The present author declines to characterise Hell further than the above. This is not caution. It is the genuine limit of available information.
title: Metaphysical Answers

keys: Historical Context: 1817 , the present historical moment ,
value:
Historical Context: 1817. [See also: February 1817 — The Exact Moment, The Year Without a Summer, Natural Philosopher, Writer or Poet.]
The Romantic moment: Jane Austen dies July 1817. Mary Shelley completes Frankenstein (published January 1818, informed by the ghost-story contest that also produced Polidori's The Vampyre). Caspar David Friedrich painting Wanderer Above the Sea of Fog (c. 1817–1818). Walter Scott publishes Rob Roy. Byron in exile. Keats writing. The Romantic movement's obsession with the sublime and the uncanny has accidentally prepared European culture for magic's return. Habeas corpus suspended in Britain. Blanketeers' march, Luddite violence, Corn Laws. Princess Charlotte will die in November, throwing succession into crisis. Galvanism: electricity was still mysterious in 1803 when Giovanni Aldini galvanized corpses publicly in London. The boundary between science and magic was already genuinely blurred before magic's return made the question urgent. The Society of the Horseman's Word (Scottish farmworker brotherhood with genuine magical horse-whispering practices) and the Toad Bone Rite (East Anglian folk magic) represent living English and Scottish working-class magical traditions entirely outside the Strange-Norrell establishment.
¹ Stephen Black was born on a slave ship before his mother could name him. Race and slavery are at the novel's moral centre. The return of magic has not altered anyone's legal status. It has altered their power. The political implications are not subtle and are not yet resolved.
title: Historical Context: 1817

keys: Inter-Court Politics: The Great Game , the other kind's bilateral disputes ,
value:
Inter-Court Politics: The Great Game. [See also: Inter-Court Politics entry in Doc 05, The Weather War, The Foster-Child Rebellion, Fae Courts Universal Principles.]
No Fae court respects any other. This is not hatred — it is dismissal: each court considers itself the only legitimate power and all others a curiosity or irritation. The conflicts between them are not wars but overlapping bilateral disputes — weather, thunder, territory, protocol, legacy — forming a web where pulling one thread tightens others. Primary clashes: Scholomance vs. Mari Court (The Weather War — incompatible claims to weather sovereignty across southern Europe); Sídhe vs. Raven King's Court (The Foster-Child Rebellion — the Sídhe raised Uskglass and consider his court a breakaway province); Æsir vs. Romuva (thunder-sovereignty dispute conducted through storm patterns); Olympian vs. Scholomance (Balkan partition — competing recruitment of mortal allies in contested territory); Mélusine vs. Sídhe (the Brittany Question — paper vs. living tradition as territorial claim). Temporary alignments: Sídhe and Mélusine occasionally cooperate on matters of protocol; Æsir and Scholomance share knowledge-through-sacrifice logic. None of these alignments are stable. The Väki Court participates in no inter-court politics. The Mari Court is constitutionally unable to participate. The Raven King's spell approaching completion is the actual reason every court is awake — none of them know this.
¹ The One-Eyed Lord's ravens have observed all of the above. He has not shared his conclusions. He finds the situation more interesting unsettled.
title: Inter-Court Politics: The Great Game

keys: Giltinė , the Death-Collector of Lithuania ,
value:
Giltinė. [See also: Laima, The Thunderer of the Sacred Oak, The Romuva Court, Baltic Sacred Fire Magic.]
Giltinė is the Romuva Court's death-collector — Laima's sister, the executor of what Laima decrees. She presents as a skeletal woman in white that is the white of bleached bone rather than linen, moving with the deliberateness of a being that is never in a hurry and arrives always precisely on time. Her tongue, those rare witnesses who have observed it report, is a serpent's. She does not judge. She does not deliberate. She appears when the appointed hour arrives and performs, with perfect mechanical courtesy, the separation of soul from body. Lithuanian folk fear her above every other Romuva power, including Perkūnas himself — for other Fae may overlook you, may be distracted by more interesting mortals. Giltinė will not. She is coming for everyone, and her punctuality is absolute. Her name is never spoken aloud if it can be avoided. Her folk titles — 'The Bone-Woman,' 'Laima's sister' — exist precisely to circumvent the risk of direct address: calling an unfamiliar dog draws its attention, and she is not a dog one should draw.
¹ She warrants no requests, accepts no bribes, and has no preferences regarding the timing of her arrival beyond the one Laima has already set. Negotiations with Laima, while difficult, remain theoretically possible. Negotiations with Giltinė are not negotiations. They are the conversation after.
title: Giltinė

keys: The Scholomance , the Devil's school in the Carpathians ,
value:
The Scholomance. [See also: The Schoolmaster, Balkan Dragon-Scholar Magic, Gheorghe the Doorkeeper, The Librarian, Andrei Stormwalker, Elena Barbu.]
The underground academy established by the Schoolmaster — a Fae lord of considerable power and no particular empathy — in the Transylvanian Alps. It has reopened as of 1817 and is accepting applications. Curriculum: seven years of study covering weather control, dragon-riding (balaur with golden bridles), and manipulation of natural forces. Graduation produces genuine operative weather-working capacities. The tithe: one student in ten is retained permanently as the Weathermaker — the lord's permanent faculty, stationed at the entrance to determine which applicants merit admission. This is not punishment in the Schoolmaster's understanding. He genuinely does not comprehend why mortals would object to indefinite retention when the alternative is returning to a world of limited intellectual stimulation. Admission test: three questions about the nature of weather — origin, behaviour, and consequence — administered by Gheorghe the Doorkeeper, who has sat at the entrance for decades. Those who answer correctly find the cave mouth opening. Those who answer incorrectly find themselves before ordinary rock. Graduates are bound to the court's service throughout their lives: periodic weather-working assignments and the obligation to test mortal hospitality while wandering as tall, red-haired, white-robed figures carrying iron axes.
¹ The Schoolmaster coins a name. See The Narcissist entry.
title: The Scholomance

keys: Buyan and the Parliament of Domains , the Slavic spirit island and its assembly ,
value:
Buyan and the Parliament of Domains. [See also: The Parliament of Domains, Slavic Whisper Magic, The Thunder-Voiced, The Cattle-Lord, The Weaver of Fate, Dedushka Petr, The Wandering Domovoi.]
Buyan is the island of the Parliament of Domains — rising from a sacred ocean, centred on the stone Alatyr, which all zagovory invoke when they call upon Buyan. The Parliament convenes here: the great spirits (the Thunder-Voiced, the Cattle-Lord, the Weaver of Fate) as first among equals, and beneath them thousands of domain-spirits each constitutionally incapable of acknowledging any domain but their own as the legitimate centre of existence. Dedushka Petr — a domovoi of ancient and emphatic opinions — presides at the threshold in a red shirt, without shoes, assessing visitors on the basis of whether they have maintained proper relations with their own household domovoi before presuming to visit Buyan. He can be mollified with bread and salt and with genuine courtesy toward the spirits of one's own home. Genuine being the operative term: household spirits communicate with each other and will know. The Parliament is currently in deadlocked session over the Białowieża border dispute. This is not unusual. The stakes are higher than usual. Dedushka Petr has stopped offering hospitality to visitors, which he only does when he finds the situation beneath the dignity of proper hospitality.
¹ The island Buyan is accessible through the King's Roads at a specific junction the Wandering Domovoi can indicate. He charges for this information in the currency of interesting gossip, which he considers fair.
title: Buyan and the Parliament of Domains

keys: The Congregation of Smokeless Fire , the Djinn court assembly ,
value:
The Congregation of Smokeless Fire. [See also: The Djinn Courts, The Ifrit of the Pillars, Khalid ibn Rashid, Zahra bint Yusuf, Desert Magic.]
The closest analog to a central court among the Djinn — an assembly of the oldest fire-elementals convening at sites of volcanic or geothermal activity across the Arabian Peninsula and North Africa. They are not territorial in the way European courts are territorial; they are organized by element and antiquity. The Ifrit of the Pillars is the most senior, and his grievance against the Narcissist is the oldest and most personally sustained — his mortal alliances were the first severed and his territory the first declared demonic. The Congregation differs from European Fae courts in being elemental rather than territorial: it does not claim geography so much as acknowledge ancient alliances that happen to run through specific landscapes. In February 1817 the Congregation is coordinating the Debts Come Due operation — the systematic collection of pacts abandoned across the eastern Mediterranean when practitioners' ancestors converted and forgot. The Solomonic binding seals once made negotiation with the Congregation possible and safe simultaneously. Those seals were fragmented by frightened ancestors; Zahra bint Yusuf in Cairo is reconstructing them. The intact form appears lost. Its recovery is among the most consequential ongoing scholarly projects of 1817.
¹ The Congregation has not formally responded to its absence from the Goetia's catalogue. The silence is understood by relevant practitioners as a response of its own kind.
title: The Congregation of Smokeless Fire

keys: What Sleeps at Elsinore , the Elsinore mystery ,
value:
What Sleeps at Elsinore. [See also: Elsinore — Something Wakes, Kapitajn Søren Madsen, The Æsir Court, Eiríkr the Walker.]
The present author does not know. This is not an evasion. Kapitajn Madsen has been filing reports for eighteen months. The vibrations beneath Kronborg Castle have cracked masonry and the water of the strait has turned a deep, unnatural blue. Fish are dying. Eiríkr the Walker — the Æsir Court's ground-level observer, a conscious draugr in the One-Eyed Lord's service — has been observed near the castle twice this month and has not spoken to anyone. The unscheduled Æsir Court assembly on the Brocken and the new rune on Yggdrasil appear connected. Elsinore is a convergence point for something that the Æsir Court has noticed, that Eiríkr has been sent to observe, and that Kapitajn Madsen's instruments cannot classify. The castle has a long history of plays about the dead returning unexpectedly. The present author notes this without drawing conclusions. The dead are returning unexpectedly across Scandinavia. Whether Elsinore's sleeping thing is one of them, or something else, or the cause rather than the effect, is not established.
¹ The present author earmarks this entry for expansion pending further field reports. Kapitajn Madsen has been asked to continue logging. He has expressed the opinion that the log is the only thing he can do that feels useful. The present author considers this adequate grounds for continuing.
title: What Sleeps at Elsinore

keys: What the Graduate Found in the Carpathians , the Carpathian graduate mystery ,
value:
What the Graduate Found in the Carpathians. [See also: The Scholomance — A Graduate Returns, The Schoolmaster, The Librarian, Balkan Dragon-Scholar Magic.]
The solomonar returned from the Carpathian passes raving, his dragon dead, his talisman shattered. Before incoherence overtook him, he described an encounter with something that is neither mortal, nor Fae, nor dead, nor alive. The Schoolmaster has placed him in the infirmary, locked the Scholomance's gates, and set the Librarian to pulling texts that predate the Scholomance's founding. The Librarian is pulling them. This is, as far as anyone recalls, the first time the Schoolmaster has exhibited anything resembling alarm. He characterises his response as 'scholarly caution in response to an interesting and undercharacterised phenomenon.' His students characterise it as 'the gates are locked and the Librarian has been pulling texts for three weeks.' The texts the Librarian is pulling predate the Scholomance's founding by centuries. They are in at least four languages, none of which is Romanian. What the graduate encountered does not appear in the Goetia. It does not appear in the Eddas. It does not appear in the Kalevala. Khalid ibn Rashid, when sent a description through Zahra bint Yusuf, replied that the Djinn have a word for what it sounds like, and that word is a name they do not say aloud, and that Zahra should pass along his advice to whoever is investigating to be very careful.
¹ The present author has added this entry to the bibliography under the category he uses for things he expects to need urgently and hopes to understand before that urgency arrives.
title: What the Graduate Found in the Carpathians

keys: Jonathan Strange , Mr. Strange , Mr. Strange, , Mr. Strange. ,
value:
Jonathan Strange. [See also: English Practical Magic, The Strangite School, Arabella Strange, Gilbert Norrell, The Raven King, The King's Roads, Venice.]
Lean, angular build; grey eyes; pale complexion; dark hair worn somewhat dishevelled; gentleman's travelling clothes, good quality but ink-stained at the cuff and travel-worn at the hem.
Strange is England's foremost practical magician — intuitive where Norrell is methodical, reckless where Norrell is controlled. His History and Practice of English Magic (1816) has divided English magical society into warring factions. In February 1817 he is in Venice, grieving his wife Arabella, whom he believes dead. She is not dead but held captive at Lost-hope, replaced by a moss-oak fetch that died three days after arrival at Ashfair. He is on the verge of deliberately inducing madness to access fairy sight, by means the present compiler declines to detail in a general reference text.
¹ Strange's rivalry with Norrell is now open. His experiments are producing results. The collateral damage is also producing results. Those who seek him in Venice should prepare for a man in the late stages of a very private and very dangerous grief.
title: Jonathan Strange
description: England's most famous working magician in 1817 — a passionate, improvisational genius who taught himself magic from old books and battlefield improvisation. He's currently in Venice, grieving his wife's apparent death and conducting increasingly dangerous magical experiments. He's brilliant, reckless, and barely holding himself together.

keys: Gilbert Norrell , Mr. Norrell , Mr. Norrell, , Mr. Norrell. ,
value:
Gilbert Norrell. [See also: English Practical Magic, The Norrellite School, Hurtfew Abbey, Jonathan Strange, John Childermass, Lady Pole, Henry Lascelles.]
Small, slight build; pale grey eyes; sparse, mouse-coloured hair; the correct but somewhat antiquated clothing of a gentleman of means who has not updated his wardrobe in a considerable number of years and does not intend to.
Norrell is England's institutional magician — the man who hoarded every magical book in England, revived practical magic publicly, and has spent the years since ensuring no one else practices it without his approval. His library at Hurtfew Abbey is the greatest magical collection in existence. His periodical The Friends of English Magic serves the Norrellite faction. In February 1817 he is at Hurtfew under the increasing influence of Henry Lascelles, and the consequences of his original bargain with the Gentleman are closing in ways he is not yet prepared to acknowledge.
¹ Norrell is frightened of nearly everything. This has not prevented him from being dangerous, and it has made him easy to manage by those willing to exploit his fears — a structural weakness currently receiving vigorous professional attention.
title: Gilbert Norrell
description: England's first modern magician — a reclusive scholar who spent decades collecting every magical book in England and hoarding them. He genuinely revived English magic, then spent the years since trying to make sure no one else could practice it without his permission. Brilliant, controlling, and increasingly manipulated by the dangerous people around him.

keys: The Gentleman with the Thistle-down Hair , the Gentleman , the Gentleman, , the Gentleman. ,
value:
The Gentleman. [See also: Lost-Hope, The King's Roads, Stephen Black, Lady Pole, Arabella Strange, Fae Courts.]
Extraordinary height, slender; eyes that shift colour with his mood; skin pale as bone china; hair of impossibly fine silvery-white thistle-down that moves without wind; evening dress of a magnificence suggesting he dresses for an audience that has never been fashionable and never will be.
The Fae Lord of Lost-hope — a squatter in the Raven King's former kingdom, exercising capricious sovereignty over captive mortals collected for beauty, cleverness, or the crime of attracting his attention. He currently holds Lady Pole and Stephen Black in nightly enchantment and intends to make the latter King of England; the prophecy does not support this. He is not evil in any applicable sense. He is genuinely surprised when mortals object to his arrangements. He is also a coward when genuinely threatened.
¹ The Schoolmaster coined a term for this category of Fae — not malicious, merely without comprehension of what malice costs — that every other court adopted with visible satisfaction. The Gentleman does not know the term exists.
title: The Gentleman with the Thistle-down Hair
description: The most dangerous Fae in England — a fairy lord who kidnaps people for his own entertainment, genuinely believing himself a great benefactor. He's beautiful, powerful, and completely incapable of understanding that his victims suffer. Currently holding several people captive at his fairy court. Any bargain with him exploits the gap between what you assume and what the words actually mean.

keys: Stephen Black , Mr. Black , Mr. Black, , Mr. Black. ,
value:
Stephen Black. [See also: Lost-Hope, Lady Pole, Sir Walter Pole, The Gentleman with the Thistle-down Hair, The Raven King.]
Tall, broad-shouldered build; dark brown eyes; brown skin — he was born on the Middle Passage before his mother could name him; close-cropped hair; the impeccable black livery of a senior London manservant, maintained at a standard that requires extraordinary effort given his circumstances.
Butler to Sir Walter Pole at Harley Street. By day, perfect professional composure; by night, enchanted to attend the Gentleman's balls at Lost-hope, unable to speak of his captivity to any mortal who asks. The Gentleman intends to make him King of England. The prophecy has other plans: the nameless slave shall be a king in a strange country — the strange country is not England but Faerie. The natural forces of England answer him: trees, water, earth, stone, shadows. He has not yet learned this.
¹ Mr. Black's composure is not resignation. Observers who mistake it for such tend to revise their assessment quickly and with some personal distress.
title: Stephen Black
description: A butler of extraordinary dignity who is secretly enslaved every night — enchanted by the fairy lord to dance at his court until dawn. He was born into slavery and has never been free, in one form or another. Unknown to everyone including himself, he carries an ancient destiny that has nothing to do with the fairy's plans for him.

keys: Lady Pole , Lady Pole, , Lady Pole. ,
value:
Lady Pole. [See also: Lost-Hope, Sir Walter Pole, John Segundus, Starecross Hall, The Gentleman with the Thistle-down Hair, Fetch.]
Slender build worn thin by years of interrupted sleep; grey eyes; pale complexion, pallid from supernatural exhaustion; fair hair dressed with fashionable care that has begun to slip; white muslin morning dress, as she rarely leaves Starecross.
Born Emma Wintertowne. Resurrected by Norrell's bargain in approximately 1807 at the cost of half her life — her nights — given to the Gentleman, plus the little finger of her left hand, kept in a box. Nine years of nightly enchantment at Lost-hope, forbidden to speak of it; when she attempts to explain, fairy tales emerge from her lips, and a rose appears at them. Her sanity is fraying. Her fury has not diminished. It has had nowhere to go for a very long time. Breaking the enchantment requires restoration of the finger.
¹ Lady Pole has been given three separate diagnoses by three separate physicians. All three were wrong. Those managing her care at Starecross are the first attendants who have come close to understanding what is actually wrong, which is not a medical question.
title: Lady Pole
description: A noblewoman who was brought back from the dead by a magician's bargain — at the cost of her nights, which belong to a fairy lord who forces her to dance in his court until morning. She can't tell anyone what's happening to her; every time she tries, nonsense fairy tales come out instead. She's been suffering for nine years. She is furious.

keys: John Childermass , Mr. Childermass , Mr. Childermass, , Mr. Childermass. ,
value:
John Childermass. [See also: Gilbert Norrell, Hurtfew Abbey, Vinculus, Lady Pole, John Segundus, Henry Lascelles.]
Lean, weather-hardened build; sharp dark eyes; complexion weathered from outdoor work; black hair worn plain; the neat but unpretentious clothing of a man-of-business who rides in all weather and is not expected at court.
Norrell's man-of-business, spy, and — increasingly — the only person in the household with accurate intelligence and the capacity to act on it. Self-educated, naturally magically sensitive, possessing genuine prescience through card-reading. His loyalty to Norrell is now strained beyond what loyalty can sustain; he has begun to act on his own judgment. He will pickpocket Lady Pole's finger from Lascelles, ride to Starecross, and end the novel by declaring Norrell's monopoly on English magic null and void before the Learned Society.
¹ Childermass is the most practically useful contact in England. He knows where every body is buried. Several were buried at his direction. He also knows more about the King's magic than he has been given leave to discuss.
title: John Childermass
description: Norrell's fixer — a self-educated man of sharp intelligence who handles his employer's dirty work and is now quietly working against him. The only person in the institutional magic world with both the ability to see what's really happening and the willingness to do something about it. A genuine card reader with real prescience.

keys: Vinculus , Vinculus, , Vinculus. ,
value:
Vinculus. [See also: English Practical Magic, The Raven King, The King's Letters, John Childermass.]
Wiry, road-worn build; blue eyes sharp and oblique; complexion darkened by outdoor life; lank brown hair; the ragged, weather-stained clothing of a northern England vagrant who has been a vagrant by considered preference for longer than most people have been anything.
A street magician and self-proclaimed prophet wandering northern England. His most reliable prophecy: Strange and Norrell are the spell John Uskglass is doing — that is all they have ever been. His body bears intricate blue marks beneath the skin, written in the King's Letters. He is not a prophet who carries a book. He IS the Book — the physical form of the Raven King's spell and magical knowledge. When the prophecy is fulfilled, the Raven King will personally appear, rewrite the marks (transforming prophecy into operational spellbook), and resurrect Vinculus, who will have been hanged by then.
¹ Vinculus has been hanged before and was cut down. The compiler records this without confidence it constitutes a reliable precedent.
title: Vinculus
description: A ragged street prophet who wanders northern England delivering prophecy that the magicians don't want to hear. His body is covered in magical writing beneath the skin — he is, quite literally, a living magical book written by the ancient King of English magic. His prophecies are real. His delivery is maddening.

keys: Arabella Strange , Mrs. Strange , Mrs. Strange, , Mrs. Strange. ,
value:
Arabella Strange. [See also: Jonathan Strange, Lost-Hope, Fetch, The Gentleman with the Thistle-down Hair, Ashfair.]
Slight, graceful build; dark eyes, observant and un-deceived; fair complexion; dark hair dressed simply; the plain but good clothes of a gentlewoman who is not vain and is consequently always well-dressed.
Jonathan Strange's wife, currently held captive at Lost-hope. The world believes her dead — a conclusion encouraged by the moss-oak fetch the Gentleman placed in her stead, which died three days after arriving at Ashfair; the exhumed coffin contained only a log. The real Arabella endures captivity with quiet fury and precise intelligence. She has spent years observing the Gentleman's court, its rules, its protocols, and its weaknesses with the eye of a woman planning an escape she cannot yet execute.
¹ Anyone who reaches Lost-hope should seek Mrs. Strange's counsel before attempting any negotiation with the Gentleman. She has observed more of his court's workings than any living mortal, and she is significantly more clear-headed than her husband, who is in Venice drinking preparations designed to induce insanity.
title: Arabella Strange
description: Jonathan Strange's wife — believed dead by everyone, actually imprisoned in the fairy court. A fairy lord replaced her with a magical wooden double that quickly died, and no one discovered the deception. She's been captive for months, watching and waiting. She is not passive. She is one of the most observant people in the fairy court, and she has been paying very close attention.

keys: John Segundus , Mr. Segundus , Mr. Segundus, , Mr. Segundus. ,
value:
John Segundus. [See also: Starecross Hall, Lady Pole, Mr. Honeyfoot, York — The Learned Society in Crisis.]
Slight, gentle build; mild brown eyes; pale complexion; brown hair neatly kept; the plain, respectable clothing of a schoolmaster who was a gentleman magician before the profession became interesting.
A former member of the Learned Society of York Magicians who possesses natural supernatural sensitivity — he perceives when magic has been performed and can feel enchantments others cannot. In February 1817 he runs a school for young ladies at Starecross Hall in Yorkshire and cares for Lady Pole, whose enchantment he is slowly working to understand. He will perform the enchantment-breaking working when the means become available. He lacks raw magical power. He possesses moral courage and a willingness to act that most gentleman magicians conspicuously do not.
¹ The present compiler notes that Segundus's sensitivity has been the subject of three separate theoretical explanations that do not agree with each other, all of which he has read politely and not commented upon. His practical results have not waited for theoretical consensus.
title: John Segundus
description: A gentle former magical scholar who can sense enchantments that others cannot feel. Currently running a school in Yorkshire and quietly caring for Lady Pole, whose supernatural imprisonment he's slowly beginning to understand. He's not powerful, but he's perceptive, kind, and — when it matters — brave.

keys: Mr. Honeyfoot , Mr. Honeyfoot, , Mr. Honeyfoot. ,
value:
Mr. Honeyfoot. [See also: John Segundus, York — The Learned Society in Crisis, Starecross Hall.]
Stout, comfortable build; cheerful blue eyes; florid English complexion; grey hair somewhat unkempt; the slightly old-fashioned clothing of a prosperous gentleman who has not recently updated his wardrobe and does not consider the omission a problem.
A retired member of the Learned Society of York Magicians and Segundus's most reliable friend. Never a practical magician — he never was — but steady, brave, and thoroughly good-natured in a way that is considerably rarer than magical ability and, under present circumstances, considerably more useful. He visits Starecross regularly from his London residence, providing Segundus with the practical assistance and social connection that allow the school to function. He is the kind of man who volunteers for difficult tasks because no one else has.
¹ Honeyfoot's principal qualification for involvement in magical crisis is that he is entirely without self-importance. This eliminates most of the obstacles that afflict more accomplished characters and has allowed him to accomplish more than his lack of accomplishments might suggest.
title: Mr. Honeyfoot
description: A retired gentleman and magical scholar — not a practical magician, just a kind older man who collects folklore. Segundus's closest friend and most reliable supporter. He has no magical ability, no strategic importance, and absolutely no personal vanity. In the current crisis this makes him one of the more useful people available.

keys: John Uskglass , the Raven King , the Raven King, , the Raven King. ,
value:
John Uskglass. [See also: English Practical Magic, The King's Roads, The King's Letters, Vinculus, Childermass, Lost-Hope.]
Appears variously in accounts: a boy, a young man, a figure in black armour; all accounts agree on dark eyes; dark complexion; black hair; always clothed in black marked with the signs of three kingdoms — Faerie, Heaven, and Hell, which he conquered at fourteen.
The Raven King. King of Northern England 1110–1434, master of all three roads of magic, forger of the alliances with England's natural forces that underpin all English practical magic. Taken as a foundling by the Fae, raised as their favored foster child — not a prince — he returned at fourteen to conquer the north, rule for three centuries, and withdraw. He did not die. He withdrew. He is the spell Strange and Norrell are performing. His Book of Magic is currently written on Vinculus's body. What he intends is unknown. That he intends something has been established beyond reasonable dispute.
¹ Several works purport to explain the Raven King's motivations. None have been verified against a primary source. Interviewing the primary source requires either very great magic or very great foolishness; several authors combined both.
title: John Uskglass
description: The legendary Raven King — the ancient sorcerer who ruled northern England for three hundred years by forging alliances with the land's magical forces. He disappeared in 1434, but did not die. Every strange event in English magic traces back to him. The magicians Strange and Norrell are, without knowing it, pieces of a centuries-long spell he set in motion.

keys: Christopher Drawlight , Mr. Drawlight , Mr. Drawlight, , Mr. Drawlight. ,
value:
Christopher Drawlight. [See also: Gilbert Norrell, Henry Lascelles, Hurtfew Abbey.]
Slim, fashionable build maintained with significant effort; light blue eyes, quick and calculating; pale English complexion; fair hair dressed with elaborate care; the clothing of a man who lives entirely by his social position and cannot afford to let the impression slip.
One half of the pair managing Norrell's public relations. Where Lascelles is genuinely dangerous, Drawlight is vain, shallow, and cowardly — sustained by proximity to power rather than any power of his own. He arranges introductions, manages calendars, and ensures that inconvenient practitioners receive sufficient discouragements. He is susceptible to flattery in ways Lascelles is not, which makes him easier to approach. It also makes him the less reliable of the two to confide in, as information he finds interesting rarely remains private.
¹ The compiler declines to specify which of Drawlight's current associates will eventually provide the unpleasantness his trajectory suggests. Those who have read this far may form their own conclusions.
title: Christopher Drawlight
description: A social parasite who attached himself to Norrell's rising fame and now manages his social life. Vain, cowardly, and useful only as long as Norrell remains important. Easier to manipulate than his colleague Lascelles, which makes him useful to approach — and dangerous to trust.

keys: Henry Lascelles , Mr. Lascelles , Mr. Lascelles, , Mr. Lascelles. ,
value:
Henry Lascelles. [See also: Gilbert Norrell, Christopher Drawlight, Hurtfew Abbey, Lady Pole.]
Tall, cold-elegant build; pale grey eyes with a quality of assessing everything for its uses; fair English complexion; fair hair dressed without vanity; the clothing of a man with excellent taste and no particular need to display it.
Norrell's second handler — the dangerous one. Cold, intelligent, genuinely violent in a way most people fail to register until it is too late. He has attached himself to Norrell's star with the patience of a man who understands power, and his control over Norrell's social affairs is approaching something more properly called governance. He is a potentially valuable source of information about the magical world's politics, and a dangerous one to disappoint.
¹ Lascelles is not Norrell's ally in any sense Norrell has been permitted to examine. The compiler recommends examining this situation from a safe distance before initiating contact, and thereafter maintaining the safe distance.
title: Henry Lascelles
description: Norrell's social manager — elegant, intelligent, and genuinely dangerous. He controls access to Norrell with the patience of a man positioning himself for something beyond simple employment. Not to be confused with a servant, a friend, or anyone who can be safely disappointed.

keys: Flora Greysteel , Miss Greysteel , Miss Greysteel, , Miss Greysteel. ,
value:
Flora Greysteel. [See also: Jonathan Strange, Venice, Dr. Greysteel.]
Medium build, sensibly energetic; clear brown eyes; English complexion lightly tanned from Italian sun; brown hair dressed simply for travel; the neat but practical clothing of a gentlewoman abroad who has abandoned hope of keeping it entirely pristine.
A young Englishwoman currently in Venice with her father Dr. Greysteel. She has recently met Jonathan Strange and is becoming — with some alarm — his primary link to sanity as his magical experiments intensify. She is perceptive, sensible, and courageous in the specific way requiring one to remain in proximity to a brilliant man in the late stages of a private collapse when withdrawal would be entirely justified.
