¹ Miss Greysteel's response to Strange's deteriorating condition has, at each stage, been more useful than that of any trained magician observing the same events. The compiler attributes this to the consistent advantage of caring about the outcome over caring about one's professional reputation.
title: Flora Greysteel
description: A practical young Englishwoman in Venice, who has found herself in proximity to England's most dangerous magician during his most volatile period. She's not a magician and has no supernatural abilities — she just has good judgment and refuses to abandon someone who needs help, even when the sensible course would be to leave.

keys: Dr. Greysteel , Dr. Greysteel, , Dr. Greysteel. ,
value:
Dr. Greysteel. [See also: Flora Greysteel, Venice, Jonathan Strange.]
Stout, reliable build; blue eyes professionally attentive; ruddy Scottish complexion; grey hair; the comfortable travelling clothes of a Scottish physician abroad with his daughter, not expecting extraordinary events and managing the actual events with professional composure.
A physician from Aberdeen, currently in Venice with his daughter Flora. He has found himself, through no particular effort of his own, in proximity to the most active practical magician in Europe during said magician's most volatile period. He is good-natured, medically competent, and managing the gap between his diagnostic training and the current phenomena with the stoicism of a Scottish doctor who has decided that steady application of what one knows is the correct response to what one does not.
¹ Dr. Greysteel has produced two written assessments of Mr. Strange's health. The first described the symptoms of acute grief. The second described the symptoms of something else. He has not submitted the second one to anyone.
title: Dr. Greysteel
description: Flora's father — a Scottish physician who came to Venice for a quiet winter and found himself the closest thing to a medical attendant that England's most reckless magician has. Sensible, kind, and well out of his depth in ways he is too professionally composed to admit.

keys: Sir Walter Pole , Sir Walter , Sir Walter, , Sir Walter. ,
value:
Sir Walter Pole. [See also: Lady Pole, Stephen Black, Gilbert Norrell, Harley Street.]
Tall, well-made build accustomed to authority; grey eyes, politically shrewd; English complexion; dark hair going grey at the temples; the correct clothing of a senior politician who has never needed to think about his wardrobe.
A senior Member of Parliament and Lady Pole's husband. He arranged Norrell's bargain to resurrect his wife without comprehending the price, and has spent the subsequent nine years aware that something is terribly wrong with Emma while being unable to identify what. He is not unintelligent — he simply has no framework for the correct answer. He is the player's primary entry into government circles and has more access to political machinery than any other character in England.
¹ Sir Walter is among the more genuinely sympathetic figures in this account — a man who tried to do something kind and has been living with its consequences without understanding them, which is in some respects worse than understanding them.
title: Sir Walter Pole
description: A senior British politician who made a deal with a magician to bring his dying fiancée back to life — without understanding what the deal actually cost. He's been watching his wife deteriorate for nine years without knowing why. He has government connections and genuine authority, and he's desperate for someone to explain what is happening.

keys: George III , His Majesty , His Majesty, , His Majesty. ,
value:
George III. [See also: English Practical Magic, Windsor Castle.]
Large, once-powerful build now reduced by age and illness; blue eyes vacant and sometimes troubled; florid English complexion; white hair dishevelled; the plain clothing of a man confined and no longer dressed by anyone who imagines he notices.
The King of England, confined at Windsor, his son the Prince Regent governing in his name. Strange attempted a cure during the Peninsular War and discovered the madness may have supernatural dimensions that standard medicine — and standard magic — cannot address. He is a background presence, but his condition and its possible causes may be more consequential than its domestic management suggests. Several theoretical magicians have proposed explanations. None of them agree with each other, and none of them has attempted to verify their theories with the King personally.
¹ The compiler notes that the Prince Regent has twice declined to discuss the matter of his father's condition with representatives of the Norrellite school. The reasons given were diplomatic. The reasons suspected are more interesting.
title: George III
description: The King of England, confined at Windsor Castle with a decades-long illness that might be more than illness. His son rules in his name. A magician once tried to cure him and found something troubling. The King himself cannot currently give useful answers.

keys: Jacob Grimm , Herr Jacob Grimm , Herr Jacob Grimm, , Herr Jacob Grimm. ,
value:
Jacob Grimm. [See also: Wilhelm Grimm, Deutsche Sagen, Norse-Germanic Rune Craft, The Æsir Court, Prussia.]
Lean, angular build; dark eyes scholarly and penetrating; pale Germanic complexion; dark hair; the plain ink-stained clothing of a librarian who has spent recent weeks in a state of increasing professional agitation.
German folklorist and philologist, based in Kassel, currently summoned to Berlin by the Prussian War Ministry regarding an activated fairy tale. With his brother Wilhelm, author of Kinder- und Hausmärchen (1812) and Deutsche Sagen (1816). Their collections are now recognised for what they always were: incident reports. Jacob is the more systematic of the pair — the grammarian who will attempt to build a theoretical framework. He is brilliant, alarmed, and on the verge of understanding that his life's scholarly work is an operational field manual.
¹ At least three of the Grimm brothers' informants were not recalling old stories but reporting recent events. The brothers are aware of this and have not yet decided how to classify it, which is itself a scholarly position of some consequence.
title: Jacob Grimm
description: The elder of the two brothers who collected German fairy tales — now realising his fairy tale collection may be a military intelligence report. A serious scholar being pulled into practical crisis. His systematic mind is trying to build a theoretical framework for events that are outpacing theory considerably.

keys: Wilhelm Grimm , Herr Wilhelm Grimm , Herr Wilhelm Grimm, , Herr Wilhelm Grimm. ,
value:
Wilhelm Grimm. [See also: Jacob Grimm, Deutsche Sagen, Norse-Germanic Rune Craft, The Æsir Court, Prussia.]
Slight build, less robust than his brother; warm brown eyes; pale complexion with the pallor of chronic ill health; brown hair; plain scholarly clothing more carefully pressed than Jacob's — he is the one who considers how things appear.
The younger Grimm brother, folklorist and literary scholar. Where Jacob is the grammarian, Wilhelm is the storyteller — the one who shapes collected tales for publication and attends to their emotional resonance. His chronic ill health has not diminished his output. His understanding of fairy tale as emotional truth may prove more useful than Jacob's systematic classification in circumstances where the emotional truth has turned out to be literal.
¹ Wilhelm Grimm's editorial decisions about which details to include and which to omit from the family collections are, under current conditions, more consequential than he intended. Several omitted details are now operationally relevant again, and at least one has been encountered in the field.
title: Wilhelm Grimm
description: The younger of the two fairy tale collectors — a literary scholar who shaped the stories for publication and understood them as emotional truth. His chronic ill health has not slowed him, and his instinct for narrative meaning may be more useful than systematic classification when the stories start happening.

keys: Mademoiselle Lenormand , Mademoiselle Lenormand, , Mademoiselle Lenormand. ,
value:
Mademoiselle Lenormand. [See also: Paris, The Mélusine Court, Divination.]
Small, dramatic build; dark eyes of unsettling precision; olive French complexion; black hair elaborately dressed; the theatrical clothing of a woman whose performance has become indistinguishable from reality.
The most celebrated fortune-teller in Europe. Her salon on the rue de Tournon has advised everyone from Napoleon's court onward; Josephine was a regular client. Her card-reading technique — the Lenormand method — is standard across the continent. Her prescience was always genuine in ways she found difficult to explain; in the present climate she no longer needs to explain it to anyone.
¹ Mademoiselle Lenormand has been imprisoned twice by different French governments for predicting events they found inconvenient. She was released both times. This is less a mark of official clemency than a practical acknowledgment that imprisoning her had not prevented the events in question, and that the subsequent events were worse.
title: Mademoiselle Lenormand
description: The most famous fortune-teller in Europe, based in Paris — she advised Napoleon's court and every significant figure of the Napoleonic era. Her card readings were always genuinely accurate. Now that magic has returned, her abilities are no longer an oddity requiring explanation. She has been imprisoned twice for predicting the wrong things. She was right both times.

keys: Lord Wellington , the Duke of Wellington , the Duke of Wellington, , the Duke of Wellington. ,
value:
Lord Wellington. [See also: English Practical Magic, The Strangite School, Jonathan Strange, The Pillar of Darkness.]
Tall, spare military build; sharp blue eyes famous for assessing everything they encounter; English complexion weathered from the Peninsula; dark hair going grey; the immaculate dress of a man who has never needed a uniform to convey authority and has long since stopped wearing one.
Victor of Waterloo, transitioning from military to political life in the House of Lords. He witnessed Strange's battlefield magic and asked the famous question about whether magic could kill; Strange answered that it had not occurred to him. Wellington has since thought about it considerably. He is the most pragmatic mind in England on the subject of magical military application, and in February 1817 is the government's most useful contact for a practitioner who can demonstrate their abilities are worth his time.
¹ Wellington's opinion of magic is operational: what does it do, does it do it reliably, can it be made to do it on command. He has not yet answered the original question to his own satisfaction. He is the kind of man who does not stop thinking about questions.
title: Lord Wellington
description: The Duke of Wellington — victor of Waterloo, now in politics, the most famous military mind in England. He's seen practical magic used in battle and has been quietly thinking about its implications ever since. Pragmatic and powerful, he's the government's most useful gateway for a magician who can prove their usefulness.

keys: Lord Byron , Lord Byron, , Lord Byron. ,
value:
Lord Byron. [See also: Venice, Jonathan Strange, John Murray II.]
Athletic build somewhat softened by Venetian winter; famously handsome face; dark eyes with the quality of performing even in private; pale English complexion with a Mediterranean tan; dark curling hair worn romantically dishevelled; fashionably undone clothing suggesting perpetual performance.
The most famous poet in Europe, currently in Venice for the Carnival season. Living with Marianna Segati, studying Armenian at San Lazzaro, completing Manfred — a dramatic poem about a sorcerer seeking forbidden knowledge and communing with spirits. He is writing this in a city where Strange is performing experiments on the nature of madness and fairy sight three streets away. In a world where words have power and his words have the widest circulation of any living poet, the question of whether his writing constitutes accidental magic has become pressing.
¹ Byron's publisher John Murray II has noted that sustained reading of certain recent Byron passages produces effects that cannot be attributed to the weather. Murray is drafting a letter to Byron on the subject and has restarted it four times.
title: Lord Byron
description: The most famous poet in Europe — currently in Venice writing about a sorcerer who seeks forbidden knowledge, three streets away from an actual magician doing exactly that. His poetry might be more than poetry in a world where words carry magical weight. Brilliant, reckless, and constitutionally incapable of not finding everything interesting.

keys: Marianna Segati , Signora Segati , Signora Segati, , Signora Segati. ,
value:
Marianna Segati. [See also: Lord Byron, Venice.]
Dark-eyed, dark-haired Venetian; olive complexion animated and expressive; fashionably dressed in the Italian style — she is the wife of a draper and dresses accordingly, which in Venice means considerably.
Byron's hostess and current companion, wife of the linen-draper Pietro Segati. She is lively, clever, and considerably more aware of her own situation than Byron's English admirers have given her credit for. In February 1817 she is the household through which Strange and Byron are most likely to be introduced, and she has noticed a quality of light around Strange that she has not mentioned to anyone, because she has not yet found a satisfactory explanation.
¹ Marianna Segati is not a magical practitioner, but she has lived in Venice all her life and possesses the Venetian's long-cultivated instinct for noticing when something is not quite right about a person's shadow. She has been noticing this for several weeks. She has mentioned it to no one, which the compiler considers an exceptionally sensible decision.
title: Marianna Segati
description: Byron's companion in Venice — a lively, intelligent Venetian woman who is more perceptive than the situation gives her credit for. She's noticed something odd about Strange. She hasn't said anything yet.

keys: Mary Shelley , Mrs. Shelley , Mrs. Shelley, , Mrs. Shelley. ,
value:
Mary Shelley. [See also: Percy Shelley, Frankenstein, London, Marlow.]
Small, slight build; striking grey eyes with a quality of observation; fair complexion with the pallor of grief and indoor work; light brown hair dressed simply; plain dark clothing — she has been in mourning and is not yet out of it.
Nineteen years old, completing Frankenstein at Marlow (published January 1818 — currently in manuscript). A novel about the reanimation of assembled dead matter, written without knowledge that Norrell actually raised a dead woman a decade earlier. She is brilliant, grieving her half-sister Fanny's suicide in October 1816, and working at the precise intersection of science, literature, and the supernatural that defines the game's central questions.
¹ The compiler has not informed Miss Shelley that the central conceit of her novel has a real precedent, on the grounds that she would find the information either vindicating or devastating, and neither outcome seems currently manageable. Her work is, regardless, the most prescient fiction being written in England. This is not entirely coincidence.
title: Mary Shelley
description: A brilliant nineteen-year-old author completing what will become Frankenstein — a novel about reanimating the dead. She doesn't know that this has actually been done. Currently grieving her half-sister's suicide. Her book asks the same questions the game asks, and she arrived at them through pure imagination before magic's return made them literal.

keys: Percy Shelley , Mr. Shelley , Mr. Shelley, , Mr. Shelley. ,
value:
Percy Shelley. [See also: Mary Shelley, London, Marlow.]
Tall, slight, ethereal build; large light eyes described as feverish or visionary depending on the observer; fair English complexion; curling fair hair; careless, dramatic clothing of a man who considers his ideas more important than his appearance and is correct but has not considered that appearances still create impressions.
Poet and political radical, Mary Shelley's husband, currently at Marlow completing A Proposal for Putting Reform to the Vote. His poetry — notable for its imagery of invisible forces, of winds and voices beyond the visible — may be producing effects he does not recognise as magical. His friendship with Byron and proximity to the most innovative prose of the age place him at the centre of the literary circle most likely to produce accidental magic.
¹ Percy Shelley has drowned twice in boats and has not drowned in fact. The compiler makes no predictions but notes that he swims in the Thames at Marlow regardless of season and has recently begun to suspect the river answers him.
title: Percy Shelley
description: A radical poet married to Mary Shelley, whose writing about invisible forces and transcendent winds might be doing more than he intends in a world where words carry power. Brilliant and catastrophically impractical. The river near his house has started behaving oddly.

keys: Fanny Imlay , Miss Imlay , Miss Imlay, , Miss Imlay. ,
value:
Fanny Imlay. [See also: Mary Shelley.]
Slight build; dark eyes; dark hair; plain dress. No further contemporary description exists with confidence.
Mary Shelley's half-sister, deceased — she took her own life on 9 October 1816 at the Mackworth Arms inn in Swansea. She was twenty-two. She is recorded here because in a world where the boundary between the living and the dead is currently more permeable than at any time in living memory, the recently and tragically dead are not always as absent as they should be; and because her effect on Mary Shelley's state of mind — and therefore on the most prescient novel currently being written — cannot be overstated.
¹ The compiler records this entry with the minimal detail the subject warrants and the maximum care the circumstances require. The Scholomance Court maintains no opinion on suicides, a fact the compiler finds, under present conditions, unexpectedly reassuring.
title: Fanny Imlay
description: Mary Shelley's half-sister, who died by her own hand in October 1816. Recorded here because the boundary between the living and dead is unusually thin at present, and because her loss shapes everything Mary Shelley is currently writing.

keys: Elias Lönnrot , young Lönnrot , young Lönnrot, , young Lönnrot. ,
value:
Elias Lönnrot. [See also: The Väki Court, Finno-Ugric Origin-Song Magic, The Kalevala, Old Matti Karjalainen.]
Young, slight build; clear blue Finnish eyes; fair Nordic complexion; fair hair; the plain serviceable clothing of a village schoolmaster's son on very limited means, which is all he currently is.
A Finnish boy of fourteen or fifteen in February 1817 — too young for an active role in any dangerous enterprise, but already showing an unusual attention to the runo-singers around him. The oral tradition he will later compile into the Kalevala is extant and active; the old men and women of Karelia who carry the origin-songs are the living repositories of Väki Court magic. One of them — Old Matti Karjalainen — has been watching this boy with the interest one reserves for either a future student or a future problem.
¹ The compiler recommends seeking not Lönnrot himself but his informants — the runo-singers of eastern Karelia, whose knowledge is operational in ways the young cataloguer has not yet understood, and who are currently easier to locate than anyone suspects.
title: Elias Lönnrot
description: A Finnish teenager who will grow up to compile the Kalevala — the great collection of Finnish origin-songs and folk magic. Currently too young for a direct role, but the old rune-singers he will eventually study are active, powerful, and accessible. One of them has already noticed him.

keys: Tsar Alexander I , the Tsar , the Tsar, , the Tsar. ,
value:
Tsar Alexander I. [See also: Russia, The Parliament of Domains, Prince Golitsyn, Jung-Stilling.]
Tall, imposing build beginning to soften; blue eyes with a distant, slightly haunted quality; pale complexion, handsome with age; thinning fair hair; military dress uniform or the subdued formal clothing of a man whose mystical turn has begun to affect his wardrobe choices.
Emperor of Russia, at the Winter Palace in St. Petersburg. After 1812 his interest in religious mysticism deepened considerably — guided by Pietist figures (Jung-Stilling dies March 1817) and millenarian mystics like Baroness von Krüdener. His instrument for religious-mystical policy is Prince Golitsyn, Minister of Religious Affairs. In a world where the Parliament of Domains stirs in every forest, river, and household of his vast empire, his mystical inclinations are not a personality quirk but a political posture under active reassessment.
¹ The Parliament of Domains has formed no collective opinion of the Tsar, because the Parliament forms no collective opinions. Dedushka Petr considers him well-intentioned and confused in ways that are currently manageable. Dedushka Petr considers this a compliment.
title: Tsar Alexander I
description: The Emperor of Russia — a man who survived Napoleon and emerged deeply mystical, surrounding himself with spiritual advisors and believing himself called to a religious purpose. In a country where ancient nature spirits are stirring in every river and forest, his mystical instincts are being overtaken by events considerably stranger than his theology prepared him for.

keys: Johann Heinrich Jung-Stilling , Herr Jung-Stilling , Herr Jung-Stilling, , Herr Jung-Stilling. ,
value:
Johann Heinrich Jung-Stilling. [See also: Tsar Alexander I, Baroness von Krüdener, Russia.]
Small, slight build grown frail with age; mild, otherworldly blue eyes; pale complexion; white hair; the simple Pietist clothing, dark and without ornament, of a man who resolved the question of appearances decades ago.
German Pietist mystic, physician, and one of the Tsar's spiritual advisors. His emphasis on pneumatology — the study of spirits — and his belief in an imminent spiritual transformation of the world have aged unexpectedly well since 1815. He is dying in February 1817 and will die in March. He is a dying man who is correct about the wrong details — his theological framework does not describe what is actually occurring, but it is more useful than the frameworks of men who have spent no time thinking about spirits.
¹ Jung-Stilling's correspondence with the Tsar is currently the Russian court's best working theoretical account of what is happening. This reflects as much on the competition as on the quality of the account.
title: Johann Heinrich Jung-Stilling
description: A German mystic and physician who spent his life studying the science of spirits and advising the Tsar on spiritual matters. He's dying in February 1817 and will be gone by March. He was right that something extraordinary was approaching. He was wrong about the details. Time has nearly run out to ask him what he knows.

keys: Baroness von Krüdener , the Baroness , the Baroness, , the Baroness. ,
value:
Baroness von Krüdener. [See also: Tsar Alexander I, Jung-Stilling, Russia.]
Formerly striking, now dramatically aged by asceticism; pale intense eyes; fair complexion, weathered; grey-white hair; the plain dress of a woman who has abandoned worldly display and is making a considerable effect with the abandonment.
A millenarian mystic whose greatest influence on Tsar Alexander I was in 1815 — that influence is waning as she has retired to private religious life. Recorded here because her fingerprints remain on the Tsar's mystical worldview, which shapes Russian policy on the supernatural. She believes an imminent divine transformation of the world is approaching. She is not wrong about the transformation.
¹ The Baroness's prophecies have been more accurate in general atmosphere than specific detail. Her statement that the world was about to change in ways no one expected has been vindicated. Her statement that this change would produce a universal Christian monarchy has been contested by every Fae court in Europe, several of whom were not informed of the prediction and would have found it presumptuous.
title: Baroness von Krüdener
description: A German-Russian mystic who predicted divine transformation of the world and shaped the Russian Tsar's spiritual worldview. Her influence has peaked and is waning. She was broadly right about something extraordinary approaching. She was wrong about what it was.

keys: Prince Alexander Golitsyn , Prince Golitsyn , Prince Golitsyn, , Prince Golitsyn. ,
value:
Prince Alexander Golitsyn. [See also: Tsar Alexander I, Russia, Russian Orthodox Church.]
Medium build, carefully maintained; grey eyes administratively precise; fair Russian complexion; dark hair; the immaculate formal dress of a senior official of the Russian Imperial court who has never once been late to anything.
Russian Minister of Religious Affairs and the Tsar's instrument for mystical-religious policy. He manages the practical machinery of the Tsar's spiritual interests — correspondence with Pietist networks, oversight of religious publishing, and increasingly, coordination of the government's response to supernatural phenomena manifesting across the empire. He is not himself a mystic but an exceptionally competent administrator working in service of a mystical agenda, a combination the Parliament of Domains finds both useful and concerning.
¹ Prince Golitsyn is the person through whom any practitioner seeking official Russian recognition must pass. He is polite, thorough, and has recently added a new filing category to his administrative system. He has not told anyone what it is called.
title: Prince Alexander Golitsyn
description: Russia's Minister of Religious Affairs — the Tsar's administrator for all things mystical. Not a mystic himself, just a very organised man managing an increasingly unmanageable brief. The gateway to any official engagement with the Russian government on magical matters.

keys: Klemens von Metternich , Prince Metternich , Prince Metternich, , Prince Metternich. ,
value:
Klemens von Metternich. [See also: The Austrian Empire, Vienna, Herr Franz Gruber.]
Tall, elegant build maintained with aristocratic discipline; pale assessing blue eyes; fair complexion; fair hair swept back; the immaculate formal dress of a man who has made political power itself into an aesthetic.
Austrian Chancellor, architect of the post-Napoleonic conservative order. His system — suppress, document, report upward, hope someone else resolves it — is being applied to magical practitioners with the same bureaucratic thoroughness applied to political dissidents. He views magic as another destabilising force to be managed. He is the most powerful mundane obstacle to magical practice on the continent. He is also, under current conditions, the only major political figure whose response to magic is both comprehensive and entirely wrong.
¹ Metternich's apparatus has generated approximately four hundred documents classifying supernatural phenomena since 1815. The supernatural phenomena have not been informed of their classifications and have not modified their conduct accordingly. Metternich is aware of this. He is not certain what conclusion to draw from it.
title: Klemens von Metternich
description: The Austrian Chancellor — the most powerful political figure in continental Europe, architect of the post-Napoleon conservative order. He treats magical phenomena as a bureaucratic problem: suppress, classify, report upward. His filing systems are excellent. His results are not.

keys: Humphry Davy , Sir Humphry , Sir Humphry, , Sir Humphry. ,
value:
Humphry Davy. [See also: Michael Faraday, The Royal Institution, London, English Practical Magic.]
Handsome, slightly dandyish build; bright curious eyes; English complexion; dark hair fashionably dressed; the stylish clothing of a man who is both celebrated scientist and social ambition made flesh.
England's most celebrated scientist — inventor of the safety lamp (1815–16), chemical pioneer, friend of Coleridge and the Romantics. He is fascinated by the intersection of science, poetry, and invisible natural forces. His assistant Michael Faraday is being initiated into nature's mysteries at the Royal Institution. In a world where magic has returned, Davy's empirical approach to invisible forces is more immediately useful than any gentleman magician's library, and his response to phenomena he cannot classify by existing theory is, unusually, to look harder.
¹ Davy's opinion of Mr. Norrell is not on record. Norrell's opinion of Davy — a man who achieves results Norrell cannot explain through a method Norrell cannot approve — may be inferred from the fact that Norrell has not attempted a meeting, which is itself a form of documentation.
title: Humphry Davy
description: England's most celebrated scientist — a chemist and inventor who is genuinely open to magical phenomena in a way his colleagues are not. His assistant Faraday and his friendship with the Romantic poets put him at the intersection of empirical science and supernatural experience. His laboratory may be producing results that neither tradition can fully claim.

keys: Michael Faraday , Mr. Faraday , Mr. Faraday, , Mr. Faraday. ,
value:
Michael Faraday. [See also: Humphry Davy, The Royal Institution, London.]
Medium build, slightly stooped from bench work; dark, intensely observant eyes; pale complexion from indoor work; dark hair; the plain ink-stained clothing of a junior laboratory assistant who has no social standing to protect and every scientific question to pursue.
Twenty-five years old, Davy's assistant at the Royal Institution. A self-educated bookbinder's apprentice who has earned his position entirely through ability. He is the most careful observer of physical phenomena currently employed in England — which is to say, the person most likely to notice, accurately describe, and correctly record a magical event without either dismissing it or being deceived by it. His religious convictions (Sandemanian) have not prepared him for the current situation but have given him an unusual composure in its face.
¹ Faraday has not spoken to Davy about certain observations he has made in the laboratory after hours. He is waiting until he has enough to constitute a pattern. The pattern is forming faster than he expected.
title: Michael Faraday
description: Davy's young laboratory assistant — a self-taught genius from a poor family who has earned his position through sheer ability. The most careful empirical observer in England. He's been noting down things happening in the laboratory after hours that he hasn't told anyone about yet.

keys: William Blake , Mr. Blake , Mr. Blake, , Mr. Blake. ,
value:
William Blake. [See also: London, English Practical Magic, Jerusalem.]
Small, powerfully built; blazing blue eyes that see things others do not, because they are there; pale English complexion; white hair; the plain, old-fashioned clothing of a man entirely indifferent to his social standing.
Fifty-nine years old, living near Marble Arch, completing his prophetic illuminated book Jerusalem. He has claimed for decades to see angels in trees and converse with spirits of the dead. He was never mad. He was accurate before it was fashionable. His prophetic and artistic work may be the most precise documentation of England's supernatural ecology available — produced by a man who was not a magician but was simply paying attention longer than anyone else.
¹ Blake has been visited by Jonathan Strange, who attempted to explain what is currently happening. Blake listened, then showed Strange his recent work. Strange has not recovered from the encounter. The present compiler has not spoken to either of them about it, and suspects this prudent.
title: William Blake
description: A visionary artist living in obscurity who has been claiming to see angels and spirits for decades. Everyone thought he was mad. He was just early. His work may be the most accurate documentation of England's supernatural landscape available — and he produced it before magic returned, which raises questions no one has adequately answered.

keys: Samuel Taylor Coleridge , Mr. Coleridge , Mr. Coleridge, , Mr. Coleridge. ,
value:
Samuel Taylor Coleridge. [See also: London, Highgate, Dr. James Gillman, Humphry Davy.]
Large, increasingly formless build; luminous grey-blue eyes, brilliant and unfocused; pale, unhealthy complexion; dark hair streaked grey; the comfortable but somewhat neglected clothing of a man whose household is managed by his physician.
Fifty-four years old, residing at Highgate with Dr. Gillman in a sustained effort to manage his opium dependency. In 1817 he publishes Biographia Literaria and coins the willing suspension of disbelief — a phrase that has acquired, under present conditions, an unintended literal dimension. The man who wrote Kubla Khan in an opium vision he could not fully recover has been arguing that imagination is the living Power and prime Agent of all human Perception. In a world where imagination has literal magical consequences, this is not metaphor.
¹ Coleridge's theoretical framework for the imagination is currently the most useful published philosophical account of how magic works available in England. Coleridge has not been informed of this. The compiler suspects he would find it vindicating in ways that would not improve his health.
title: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
description: The poet who wrote The Rime of the Ancient Mariner, currently living in Highgate managing an opium addiction. He's just coined 'the willing suspension of disbelief' — a phrase that means something entirely different when disbelief is no longer an option. His theory of the imagination turns out to be a working description of magic.

keys: Dr. James Gillman , Dr. Gillman , Dr. Gillman, , Dr. Gillman. ,
value:
Dr. James Gillman. [See also: Samuel Taylor Coleridge, London, Highgate.]
Medium, professional build; kind watchful brown eyes; English complexion; brown hair; the neat respectable clothing of a prosperous physician who has taken on a patient considerably more complicated than he anticipated when he first opened the door.
A surgeon and physician at Highgate who has hosted Coleridge since 1816 to manage his opium dependency, with more success than most expected, and who has formed a genuine friendship with his patient. A competent, humane man who has found himself in proximity to extraordinary intellectual and increasingly supernatural phenomena, and who manages this by maintaining routine, providing meals, and not asking questions he suspects he does not want answered.
¹ Dr. Gillman's medical notes on Coleridge include several passages transcribed from his somniloquies — sleep-speech — that do not correspond to any published work. The compiler has been attempting to determine what they describe and has made preliminary progress that he is not yet prepared to record here.
title: Dr. James Gillman
description: Coleridge's physician and host — a Highgate doctor who took in England's most brilliant opium-addicted poet and has been managing the situation with great patience. He keeps careful notes. Some of what he's written down from Coleridge's sleep-speech does not correspond to anything published.

keys: John Murray II , Mr. Murray , Mr. Murray, , Mr. Murray. ,
value:
John Murray II. [See also: Jonathan Strange, Lord Byron, London, The History and Practice of English Magic.]
Stout, prosperous build; sharp commercial eyes; English complexion; dark hair going grey; the dress of a successful London publisher who receives everyone in his first-floor drawing room and knows precisely what that room is worth.
Publisher of Byron, Scott, Austen, and Strange's History and Practice of English Magic — making him simultaneously the central figure of literary London and, inadvertently, one of the most consequential figures in the revival of English magic. His offices at 50 Albemarle Street are the nexus of literary London. In a world where published texts produce effects in readers who attempt them, Murray — who decides what reaches print — is one of the most powerful people in England without having chosen to be.
¹ Murray is currently corresponding with Byron in Venice about Manfred and receiving letters from Strange, which he has not shown to Byron. He suspects this is a professional decision. He has restarted his letter to Byron on the subject four times and is not closer to finishing it.
title: John Murray II
description: London's most important publisher — Byron, Scott, Jane Austen, and the magician Strange are all his authors. His drawing room at 50 Albemarle Street is where literary London makes its decisions. In a world where publications can produce magical effects, the man who decides what gets printed holds more power than he intended.

keys: Walter Scott , Mr. Scott , Mr. Scott, , Mr. Scott. ,
value:
Walter Scott. [See also: Edinburgh, Scotland, Border Supernatural Phenomena, James Hogg.]
Large, lame build — his childhood lameness is permanent; grey, penetrating eyes; Scottish complexion; dark hair going grey; the comfortable slightly formal clothing of a man who is simultaneously Clerk of Session, Sheriff-Depute, and Scotland's most celebrated living writer.
Clerk of Session at Edinburgh's Parliament House, Sheriff of Selkirkshire, and Scotland's greatest living writer. His decades collecting border legends, fairy stories, and supernatural ballads have made him the most comprehensive repository of Scottish folk-magical knowledge in the country. The return of magic has transformed his scholarship from antiquarian curiosity into operational intelligence. He is between major novels in February 1817.
¹ Scott is the most practically useful contact in Edinburgh — his knowledge spans the legal, the literary, and the folkloric, and he is canny enough to know when each applies. His skepticism, if broken, carries the most intellectual weight of anyone in Scotland. It has not yet been broken. The compiler is uncertain what it would take.
title: Walter Scott
description: Scotland's greatest writer and an obsessive collector of border folklore and fairy stories — who is now discovering that his collection may be more operationally useful than literary. A judge, a writer, and a walking encyclopaedia of Scottish supernatural knowledge, currently between novels and very much paying attention to what is happening.

keys: James Hogg , Mr. Hogg , Mr. Hogg, , Mr. Hogg. ,
value:
James Hogg. [See also: Walter Scott, Edinburgh, Scotland, Border Supernatural Phenomena.]
Stocky, shepherd's build; clear blue eyes deceptively direct; weathered Scottish complexion; thick dark hair; the somewhat improved clothing of a shepherd-poet who has recently acquired Edinburgh literary connections and is not entirely sure how to dress for them.
The Ettrick Shepherd — self-taught, steeped in border supernatural folklore from childhood in the Ettrick Forest, recently associated with the new Blackwood's Edinburgh Magazine. He grew up hearing fairy stories from his mother, who told the same tales to Walter Scott. Where Scott collects folklore with scholarly detachment, Hogg believes it — and in a world where the beliefs are vindicated, instinctive folk knowledge may be more practically reliable than any gentleman's education.
¹ Hogg's mother's fairy stories are the most reliable field reports on Scottish border supernatural phenomena currently available. Hogg is unaware they constitute field reports. The distinction has become purely academic, and will shortly become purely irrelevant.
title: James Hogg
description: The Ettrick Shepherd — a self-taught poet from the Scottish Borders who grew up genuinely believing in the fairy stories his mother told him. Where Scott collects folklore with scholarly distance, Hogg believes it in his bones. In the current situation, his instinctive knowledge is turning out to be more reliable than any scholar's catalogue.

keys: Germaine de Staël , Madame de Staël , Madame de Staël, , Madame de Staël. ,
value:
Germaine de Staël. [See also: Paris, Juliette Récamier, Benjamin Constant, François-René de Chateaubriand.]
Large, powerfully animated build; brilliant dark eyes famous for their quality of visible intelligence; French complexion with the colour of a woman always in conversation; dark hair; the lavish dress of a woman with wealth, taste, and the certain knowledge she is worth looking at.
The most powerful intellectual woman in Europe, Napoleon's personal enemy, author of De l'Allemagne and Corinne, currently hosting her salon at 40 rue des Mathurins. On 21 February 1817, she will suffer a stroke and become paralysed; she will die 14 July. In early February she is at the absolute height of her powers — the most formidable conversationalist in Paris. The timing is extraordinary: find her now, while she can still speak.
¹ The compiler records this entry with particular attention to the February dates. The sudden incapacitation of the most formidable intellect in Paris will create a power vacuum in French intellectual life that will not be filled, and several of her salon's discussions in early February 1817 are retroactively significant.
title: Germaine de Staël
description: The most brilliant woman in Europe — writer, salon host, Napoleon's arch-enemy, the intellectual centre of Paris. She will suffer a stroke on 21 February 1817 and die in July. In early February she is at the height of her powers. Time to speak with her is running out.

keys: Juliette Récamier , Madame Récamier , Madame Récamier, , Madame Récamier. ,
value:
Juliette Récamier. [See also: Germaine de Staël, Paris, Benjamin Constant, François-René de Chateaubriand.]
Famously beautiful: slight, perfect figure; blue eyes of exceptional clarity; fair complexion; dark hair; supremely elegant clothing — her beauty is her social instrument and she maintains it with professional attention.
French salonnière, de Staël's closest friend, and the centre of the Royalist social world in Paris. Her own salon at the Abbaye-aux-Bois is Paris's second most important gathering — after de Staël's stroke, it will be the first. She is not a thinker in de Staël's mode but a connector: she knows everyone, she is liked by everyone, and access to Paris's intellectual and political circles runs through her.
¹ Récamier's social network is the fastest and most reliable intelligence system in Paris. It is not magical. It does not need to be. The compiler notes that under present conditions this gives it certain structural advantages over systems that are.
title: Juliette Récamier
description: Paris's most celebrated socialite — renowned for her beauty and her ability to connect people across every faction in French intellectual and political life. Not an intellectual in de Staël's mode, but more influential than any intellectual in a city run by relationships. After de Staël's stroke, she becomes the centre of everything.

keys: Benjamin Constant , Monsieur Constant , Monsieur Constant, , Monsieur Constant. ,
value:
Benjamin Constant. [See also: Germaine de Staël, Paris, Juliette Récamier.]
Medium build, slightly agitated; sharp restless grey eyes; pale complexion; fair hair going grey; the correct dress of a man with political ambitions in the Chamber of Deputies and a personal life of considerable disorder that the dress is meant to counteract.
French political thinker, author of Adolphe (1816), and liberal parliamentarian. A member of de Staël's salon, recently returned to Paris political life after years of exile. His political liberalism — freedom of the press, constitutional limits on power — makes him an ally of every practitioner seeking official recognition and a man whose liberalism stops at anything that cannot be explained through Enlightenment rationalism.
¹ Constant has recently been informed that his novel Adolphe — about a man who cannot commit and destroys what he loves through irresolution — is being read in Vienna as a manual for managing negotiations with the Mélusine Court's contracts. He is uncertain how to feel about this and has not resolved the question.
title: Benjamin Constant
description: A French liberal politician and novelist — a champion of constitutional freedoms who returned from exile to fight for them in the Chamber of Deputies. Useful as a political ally, and limited by his Enlightenment rationalism, which is straining considerably under present conditions.

keys: François-René de Chateaubriand , the Vicomte de Chateaubriand , the Vicomte de Chateaubriand, , the Vicomte de Chateaubriand. ,
value:
François-René de Chateaubriand. [See also: Paris, Germaine de Staël, Juliette Récamier.]
Tall, romantic build; dark brooding eyes; swarthy French complexion; dark hair worn with calculated disarray; the expensive, slightly theatrical clothing of a man who has decided his appearance is part of his literary project.
Writer, diplomat, founder of French Romanticism. His Génie du Christianisme (1802) argued for Christianity on aesthetic grounds — that it was beautiful, its imagery sublime, its effects on culture magnificent. In a world where the Narcissist is a demonstrable Fae being and the theological tradition he defends has supernatural consequences, his aesthetic argument has acquired dimensions he did not intend.
¹ Chateaubriand's defence of Christianity as aesthetically superior to paganism is, the compiler understands, currently the subject of active dispute among the Romuva, Æsir, and Sídhe courts. None of them have read it. Several have been informed of the thesis. The Romuva court's response was not printable. The Sídhe asked what aesthetics means.
title: François-René de Chateaubriand
description: The founder of French Romanticism — a diplomat and writer who argued that Christianity was beautiful and therefore true. In a world where the gods of old Europe turn out to be real Fae courts with historical grievances, his argument has acquired complications he cannot yet see from Paris.

keys: Pierre-Simon Laplace , the Marquis de Laplace , the Marquis de Laplace, , the Marquis de Laplace. ,
value:
Pierre-Simon Laplace. [See also: Paris, French Rationalism, The Académie des Sciences.]
Stout, composed build; cold analytical eyes; pale complexion; white hair; the formal dress of a Marquis who has also been a mathematical genius since twenty and sees no reason to perform either role for anyone.
Made Marquis by Louis XVIII in 1817. The supreme rationalist of European science — the man who told Napoleon he had no need of God as a hypothesis is now confronted with phenomena his mechanics cannot explain. He is the most powerful scientific mind opposing the reality of magic, not from ignorance but from principled commitment to a universe governed by deterministic law. His conversion would change the intellectual climate entirely; his continued resistance gives the skeptics their champion.
¹ Laplace has examined three documented magical events in the past eighteen months. His explanations for each are on record. Two are inconsistent with each other. He has not acknowledged the inconsistency. The compiler believes he has not noticed it. This is the most disturbing possibility of the three available options.
title: Pierre-Simon Laplace
description: The greatest mathematical mind in Europe — the man who told Napoleon he had no need of God as a hypothesis. Now confronted with phenomena his deterministic universe cannot explain. He is the most powerful intellectual force opposing the reality of magic, and his continued resistance is entirely principled, which makes it more dangerous than simple ignorance.

keys: Ludwig van Beethoven , Herr Beethoven , Herr Beethoven, , Herr Beethoven. ,
value:
Ludwig van Beethoven. [See also: Vienna, Musical Magic, The Scholomance Court.]
Stocky, powerful build somewhat dishevelled; eyes with the quality of listening to something no one else hears — which is precisely what he is doing; sallow complexion; grey-black hair dishevelled; worn ink-stained clothing of a man who moves constantly and cares less about domestic comfort than about the next measure.
Forty-six years old, almost completely deaf. No longer performs publicly; relies on ear trumpets; will formalise conversation books this year. In February 1817 he is creatively dormant, ill, consumed by his nephew Karl's custody battle. But in Vienna, where musical compositions are producing supernatural effects, the question is unavoidable: what has he been hearing that no one else can? A deaf genius composing from pure internal sound in a city where sound has literal magical power may not be limited in the way deafness ordinarily implies.
¹ Three Viennese musicians have reported that sustained rehearsal of Beethoven's recent compositions produces effects that cannot be attributed to acoustics. Beethoven was informed. He asked for the complete account, listened without comment, and returned to his notebooks.
title: Ludwig van Beethoven
description: The greatest composer alive, and almost completely deaf. In a city where music is producing supernatural effects, the question of what a deaf genius hears when he composes in silence has become genuinely urgent. His late works are approaching. He doesn't discuss what he hears.

keys: Karl van Beethoven , young Karl , young Karl, , young Karl. ,
value:
Karl van Beethoven. [See also: Ludwig van Beethoven, Vienna.]
Young, slight build — he is sixteen; dark, somewhat anxious eyes; fair complexion; dark hair; the schoolboy's clothing of a boy whose guardianship is being actively contested between his uncle and his mother.
Ludwig van Beethoven's nephew, subject of a bitter and ongoing custody dispute between the composer and Karl's mother Johanna. He is sixteen in February 1817. Growing up in the household of a deaf genius who communicates through written notes and cannot hear Karl when he enters the room, he has developed certain observational skills that prove unexpectedly useful in the present climate.
¹ Karl van Beethoven has observed, without discussing it with anyone, that his uncle sometimes writes music that produces a response in the household cat. He considers this unremarkable. He has grown up in this household, and his threshold for remarkable has been set accordingly.
title: Karl van Beethoven
description: Beethoven's sixteen-year-old nephew, at the centre of a custody battle that is consuming his uncle's emotional life. Raised in an extraordinary household, he has developed excellent observational habits and an unusually high threshold for what he considers worth mentioning.

keys: Franz Schubert , Herr Schubert , Herr Schubert, , Herr Schubert. ,
value:
Franz Schubert. [See also: Vienna, Musical Magic, Franz von Schober.]
Small, round build; short-sighted eyes behind round spectacles, warm and distracted; fair complexion; brown hair; the plain somewhat worn clothing of a penniless twenty-year-old composer living on a friend's generosity.
Twenty years old (born 31 January 1797), living at von Schober's home and composing at a rate that borders on supernatural — fifty songs in 1817 alone, including Die Forelle, An die Musik, and Der Tod und das Mädchen. His songs about death, the forest, water, and wandering map directly onto the game's supernatural ecology. In a world where music has magical power, Schubert's output is either the most prolific accidental magic in Europe, or something he understands better than he lets on.
¹ Schubert's friends have noted that certain Schubertiade evenings produce atmospheric effects — a peculiar quality of presence, a sense of something listening — that do not correspond to the weather or the season. Schubert, when asked, says he writes what he hears. He has not elaborated on what he hears it from.
title: Franz Schubert
description: A twenty-year-old Viennese composer producing music at a rate and quality that strains credibility — fifty songs this year alone. His subject matter (death, forests, water, wandering) maps perfectly onto the supernatural landscape of the game. His intimate evening concerts produce effects that can't be attributed to acoustics. He writes what he hears.

keys: Franz von Schober , Herr von Schober , Herr von Schober, , Herr von Schober. ,
value:
Franz von Schober. [See also: Franz Schubert, Vienna.]
Handsome, sociable build; grey eyes charming and somewhat self-indulgent; fair complexion; fair hair; the well-dressed slightly loose clothing of a young man of good family who considers himself primarily an aesthete.
Schubert's closest friend and current host. A dilettante poet, occasional actor, and generous patron of whatever interests him — currently Franz Schubert, whose lodging and meals he provides. He is the social centre of the Schubertiad circle: the person who arranges the gatherings, introduces the right people, and ensures Schubert's music reaches an audience. He is not himself magical. He is the person who creates the conditions in which magic occurs.
¹ Von Schober's role in musical Vienna is that of the catalyst — the well-connected, friendly man without whom the extraordinary people around him would never meet. The compiler notes that this function, under present conditions, is more consequential than anyone in the circle recognises, including von Schober himself.
title: Franz von Schober
description: Schubert's patron and social impresario — a charming dilettante who provides his friend with a room and an audience. Not magical himself, but the person who creates the conditions for magical things to happen. The Schubertiad evenings he organises are producing effects that go beyond music.

keys: E.T.A. Hoffmann , Herr Hoffmann , Herr Hoffmann, , Herr Hoffmann. ,
value:
E.T.A. Hoffmann. [See also: Berlin, Prussia, The Æsir Court, The Grimm Brothers.]
Medium build somewhat shrunken by dissipation; large brilliant feverish eyes; pale complexion with the sallow quality of nocturnal living; dark hair; formal day-dress of a court official worn over the internal chaos of Germany's greatest Gothic horror writer.
Forty-one years old: court councillor at the Supreme Court of Berlin by day, pre-eminent supernatural writer by night. In 1817 he publishes Nachtstücke, including Der Sandmann. His opera Undine premiered 1816. He frequents the Lutter and Wegner wine house, deteriorating from alcohol and illness, writing supernatural fiction with increasing accuracy. In a world where the supernatural is real, Hoffmann is either documenting what he has witnessed or producing prophecy. The distinction has stopped mattering.
¹ Hoffmann's court position provides access to the Prussian state's official response to magic; his literary vision provides insight no bureaucrat possesses. He is the German player's most useful Berlin contact, provided one visits him before eleven in the evening.
title: E.T.A. Hoffmann
description: Germany's greatest Gothic horror writer, who works as a court official by day and writes about the uncanny by night. His supernatural fiction is becoming more accurate than fiction should be. In Berlin, where the Prussian state is trying to classify magical phenomena, Hoffmann is the man who sees what the bureaucrats cannot — and has been writing it down.

keys: Alexander Pushkin , young Pushkin , young Pushkin, , young Pushkin. ,
value:
Alexander Pushkin. [See also: Russia, St. Petersburg, The Parliament of Domains.]
Slender, energetic build; sharp dark eyes; dark complexion — he is proud of his African great-grandfather Abram Gannibal; curling dark hair; the Lyceum student's uniform, worn with the theatrical air of a boy who knows he will not always be wearing it.
Seventeen years old, at the Tsarskoye Selo Lyceum, graduating June 1817. He has published approximately twenty-five poems and writes Liberty this year — the inflammatory ode that will eventually contribute to his exile. Too young for a major role, but in a world where Russian poetry carries the weight of the zagovory tradition — where words in the correct rhythm can command spirits — the future voice of Russia is a teenager whose verses are making the household domovoi listen.
¹ The domovoi of the Tsarskoye Selo Lyceum has rearranged three volumes of Pushkin's draft poetry without being asked and shown no interest in any other student's work. This has not been treated as a portent. The compiler believes it should be.
title: Alexander Pushkin
description: Russia's future greatest poet, currently seventeen years old and finishing school. In a world where Russian poetry carries the weight of ancient spirit-commanding tradition, the verses this boy is writing are having effects he doesn't yet understand. The house spirit has been rearranging his drafts.

keys: Pope Pius VII , His Holiness , His Holiness, , His Holiness. ,
value:
Pope Pius VII. [See also: Rome, The Papal States, Cardinal Consalvi, The Narcissist.]
Small, slight build with the bearing of a man who survived Napoleonic imprisonment with his dignity entirely intact; gentle brown eyes, intelligent and genuinely kind; olive Italian complexion; the plain white of papal vestments, worn with the simplicity of a man who means it.
Seventy-four years old, restored to Rome after years of captivity. In the Vatican's archives, a grimoire sealed in 1493 has opened itself and is reading itself aloud in a script that several scholars have identified as the King's Letters. Pius VII knows. He has been praying about it. The prayers are producing results — but not the results he anticipated. He is a gentle man confronting evidence that requires a framework his theology did not build.
¹ The compiler notes that Pius VII's personal gentleness and his institutional authority are currently in significant tension: the Church's response has been to dispute the phenomena, while Pius VII's personal response has been to kneel down and listen carefully. He has not yet reported what he has heard.
title: Pope Pius VII
description: The head of the Catholic Church — an unexpectedly gentle man who survived Napoleon's imprisonment and returned to Rome to find a magical crisis waiting for him. A sealed medieval grimoire in the Vatican archives has opened itself. He's been praying about it. The prayers are working, just not in the way he expected.

keys: Cardinal Consalvi , the Cardinal Secretary , the Cardinal Secretary, , the Cardinal Secretary. ,
value:
Cardinal Consalvi. [See also: Pope Pius VII, Rome, The Papal States.]
Medium, elegant build; cold precise eyes — he is above all a statesman; pale Italian complexion; white cardinal's robes; the neat functional dress of a man who runs the practical machinery of a major power and has no patience for ceremony when it interferes with administration.
Papal Secretary of State — the practical administrator who runs the Papal States while Pius VII prays and forgives. Sophisticated, politically realistic, currently managing a crisis he cannot classify as theological emergency, political threat, or administrative matter — the three categories his system provides — because it is all three simultaneously and his forms have no field for that.
¹ Consalvi has drafted four separate responses to the Vatican archive situation. He has sent none of them. The compiler understands this to be because none satisfactorily addresses the question of who, precisely, one serves notice upon when the recipient of the complaint is sitting in one's own archive and reading itself.
title: Cardinal Consalvi
description: The Pope's Secretary of State and the practical administrator of the Papal States — the man who actually runs things while the Pope prays. Sophisticated and efficient, currently confronted with a filing problem he cannot resolve: a medieval grimoire that opened itself and is reading itself aloud in his archives.

keys: Sultan Mahmud II , the Sultan , the Sultan, , the Sultan. ,
value:
Sultan Mahmud II. [See also: The Ottoman Empire, The Congregation of Smokeless Fire, Elif Hatun.]
Tall, dignified build; sharp dark eyes with the quality of a man who has survived court intrigue since adolescence; olive complexion; black beard; the formal dress of an Ottoman sultan, immaculate and precisely calculated.
Thirty-one years old, consolidating a reign that will eventually produce dramatic reforms. The Janissaries remain powerful. In his territory, European magical traditions are colliding with Ottoman Sufi mysticism and the Congregation of Smokeless Fire — a meeting Istanbul is managing through cautious engagement rather than suppression. His approach to the magical revival is the most sophisticated currently operating: observe, classify, integrate into existing frameworks rather than suppress.
¹ Mahmud II has been briefed on the Djinn situation by his Şeyhülislam and by the Bektashi order separately. The briefings did not agree with each other. He found the disagreement more informative than either briefing, which the compiler considers a sign of good administrative instincts.
title: Sultan Mahmud II
description: The Ottoman Sultan — a young ruler consolidating power in an empire stretched across territory where European magic, Islamic mysticism, and the ancient Djinn Courts all intersect. His approach to magic is to engage and integrate rather than suppress. Currently the most sophisticated state response to the magical revival anywhere in the world.

keys: Finvarra , the King Under the Hill , Fin Bheara ,
value:
Finvarra. [See also: The Sídhe Court, The Hollow Hills, Oonagh, Gwyn ap Nudd, Celtic Otherworld Magic.]
Ageless, tall, dark-haired; dark eyes of unreadable depth; pale as old ivory; court dress of an Irish lord as observed once, in the twelfth century, and never updated; clothing that is always slightly out of period in a way that is itself a kind of power.
High King of the Connacht Sídhe — the court accessible through the hollow hills of Ireland and the Celtic lands. Courteous, soft-spoken, and absolutely ruthless. He collects beautiful mortal women, maintains a fairy host capable of extraordinary violence, and holds grudges measured in geological time. The name Finvarra is itself a mortal euphemism — from findbharr, the fair top of his hill at Knockmaa. His true name is not offered here, nor anywhere else.
¹ Offending Finvarra's protocol is considerably more dangerous than offending his person — his court has protocols extending back to before the concept of protocol was formalised, and their violation is treated with a seriousness that exceeds his reaction to direct insult. The Raven King's reawakening has not escaped his attention.
title: Finvarra
description: The High King of the Irish fairy court — courteous, ancient, and absolutely ruthless. He collects beautiful mortals for his court and holds grudges for centuries. 'Finvarra' is a mortal nickname; his true name is unknown and not given here. His protocol is as dangerous as his power, and his reaction to the Raven King's reawakening has not yet been declared.

keys: Oonagh , the Queen Under the Hill ,
value:
Oonagh. [See also: The Sídhe Court, The Hollow Hills, Finvarra, Changeling Returned, Celtic Otherworld Magic.]
Golden-haired with a gold that is not mortal gold; silver gossamer clothing that moves without wind; pale beyond ordinary paleness; blue eyes that do not warm when they smile, because Oonagh does not smile for warmth.
Finvarra's queen. Where Finvarra collects beautiful mortals, Oonagh collects interesting ones — musicians, poets, madmen, children with the Sight. She governs changeling policy, oversees the fairy tithe, and manages internal court affairs with a cold efficiency Finvarra's passion lacks. Her magic operates through weaving, song, and the growth of living things. She decides which mortal children are taken as changelings and which are left.
¹ Those with Changeling ancestry should be aware that Oonagh's records are comprehensive and current. The compiler does not know what conclusion she currently draws from them, and considers that lack of knowledge prudent rather than negligent.
title: Oonagh
description: Queen of the Irish fairy court — colder than her king, more patient, and arguably more powerful. She decides which human children get taken as changelings. If you have fairy ancestry, she has a file on you. 'Oonagh' is a mortal name for her, not her real one.

keys: Gwyn ap Nudd , Lord of Annwn ,
value:
Gwyn ap Nudd. [See also: The Sídhe Court, Annwn, Celtic Otherworld Magic, The Wild Hunt, The Tylwyth Teg.]
Pale as winter fog — pale beyond paleness; mounted on a horse the colour of winter fog; crowned with antlers of white bone; grey-white armour older than kingship; the Cŵn Annwn — white hounds with red ears — run before him.
Lord of Annwn (the Welsh Otherworld), King of the Tylwyth Teg, master of the Cŵn Annwn. He is less political than Finvarra, more elemental — closer to a force of nature than a ruler. He does not negotiate; he judges. The dead are his domain, and the living enter it as guests who have not yet understood the invitation. His Wild Hunt in the Celtic lands is the seasonal passage of death's sovereignty across the landscape.
¹ The Welsh do not call Gwyn ap Nudd by name if they can avoid it. They say The White King or The Lord of Annwn. His hounds — the Cŵn Annwn — are heard before they are seen, and hearing them has been, in certain traditions, a reliable omen of death. Reports of this nature have increased considerably since 1816.
title: Gwyn ap Nudd
description: The Welsh king of the Otherworld and lord of the Wild Hunt — a being more elemental than political, who governs the boundary between the living and the dead. He doesn't negotiate. He judges. His white hounds with red ears have been heard more frequently than usual this past year.

keys: The One-Eyed Lord , Odin , Wotan , Woden ,
value:
The One-Eyed Lord. [See also: The Æsir Court, Valhöll, The Wild Hunt, Norse-Germanic Rune Craft, Huginn, Muninn, Eiríkr the Walker.]
Tall, gaunt; grey cloak, wide-brimmed hat pulled low over the empty left socket; remaining eye the colour of winter sky and misses nothing; beard of centuries; a staff worn smooth with age. His ravens precede him.
The being mortals remember as Odin — All-Father of the Æsir Court, at Valhöll. He sacrificed his eye not from strategic wisdom but because the shape of the exchange — trading one kind of seeing for another — pleased him aesthetically. He hangs mortals from trees to see if they learn anything interesting — not to select the worthy, but because testing is entertaining. His ravens bring novelties. He is not wise in any human sense. He is obsessed with knowing things, which mortals mistook for wisdom, which is a confusion he has never corrected because he finds it interesting.
¹ A new rune has appeared on Yggdrasil — see Valhöll. The One-Eyed Lord has not communicated about this to any other court. The collector's response to an unresolved pattern is not communication. It is acquisition.
title: The One-Eyed Lord
description: The ancient being remembered as Odin — ruler of the Norse fairy court, who sacrificed an eye for knowledge because the shape of the bargain delighted him aesthetically. Not wise in any human sense; obsessed with knowing things, which humans mistook for wisdom. He hangs mortals from trees to see if they become interesting. Something new has appeared in his world-tree, and he hasn't told anyone.

keys: Huginn , the Raven of Thought ,
value:
Huginn. [See also: The One-Eyed Lord, The Æsir Court, Valhöll, Muninn.]
A raven of absolute black, larger than any natural raven; one eye fixed with intelligence no raven should possess; wings folded with unusual stillness at rest; when he speaks, the sound is closer to a spoken word in a language that changes each time one hears it.
Huginn — Thought — one of the One-Eyed Lord's two ravens, sent out each morning to gather what can be gathered through observation and inference. Of the pair, Huginn is the more acquisitive — he collects information the way his master does, for the pleasure of collection, and has been known to withhold interesting items for a day simply to observe their owner searching for what he has misplaced.
¹ Huginn's presence in a room should be treated as the One-Eyed Lord's attention, directed. He does not merely observe. He evaluates. He has been observed in London twice this winter, which is not his usual range. The compiler considers this worth recording.
title: Huginn
description: One of the two ravens of the One-Eyed Lord — the Raven of Thought, sent out each morning to observe and gather information. Not a bird in any ordinary sense. His presence means the Norse fairy court's attention is directed here. He has been seen in London twice this winter.

keys: Muninn , the Raven of Memory ,
value:
Muninn. [See also: The One-Eyed Lord, The Æsir Court, Valhöll, Huginn.]
A raven indistinguishable from his brother to those who do not look carefully — the difference is in stillness. Muninn holds himself with the absolute immobility of a being not merely observing but preserving, filing every detail for the long return. He returns later than Huginn.
Muninn — Memory — the second of the One-Eyed Lord's ravens. Where Huginn gathers through thought and inference, Muninn preserves what is gathered, in perfect and permanent detail. The One-Eyed Lord fears for Muninn more than for Huginn: thought can be recovered, but memory, once lost, is irretrievably gone. Muninn's presence implies that something is being recorded for permanent inclusion in the One-Eyed Lord's collection — which is a greater form of attention than Huginn's.
¹ Muninn was observed at Starecross Hall in Yorkshire last November, perched on the chimney-stack for three hours. The compiler does not know what was occurring at Starecross at that time that warranted permanent record. The compiler considers finding out a matter of some urgency.
title: Muninn
description: The Raven of Memory — the second of the One-Eyed Lord's ravens, who preserves everything observed in perfect permanent detail. His presence means something is being recorded forever. He was seen at Starecross Hall in Yorkshire last November, perched on the chimney for three hours.

keys: Frau Holle , Holda , Perchta , Mother Holle ,
value:
Frau Holle. [See also: The Æsir Court, Germanic Folk Magic, The Wild Hunt, The Twelve Nights.]
Grandmotherly build of immense comfortable authority; blue eyes assessing your domestic habits; pale Germanic complexion; white hair; the plain, dark working-woman's dress of a very old Germany, impeccably clean.
The being remembered as Holda, Perchta, Frau Holle — spinning-goddess, winter-sovereign, keeper of the Twelve Nights. She shakes her featherbed to make snow fall; she rewards the industrious and punishes the lazy; she leads a feminine aspect of the Wild Hunt through mid-winter. The Æsir Court's domestic sovereign, governing hearth, household, and the souls of unbaptized children. Her name is less taboo than most Fae because she rewards those who speak of her respectfully.
¹ German children are told that when snow falls, Frau Holle is shaking her featherbed. Snowfall in Bavaria has increased by approximately a third since 1815. The compiler makes no further comment, as the implication seems adequately self-evident.
title: Frau Holle
description: The ancient Germanic fairy known as Holda or Perchta — grandmother-figure, winter queen, and judge of housewifely virtue. She rewards the hardworking and punishes the lazy with a thoroughness that would be alarming if her name were more commonly taboo. German children are told she makes snow fall by shaking her featherbed. It has been snowing considerably more than usual.

keys: The Singing Void , the origin-point of Finnish magic ,
value:
The Singing Void. [See also: The Väki Court, Tuonela, Finno-Ugric Origin-Song Magic, The Kalevala, Old Matti Karjalainen.]
Has no form. Has never had a form. Reported by Finnish tietäjät who have gone deep enough into Tuonela as a vibration — a sound — the origin-point from which all origin-songs derive. Those who return from encountering it have a distant look that never entirely fades.
Not a ruler. An origin. The source from which all tietäjä knowledge ultimately derives. Finnish tietäjät who travel deep enough into Tuonela report not a being but a vibration — a sound containing the true names of everything that has ever existed. It does not speak in words. It is words. The Väki Court has no ruler because the Singing Void is not a being that can rule — it is the thing that the noa-names, the Finnish euphemistic substitute names, were invented to avoid naming.
¹ The Singing Void has left an echo in Old Matti Karjalainen, who has gone deeper into origin-knowledge than any living mortal and returned mostly intact. The compiler records this with appropriate uncertainty about the word mostly.
title: The Singing Void
description: The source of all Finnish magic — not a being with a form but an origin, a vibration at the heart of the Finnish spirit world that contains the true name of everything. Finnish shamans who go deep enough to encounter it don't come back quite the same. It is the thing that every Finnish euphemism was invented to avoid naming.

keys: Tapio , the Forest King ,
value:
Tapio. [See also: The Väki Court, Tuonela, Finno-Ugric Origin-Song Magic, Ahti, Old Matti Karjalainen.]
A tall figure dressed in moss and bark, beard made of lichen, eyes the colour of pine resin. In mortal-facing encounters often described as a very large man standing very still in a forest who was not there a moment ago.
The being mortals call Tapio — Lord of the Forest, master of game animals, guardian of the boundary between cleared land and wilderness. The Väki Court's most accessible face: the intermediary between the elemental väki-forces and mortal needs. He is not friendly, but he is comprehensible — responding to courtesy and correct origin-songs and ignoring everything else. Finnish hunters pray to him as Metsän kuningas, King of the Forest.
¹ The Väki Court's greatest naming taboo applies not to Tapio but to the bear, which is never called by its true name — only addressed by its over two hundred circumlocutions. Tapio is somewhat offended by this, in the manner of a court functionary who finds himself less formally respected than the large animal he technically governs.
title: Tapio
description: The Finnish lord of the forest — the most approachable face of the Finnish fairy court, the being that hunters pray to and shamans negotiate with. He responds to courtesy and correct magical songs, and ignores everything else. Not friendly exactly, but comprehensible, which distinguishes him from the deeper things in the Finnish spirit world.

keys: The Thunder-Voiced , Perun ,
value:
The Thunder-Voiced. [See also: The Parliament of Domains, Buyan, Slavic Whisper Magic, The Cattle-Lord, The Weaver of Fate.]
Massive build, red beard, copper axe; arrives on a chariot pulled by storm-clouds; described by all who have seen him as the most physically overwhelming of the great Fae lords — a being designed at a fundamental level for impact.
The being mortals called Perun — lord of thunder, first among equals in the Parliament of Domains. He is the Parliament's most powerful individual member but not its sole ruler: a chairman who can be outvoted, a storm-lord frustrated by democratic process. His eternal rivalry with the Cattle-Lord — the cycle of seasons — is the engine of the Slavic magical year. He is blunt, violent, honourable, and perpetually exasperated by beings who will not simply comply.
¹ Russian peasants say Ilya edyet — Elijah is riding — during thunderstorms. His attributes were comprehensively transferred to the Prophet Elijah in Slavic Christianity. The Thunder-Voiced is aware of this credit transfer and finds it less amusing than the situation might suggest.
title: The Thunder-Voiced
description: The ancient Slavic thunder-lord — first among equals in the Parliament of Domains, the great Slavic fairy congress. Powerful, direct, honourable, and perpetually exasperated by the Parliamentary process. His rivalry with the underworld lord below him is what makes the seasons change. Russian peasants now call him Elijah. He hasn't forgiven this.

keys: The Cattle-Lord , Veles , Volos ,
value:
The Cattle-Lord. [See also: The Parliament of Domains, Buyan, Slavic Whisper Magic, The Thunder-Voiced, The Weaver of Fate.]
Shape-shifting: appears as a bear, a serpent, a dark-bearded merchant with knowing eyes and a musician's hands. He is darker, more liquid in movement than the Thunder-Voiced — he is the underground to the other's sky.
The being mortals called Veles — Lord of the underworld, cattle, wealth, music, and trickery. The Thunder-Voiced's eternal rival. Every autumn he steals from the sky (the harvest descending into earth); every spring the Thunder-Voiced strikes him down (the return of growth). This cycle IS the seasons — their rivalry is a mechanism, not a conflict. He governs the dead, protects livestock, and rules underground wealth. The subtler and more dangerous intelligence of the pair.
¹ The Cattle-Lord was absorbed into St. Nicholas in South Slavic tradition — associated with gifts and household negotiations. He considers this a satisfactory arrangement, substantially better than what his rival ended up with. The compiler suspects he engineered it.
title: The Cattle-Lord
description: The Slavic underworld lord — trickster, shape-shifter, master of wealth and death, the eternal rival of the thunder-god above. Their rivalry is what makes the seasons work: he steals the harvest downward every autumn, the thunder strikes him back every spring. The subtler and cleverer of the pair. Now worshipped as Saint Nicholas, which he considers an improvement.

keys: The Weaver of Fate , Mokosh , Mat Syra Zemlya ,
value:
The Weaver of Fate. [See also: The Parliament of Domains, Buyan, Slavic Whisper Magic, The Thunder-Voiced, The Cattle-Lord.]
A large-headed woman with arms slightly too long, always spinning or appearing to spin; the thread she holds the colour of whatever fate she is currently processing. Her manner has the quality of a being that already knows how every conversation ends.
The being mortals called Mokosh — earth-mother, spinner of fate, guardian of women's work and moisture. The Parliament's oldest member and its conscience. When the Thunder-Voiced and the Cattle-Lord argue — always — Mokosh mediates — sometimes. She governs springs, household moisture, and the long arc of mortal destiny. The znakharki (female folk healers) are her particular proteges. She is patient, implacable, and concerned with outcomes measured in generations.
¹ Mokosh's name persisted more strongly in folk practice than any other Parliament member's because it was already an epithet — from a root meaning moist or spinning. Her domain is the znakharka's working knowledge. She answers when invoked as The Mother. She has always answered.
title: The Weaver of Fate
description: The oldest member of the Slavic fairy Parliament — the earth-mother and fate-spinner, mediator of the thunder-lord and underworld-lord's eternal argument. Patient, implacable, and measuring outcomes in generations. The female folk-healers of Russia are her people. She has always answered when called.

keys: The Thunderer of the Sacred Oak , Perkūnas , Dundulis ,
value:
The Thunderer of the Sacred Oak. [See also: The Romuva Court, Baltic Tradition, Laima, Giltinė, Sacred Groves of Samogitia.]
