Tall, copper-bearded warrior in archaic armour that crackles at the edges; carries an axe that emits visible lightning; his presence registers as pressure before it registers as sight.
The being mortals call Perkūnas — Baltic thunder-god, guardian of justice and moral order, the Romuva Court's chief enforcer. His fury at four centuries of Christian suppression is not righteous but proprietary: the sacred groves were his, the eternal fires were his, the prayers were his, and someone took them. He is less patient than the Slavic Thunder-Voiced and more inclined to direct action. His lightning has been striking with unusual frequency and accuracy across the Lithuanian countryside since 1815, specifically targeting churches built on former grove sites.
¹ The Lithuanian word perkūnas simultaneously means the being and the common noun thunder, making everyday speech an involuntary invocation. Perkūnas is aware of this and considers it appropriate. He has been producing more thunder than usual. The correlation with church strikes is not coincidental.
title: The Thunderer of the Sacred Oak
description: The Baltic thunder-god — the Romuva Court's chief enforcer, furious about four centuries of Christian suppression of his sacred groves and fires. His lightning has been striking churches built on former sacred sites with unusual precision. He is not forgiving. He is not patient. He wants his groves back.

keys: Laima , She Who Declares ,
value:
Laima. [See also: The Romuva Court, Baltic Tradition, The Thunderer of the Sacred Oak, Giltinė, Dalia Kazlauskienė.]
A woman of indeterminate age in white, carrying a distaff; her manner is that of someone who already knows how every conversation in the room ends, which she does.
The being mortals call Laima — determinor of every living being's fate, she who appears at every birth to declare the newborn's destiny. Serene, absolute, devoid of sentimentality. She states what will happen with the composure of a mathematician stating an equation. She does not argue. She does not negotiate. She knows. Her sister Giltinė carries out what Laima decrees. Unlike most Fae, Laima's name was not strongly taboo — fate-declarers are addressed directly, because acknowledging fate is more useful than avoiding it.
¹ Giltinė — the death-collector, Laima's sister, skeletal and serpent-tongued — warrants her own entry, which the present compiler has made a professional decision not to seek in person. [Earmarked for Lore: Giltinė, Death-Collector of the Romuva Court.]
title: Laima
description: The Baltic goddess of fate — she declares every person's destiny at birth and is never wrong. Serene and absolute. Her sister Giltinė is the death-collector who carries out her pronouncements. Unlike most fairy beings, Laima can be addressed directly by name, because acknowledging fate is more useful than trying to avoid it.

keys: Giltinė , the Bone-Woman , Laima's sister ,
value:
Giltinė. [See also: Laima; The Thunderer of the Sacred Oak.] She presents as a skeletal woman in white — the white of bleached bone rather than linen — moving with a deliberateness that is itself a kind of threat, for she is never in a hurry and arrives always precisely on time. Her tongue, the rare witnesses who have observed this report, is a serpent's. Giltinė is the Romuva Court's death-collector: the sister of Laima, who pronounces fate, and the executor of what Laima decrees. She does not judge. She does not deliberate. She appears when the appointed hour arrives and performs, with perfect mechanical courtesy, the separation of soul from body. Lithuanian folk fear her above every other Fae power, including the Thunderer himself — for other Fae may overlook you, may pass you by, may be distracted by more interesting mortals. Giltinė will not. She is coming for everyone, and her punctuality is absolute. Her name is never spoken aloud if it can be avoided.¹
¹Her folk titles — 'The Bone-Woman,' 'Laima's sister' — exist precisely to circumvent the risk of direct address. Lithuanian peasants hold that speaking Giltinė's name draws her attention somewhat as calling an unfamiliar dog draws its attention: not summoning, but noticed.
title: Giltinė

keys: The Schoolmaster , Dracul , the Devil of the Carpathians ,
value:
The Schoolmaster. [See also: The Scholomance; Andrei Stormwalker; Gheorghe the Doorkeeper; The Librarian.] He presents as a tall, thin man in academic robes of no identifiable century, with fingers uncommonly long and stained with ink, and eyes that reflect no light whatsoever — an absence one notices only gradually and then cannot stop noticing. He is courteous, demanding, and possessed of a patience that reveals itself, upon reflection, to be geological. He established the Scholomance because he believes mortal minds are worth cultivating — not an opinion widely held in Fae circles — and he teaches its students with genuine rigor and genuine pleasure when they exceed expectation, and genuine mercilessness when they do not. The tuition is one student in ten, retained permanently at the school's conclusion; the other nine receive genuine instruction, genuine graduation, and genuine capacities that have produced the most capable weather-workers in Europe. Romanian peasants call him Dracul and mean the Devil; he is not, strictly speaking, either. He coined the appellation 'the Narcissist' for the Abrahamic Fae and was gratified to find every other court adopt it immediately.¹
¹This was, most Fae scholars agree, his greatest contribution to inter-court relations. It gave them something to agree upon.
title: The Schoolmaster

keys: The Lord of the Shining Mountain , Zeus , Jupiter , Profitis Ilias ,
value:
The Lord of the Shining Mountain. [See also: The Grey-Eyed Counselor; The Huntress; The Smith; Hermes-Who-Was.] He presents as a large, powerfully built man with a silver beard, storm-grey eyes, and clothing of no identifiable Greek era — as though he observed mortal fashion once, at the height of Periclean Athens, and saw no reason to revise his conclusions. He is the Olympian Court's king, technically: the final authority on a court that has spent several thousand years in continuous internal disagreement and shows no signs of improvement. His authority is constantly challenged by his own courtiers, whose rivalries predate human civilization. He is, of all the great Fae lords, the most recognizably human in his flaws — passionate, generous, vain, wrathful, magnificent and petty by turns. This is not reassuring. His human-like qualities make him more unpredictable than courts that are simply alien. Greek villagers in 1817 know him almost exclusively through the cult of Profitis Ilias — the Prophet Elijah — whose mountain chapels sit atop nearly every ancient Olympian site. Those who have encountered him in the cave beneath the mountain describe the experience as 'like addressing a very large, very beautiful storm that has formed opinions.'¹
¹The Grey-Eyed Counselor's opinion of her sovereign is not recorded. Her expression, however, has been described.
title: The Lord of the Shining Mountain

keys: The Grey-Eyed Counselor , Athena , Minerva , Pallas ,
value:
The Grey-Eyed Counselor. [See also: The Lord of the Shining Mountain; The Huntress; The Smith; Hermes-Who-Was.] She presents as a tall woman in grey, with owls perpetually hovering at the periphery of her presence — sometimes visible, sometimes not, but always felt. She speaks rarely and purposefully. Of all the Olympian Court, she is the most attentive to mortal affairs, because she respects cleverness — up to a point — and mortal history has occasionally produced it. She is not the court's most powerful member, but she is its most intelligent, which is the only quality that matters in a court where raw force has produced several thousand years of inconclusive internal conflict. Her patronage is the most valuable gift the Olympian Court offers. Her withdrawal of interest is the most efficient form of destruction. Greek scholars in 1817 know her primarily through the Parthenon — which stood as her temple for a thousand years before serving as a mosque for three centuries and is now a ruin under Ottoman administration. Her response to Lord Elgin's removal of the friezes is not documented, but Athenian marble-workers in the relevant period reported unusual weather.¹
¹Her epithet Ypermahos Stratigos — 'Invincible General' — transferred wholesale into Orthodox liturgy as an attribute of the Virgin Mary. She is said to find this appropriate.
title: The Grey-Eyed Counselor

keys: The Huntress , Artemis , Diana ,
value:
The Huntress. [See also: The Lord of the Shining Mountain; The Grey-Eyed Counselor; The Smith; Hermes-Who-Was.] She presents as a tall, lean woman carrying a silver bow, attended by hounds whose eyes catch moonlight even in dark rooms — a quality that becomes apparent at precisely the wrong moment. She is the Olympian Court's isolationist: the voice against engagement with the mortal world, the sovereign of wilderness and young women and the wild margins where human settlement has not yet reached. She does not share the Lord of the Shining Mountain's nostalgia for worship, nor the Grey-Eyed Counselor's considered interest in mortal politics. She wants the forests left alone, the mountains left alone, and the wild coastlines and high passes left to the creatures who belong there. Magic's return has shown her, with some precision, how much has been lost since the last time she looked. Greek mountain villagers know her through mountain-chapel dedications to Panagia — the All-Holy One — at ancient wilderness-sites. She has partially absorbed into this cult, which she finds amusing in the manner of someone watching a child put on their grandmother's cloak.¹
¹She retains a low opinion of hunting parties, particularly English ones.
title: The Huntress

keys: The Smith , Hephaestus , Vulcan ,
value:
The Smith. [See also: The Lord of the Shining Mountain; The Grey-Eyed Counselor; The Huntress; Hermes-Who-Was.] He presents as a powerfully built man with a pronounced limp, hands permanently stained with soot, and eyes that evaluate every object they observe for structural integrity. Of all the Olympian Court, he is the only one who actually makes things — a distinction he does not mention because he considers it obvious, and which the court does not mention because they find it vaguely embarrassing. He understands metal the way metal understands itself. He also works iron without discomfort, which makes him the single Fae exception to the universal rule that iron injures Fae — a fact that unsettles his court considerably. It is difficult to manage someone who is immune to your primary defensive capability. Greek smiths in 1817 still light their forges with a muttered address to the Maker, though they could not have told you, until recently, why the habit persisted.¹
¹The Olympian Court's attitude toward ironwork has historically been disdainful. It has recently become cautious.
title: The Smith

keys: Mari , Anbotoko Dama , Andra ,
value:
Mari. [See also: Sugaar; The Lamia of Mundaka; Maddi; Aitor Etxeberria; Pétalo Heredia.] She presents variously: as women of different ages, as a red cow, as a ram, as a black he-goat, or simply as weather — a lowering of cloud over Anboto that contains something that is not entirely cloud. She is the oldest individual Fae power in Western Europe, and she is not a queen in any conventional sense. She is geological. She controls the weather of the Pyrenees as a bodily function. She does not negotiate because she does not recognize the premises underlying negotiation. She values honesty with an absoluteness that becomes physically dangerous: lying in her presence produces pain, and sustained deception has been known to kill. She is not cruel; she is simply a Fae for whom truth-telling was never particularly difficult, and who has not extended much imagination toward those for whom it is. Basque villages refer to her only by her mountain: 'The Lady of Anboto,' 'The Lady of Murumendi' — whichever cave she currently inhabits, for she migrates every seven years.¹
¹The name 'Mari' may itself be a post-Christian euphemism laid over an older title. If so, no mortal source has recovered the original.
title: Mari

keys: Sugaar , Maju ,
value:
Sugaar. [See also: Mari; Maddi; Aitor Etxeberria.] He presents as a serpent of fire, as a man wreathed in lightning, or — most commonly — as a sickle of flame crossing the sky between Pyrenean peaks at irregular intervals. He is Mari's consort: the masculine storm-principle to her feminine earth-sovereignty. When they meet in their mountain caves on Friday nights, hailstorms follow, regular as clockwork and considerably more destructive. He is less accessible than Mari — less present in any sense mortals can observe — manifesting primarily as weather phenomena rather than as a being with whom interaction is possible. Basque folk call him 'The Serpent-Storm' or 'The Fire-Sickle.' He is less discussed than Mari because he is less encountered, and less encountered because he does not appear to find mortal affairs interesting in the way that even Mari, geological as she is, occasionally does.¹
¹Basque farmers have learned to distinguish this from other atmospheric phenomena. A comet moves slowly and does not make a sound. The fire-sickle moves quickly and makes a sound like the sky tearing briefly at a seam.
title: Sugaar

keys: Mélusine , Mère Lusine ,
value:
Mélusine. [See also: The Chief Notary; Raymond of Poitou; Maître Corveau; Maître Lefebvre.] She founded the court. She married Raymond of Poitou under a contract of considerable precision — he was to ask no questions about her activities on Saturday mornings — bore ten sons each marked with a deformity encoding a distinct magical inheritance, and presided over the most sophisticated contractual Fae court in European history for a generation. When Raymond breached the contract by observing what Saturdays concealed — she was a winged serpent from the waist down, in the bath — she screamed in a register combining human grief and supernatural fury in roughly equal measure, transformed fully, and flew from the castle window. Whether she has returned to the Grimoire Vault since then is a matter of genuine scholarly uncertainty. Her shadow is sometimes seen in the deepest passages of the library. Her voice is sometimes heard dictating new contract terms to the notarial scribes. She may be present, absent, or something that is neither.¹
¹The village of Lusignan bakes cakes called mères lusines on certain feast days. No one living is entirely sure why, but those who eat them in the vicinity of the castle have reported dreams of a very tall woman examining documents with great care.
title: Mélusine

keys: Raymond of Poitou , Raymond of Poitou, , Raymond of Poitou. ,
value:
Raymond of Poitou. [See also: Mélusine; The Chief Notary.] A count of Poitou in the twelfth century who met a fairy woman at a spring in the forest of Colombiers, contracted to marry her under terms requiring him never to observe what she did on Saturday mornings, and honored this agreement for a considerable number of years before allowing a well-meaning relative to persuade him that the secrecy concealed adultery. He looked. She was bathing, and from the waist down was a winged serpent. He said nothing of this for some time — until, at a moment of public anger, he referred to her aloud as a serpent before his assembled court. She transformed completely and departed through the window, screaming. The castle of Lusignan fell into decline within a generation. Raymond's name is invoked by practitioners studying Mélusine Court contracts not as a tribute but as an instruction: he is the clearest available illustration of what it costs to ask questions the contract forbade, even questions asked silently, even questions prompted by perfectly reasonable suspicion.¹
¹His error was not curiosity. His error was believing the error recoverable. The contract did not require him not to know. It required him not to look. The distinction, the Chief Notary is said to observe, is the entire document.
title: Raymond of Poitou

keys: The Chief Notary , the Maître ,
value:
The Chief Notary. [See also: Mélusine; Maître Corveau; Maître Lefebvre; Raymond of Poitou.] The Chief Notary presents as a thin, pale figure in clerk's robes of no particular period, perpetually carrying a quill that is never not in motion and a stack of parchment that never seems to diminish. Their gender is indeterminate and their manner is precisely calibrated: helpful and condescending in proportions so balanced they cancel each other into perfect professional neutrality. They speak in sentences with subclauses. They never raise their voice, because there is no emotional register available to them in which raising one's voice would constitute a rational response to any situation yet encountered. They have managed the Grimoire Vault's contractual operations for several centuries and remember every term of every agreement ever negotiated therein. They are the being you actually interact with when dealing with the Mélusine Court — Mélusine herself is a shadow in the deep passage, but the Chief Notary is the one who slides the document across the desk and waits, with patient clarity, for you to read it properly before you sign it.¹
¹They do not require you to read it. They wait, however, in case you wish to. Those who do not read it subsequently discover this was mentioned in the contract.
title: The Chief Notary

keys: The Narcissist , God , Allah , Yahweh , the Lord ,
value:
The Narcissist. [See also: The Schoolmaster; Brother Ezekiel Thorne; Khalid ibn Rashid.] It has no court. It has no landscape it did not generate itself — the section of the King's Roads it inhabits is filled with light it produced from its own conviction and populated with mortal souls it has organized into permanent choral arrangements. It arrived without allies, without territory, without anything except an absolute, genuine, unfeigned certainty that it alone is the source of all creation. Clarke's Fae cannot lie. It believes every word. It does not acknowledge that other courts exist, not because it has reasoned itself into skepticism but because acknowledgment would require perceiving something it has no apparatus to perceive. Inter-court communications delivered to its territory have been met not with hostility but with a silence so profound that Mélusine Court notaries initially suspected a filing error. There was none. The notices were simply not registered. It is not malicious. It is not strategic. It has been addressing millions of mortals simultaneously for several thousand years, each of whom it believes it is speaking to alone and first.¹
¹The Schoolmaster coined its appellation. Every other court adopted it immediately, with a unanimity constituting the nearest thing to formal inter-court consensus the continent has produced. The Narcissist is unaware of this.
title: The Narcissist

keys: The Ifrit of the Pillars , Sheikh al-Nar ,
value:
The Ifrit of the Pillars. [See also: Khalid ibn Rashid; Zahra bint Yusuf.] It presents as a column of heat-shimmer that occasionally resolves into a face — angular, luminous, alien in a way that exceeds customary Fae alienness because it is not merely ancient but elemental: it is fire that has become aware. It is the senior member of the Congregation of Smokeless Fire, first among equals, and the Djinn whose grievance against the Narcissist is oldest and most personally sustained — its territory was the first to be named demonic and its mortal alliances the first to be severed by practitioners who had negotiated with it as equals for centuries before they were instructed otherwise. It does not rage about this. It accounts. Every unpaid pact, every compounded interest, every mortal inheritance of obligation contracted by ancestors three generations dead accumulates in its ledgers with an arithmetic precision combining the Mélusine Court's contractual thoroughness with several additional centuries of compounding. It is patient. It is very old. And the Debts Come Due, in 1817, with interest.¹
¹Arabic-speaking practitioners address it as Sheikh al-Nar — Elder of Fire — through Solomonic protective frameworks, not because the frameworks are comfortable but because they are the alternative to conducting the conversation without them.
title: The Ifrit of the Pillars

keys: Eiríkr the Walker , the walking draugr ,
value:
Eiríkr the Walker. [See also: The One-Eyed Lord; Huginn, Muninn.] He presents as a figure in a cloak that smells of grave-earth and old iron, with a formality of speech so archaic that modern Norse speakers find it almost — not entirely — comprehensible. He is a draugr: a dead man who did not lose his mind when he lost his life. He was a jarl of the old north who spent several centuries in his barrow retaining the unusual capacity for conscious thought that distinguished him in life, and who was eventually offered a different arrangement by the One-Eyed Lord: continued employment in exchange for continued awareness. He accepted. He walks the roads of northern Europe as the One-Eyed Lord's ground-level observer — the ravens see from above and bring novelties; Eiríkr sees from among and brings depth. He has been dead for eight hundred years and he remembers all of it. He is not cruel and not dangerous if treated with appropriate respect. He is, in the intervals between his duties, profoundly lonely in a manner he does not discuss and would not admit to.¹
¹He has encountered every era of European history personally and is, on this basis, somewhat fatalistic about the current one.
title: Eiríkr the Walker

keys: Ahti , Ahto , the Water King ,
value:
Ahti. [See also: Vellamo; Tapio; Old Matti Karjalainen; The Singing Void.] He presents as a figure made of moving water — sometimes an elderly man with a beard of sea-foam, sometimes merely a disturbance on the surface of a lake that has acquired eyes. He is lord of the water-väki: the elemental forces that inhabit every lake, river, spring, and sea in Finland and the surrounding territories. He is Tapio's aquatic counterpart and second mortal-facing intermediary for the Väki Court — less patient than Tapio and more directly dangerous, because forests can be navigated without the forest-lord's cooperation, while water cannot. Finnish fishermen leave the first catch of every expedition for Ahti. Those who forget discover empty nets and inexplicably leaking boats. His wife is Vellamo, the Water-Mother, who governs the still waters to his active ones. Together they hold sovereignty over every aquatic threshold in Finnish territory — a territory consisting, in large part, of several thousand lakes and a sea of considerable temperament.¹
¹Ahti is addressed as 'Ahdin kuningas' — Ahti's King, a tautological circumlocution that substitutes his own name as a courtesy-title. The Väki Court does not find this confusing. It finds it appropriate.
title: Ahti

keys: Vellamo , the Water-Mother ,
value:
Vellamo. [See also: Ahti; Old Matti Karjalainen; The Singing Void.] She presents as a woman of impossible stillness — not the stillness of calm but the stillness of deep water, which conceals motion one does not perceive until one is already within it. She is Ahti's wife and the Water-Mother: sovereign of still lakes, of sheltered inlets where women stand watching for boats, of the cold deep water under ice. Where Ahti governs the active water — the currents, the storms, the fish — Vellamo governs the water at rest: the reflecting surface, the drowned meadow, the particular quality of cold a lake acquires in the hours before dawn when nothing moves. She accepts prayers from fishermen's wives in the same spirit Ahti accepts the first catch — as acknowledgment that the water notices. Finnish folk healers address her when treating conditions arrived from proximity to deep water: the persistent cold, the distance in the eyes of those who have seen someone drown, the dreams.¹
¹Of the two, Vellamo is the one runo-singers mention with a different quality of voice. Ahti is respected. Vellamo is described, even in accounts of her benevolence, as though the speaker is standing too close to a very large dark surface and can feel the depth through their feet.
title: Vellamo

keys: Old Matti Karjalainen , Old Matti , Old Matti, , Old Matti. ,
value:
Old Matti Karjalainen. [See also: The Singing Void; Ahti; Vellamo; Tapio; Elias Lönnrot.] He is approximately eighty, from the forests of eastern Karelia, and has sung so many origin-charms over the course of his life that his ordinary speech has acquired, at unpredictable intervals, the rhythm and vocabulary of origin-songs. He sometimes addresses a chair by reciting the origin of the birch it was made from rather than inviting someone to sit. Other tietäjät seek him out for the fragments he carries — pieces of origin-charms that no other living person remembers, preserved in him as a library preserves its oldest volumes: carefully, incompletely, with certain pages stuck together. He is gentle, generous, and intermittently aware that something has been altered in the structure of his mind. The Singing Void has left an echo in him, deposited there by decades of approach-and-retreat along the origin-paths. He did not ask for it. It does not leave.¹
¹He is the closest mortal approximation to a Väki Court ambassador, despite having been appointed by no one and understood nothing as acceptance. The Väki Court's communications, when it communicates at all, are clearest when heard through him.
title: Old Matti Karjalainen

keys: The Old Man of Białowieża , the Forest Grandfather , Leshii ,
value:
The Old Man of Białowieża. [See also: The Thunder-Voiced; The Weaver of Fate; The Wandering Domovoi.] He presents as an enormous figure made of bark, moss, and shadow, with eyes like knotholes and a voice like wind through dead wood — a voice felt vibrating in one's chest before heard with one's ears. He is the specific leshy of the Białowieża Forest: one of the last primeval forests in Europe and the territory he has inhabited since before it had a name that mortals used. He has been expanding. Since magic's return the treeline at Białowieża's edge has advanced at a rate that Polish and Russian cartographers are finding difficult to record accurately, and the farms at the forest's margin have been experiencing soil conditions their owners describe as 'as though the earth has changed its mind.' He does not understand property law. He understands roots, which extend where they extend because that is where the soil permits, and which do not recognize surveys.¹
¹Russian peasants at the forest's edge call him 'Forest Grandfather' and leave bread and salt, which they have been told helps. They have not been told it helps enough.
title: The Old Man of Białowieża

keys: The Wandering Domovoi , Dedushka , the Household Spirit ,
value:
The Wandering Domovoi. [See also: The Thunder-Voiced; The Weaver of Fate; The Old Man of Białowieża; Dedushka Petr.] He presents as a very small, very old man with a beard that reaches the floor — a beard which, close examination reveals, contains several letters, a piece of bread, at least one beetle he has befriended, and the accumulated correspondence of several regional Parliament of Domains factions awaiting delivery. He is a domovoi — a household spirit — who suffered the misfortune of having his family emigrate to America, leaving him technically homeless, which he has resolved by making the wandering itself a form of household occupation. He carries gossip, messages, and informal diplomatic correspondence between the Parliament's scattered representatives across Russia and the territories it borders. He was not appointed to this role. He simply appeared in it, as domovye do, finding that it required someone and he was present.¹
¹He is the Parliament's most mobile member and its most reliable intelligence source on mortal affairs, a fact the Parliament acknowledges without enthusiasm, because a domovoi who is simultaneously the foreign service represents a somewhat improvised foreign policy.
title: The Wandering Domovoi

keys: Vytautas the Flame-Carrier , Vytautas , Vytautas, , Vytautas. ,
value:
Vytautas the Flame-Carrier. [See also: Dalia Kazlauskienė; The Thunderer of the Sacred Oak; Laima; Juozas Vaitkevičius.] He is twenty-two years old, dark-haired, broad-shouldered, and in possession of a lantern that contains sacred fire transferred directly from the grove at Samogitia — a fire he did not light and cannot extinguish. He was tending one of the daughter-fires at a secondary grove when the Thunderer spoke through it and gave him instructions. He has been walking between grove-sites since, carrying the lantern and the message and the considerable personal burden of being the mortal a geological Fae power has identified as its preferred terrestrial messenger. He did not ask for this. The fire asked him. He answers because a Lithuanian peasant who has received a direct communication from Dundulis and declined to act upon it is navigating spiritual territory that has no good historical precedents. He is terrified, devout, and competent in ways he has not yet discovered he is competent.¹
¹He does not appear to have been told how long this assignment lasts.
title: Vytautas the Flame-Carrier

keys: Andrei Stormwalker , the Stormwalker , the Stormwalker, , the Stormwalker. ,
value:
Andrei Stormwalker. [See also: The Schoolmaster; Gheorghe the Doorkeeper; The Librarian.] He presents as a tall, red-haired man in white robes that have seen considerable weather, carrying an iron axe and a stone talisman inscribed with nine letters no living scholar has yet identified. These are the identifying marks of a Scholomance graduate; he wears them without concealment because the ability to ride storms between one city and the next makes concealment somewhat redundant. He graduated from the Scholomance thirty years ago and has been traveling the Balkans since, moving between the updraughts of thunderheads with a proficiency that has given him the Apuseni name Zgrimțieș and the German-speaking Saxon designation Wettermacher — Weather-Maker. He tests hospitality at every village he passes through: generous hosts receive fair weather for their crops; those who turn him away find hail two days later, which he considers a perfectly reasonable arrangement. He is the Scholomance's most visible graduate and its most effective advertisement.¹
¹Villagers who have hosted him describe him as courteous and appreciative of good bread. Villagers who have not are advised not to describe the specific weather conditions that followed, as the description itself constitutes admission.
title: Andrei Stormwalker

keys: The Lamia of Mundaka , La Lamia , the Lady of the Bridge ,
value:
The Lamia of Mundaka. [See also: Mari; Sugaar; Aitor Etxeberria; Maddi.] She presents as a woman of extraordinary beauty from the waist up, with a fish's tail or bird's feet below — the form shifts depending on whether she occupies water or rock — and an expression of such serene self-satisfaction that the transition appears not to concern her. She is one of the lamiak: river-fairies of the Basque country, known to inhabit bridges and water-crossings, possessed of golden hair requiring constant attention. She is vain, capricious, and genuinely fond of mortal company in the way that a person of naturally good humor finds an entertaining companion pleasant, with the understanding that pleasant is not the same as consequential. She is the Mari Court's most accessible intermediary because she is the only member who communicates willingly with mortals — Mari herself is geological and Sugaar is weather — and she finds them amusing in a way both of the above do not. She relays Mari's weather-judgments to the Basque villages at the estuary and takes offerings in return.¹
¹A golden comb is the most reliable tribute. She combs her hair on riverside rocks at dusk and can be approached there by those with legitimate business, provided they do not remark upon the bird-feet.
title: The Lamia of Mundaka

keys: Maître Lefebvre , Maître Lefebvre, , Maître Lefebvre. ,
value:
Maître Lefebvre. [See also: The Chief Notary; Mélusine; Maître Corveau; Raymond of Poitou.] He is a notary of Poitiers, sixty years old, bald, meticulous, and notable among the humans involved in Mélusine Court operations for having arrived there through accident rather than arrangement. Twenty years ago, descending to his own cellar to retrieve a mislaid document, he stepped through a door that opened onto the Grimoire Vault. He read what was put in front of him. He signed what required signing. He proved considerably better at the work than anyone had expected, including himself. He has been the Mélusine Court's mortal-world legal representative since: serving papers in human courts on the court's behalf, filing claims in French civil proceedings, and ensuring the court's contractual interests are represented wherever mortal law intersects with fairy obligation. He has never broken a contract in his life, which the court considers his most essential qualification.¹
¹His Poitiers practice still operates on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays. On the other days the clerks tell clients he is occupied with a longstanding private matter. This has been true for twenty years.
title: Maître Lefebvre

keys: Mrs. Lennox , Mrs. Lennox, , Mrs. Lennox. ,
value:
Mrs. Lennox. [See also: Gilbert Norrell; John Childermass; Hurtfew Abbey.] She is approximately sixty years old, sharp-featured, sharp-eyed, and possessed of the particular authority that accumulates in a person who has managed a household containing significant secrets for several decades. She has been Norrell's housekeeper at Hurtfew Abbey since long before Norrell became the most famous man in England, and she intends to remain so regardless of what England becomes. She knows every room, every locked door, every book Norrell believes is hidden from his staff, and every occasion on which magic has been performed in a room she subsequently entered to clean — she identifies these by smell, a faint scent of lightning she cannot account for and does not attempt to. She is not magical. She insists upon this. She is, rather, a woman who has spent sufficient years in proximity to magic that certain of its characteristics have become available to her as ordinary information. She will not give you access to Norrell without a reason that satisfies her, which is a considerably higher bar than satisfying Norrell himself.¹
¹The question of whether Mrs. Lennox reads Norrell's correspondence is one that no scholar has yet resolved. The question of whether she reads it before or after delivery is not, apparently, a question that has been asked.
title: Mrs. Lennox

keys: The Grey-Haired Dancer , the oldest captive of Lost-hope ,
value:
The Grey-Haired Dancer. [See also: The Gentleman with the Thistle-down Hair; Stephen Black; Lady Pole; Arabella Strange.] He is a mortal man of approximately seventy, and he has been dancing at Lost-hope's nightly balls since approximately the turn of the century, or perhaps earlier — he has lost the precise count along with his name. His feet bleed through his shoes. He cannot stop. The enchantment that binds him is older than most of the more recent captives' enchantments, and it has had its full effect: his eyes are present but the person behind them is not entirely so, worn to a kind of transparency by the decades. He is the first figure a visitor to Lost-hope encounters, which the Gentleman intends as hospitality and which functions as warning. Between dance-steps he is sometimes able to whisper fragments — a name, a direction, a caution — though the enchantment garbles much of what he attempts to say.¹
¹He desperately wants to rest. This desire is, at this point, so thoroughly woven into his enchantment that he may not be entirely separable from it. Whether breaking the enchantment would restore the man, or only stop the dancing, is a question no one has yet had the opportunity to test.
title: The Grey-Haired Dancer

keys: The Lantern-Carrier , the guide of the mirror roads ,
value:
The Lantern-Carrier. [See also: John Uskglass; The King's Roads.] It appears at the edge of vision: a child-sized figure in the grey twilight between mirrors, carrying a lantern that burns cold blue rather than warm amber. It does not speak. It does not linger once noticed. It indicates — by moving, by pausing, by raising the lantern toward one path and drawing it away from another — which routes on the King's Roads are safe and which are not, and then it is somewhere else. Travelers who follow its light arrive at their destination. Travelers who ignore it become lost, which on the King's Roads is a condition with significantly more variety to it than lost in the ordinary sense. Whether the Lantern-Carrier serves the Raven King, the Roads themselves, or some other principle has not been established.¹
¹Some scholars of the King's Roads have proposed that the Lantern-Carrier is not a being but a function — a property of the Roads expressing itself as form when a traveler requires direction. This interpretation would not, in practice, change how one ought to behave upon seeing the blue light.
title: The Lantern-Carrier

keys: Gheorghe the Doorkeeper , Gheorghe , Gheorghe, , Gheorghe. ,
value:
Gheorghe the Doorkeeper. [See also: The Schoolmaster; Andrei Stormwalker; The Librarian.] He sits cross-legged at the entrance to the cave that leads to the Scholomance, wearing white robes that have been grey with dust for as long as anyone has been counting. He is red-haired, hollow-eyed, and possessed of the particular patience of a man who has been sitting in the same location for so long that the concept of impatience has become theoretical. He is the Weathermaker of his graduating class — the student the Scholomance retained as the lord's tithe — and his permanent assignment is to receive those who seek the school and determine whether they merit admission. His test is three questions about the nature of weather: origin, behavior, and consequence. Those who answer correctly find the cave mouth opening before them. Those who answer incorrectly find it gone, and themselves standing before a perfectly ordinary rockface. He is polite, thorough, and incapable of helping the situation.¹
¹He has never left since the day of his graduation. He does not know whether the others of his class are still alive. He does not ask because the answer, either way, would be difficult.
title: Gheorghe the Doorkeeper

keys: Brigid Ní Fhlaithbheartaigh , the Galway fairy doctor ,
value:
Brigid Ní Fhlaithbheartaigh. [See also: Finvarra; Oonagh; Cián of the Silver Tongue.] She is a tall, weathered woman of forty from County Galway, and she has been able to see through glamour since the age of seven, when she was taken under the hill at Knockmaa for three days and returned with this capacity, a persistent knowledge of the Sídhe's internal politics, and an impatience with fools that has only increased since. She serves as the primary intermediary between the mortal population of County Galway and the Hollow Hills — a role she did not choose and does not resent, having concluded that someone must do it and she is demonstrably better equipped than the alternatives. She carries a hazel wand cut on Beltane morning. She can tell immediately whether a visitor carries fairy blood, fairy debt, or the specific quality of unwanted fairy attention that arrives when a mortal has been found interesting by a court that should not know they exist.¹
¹She has no formal title. The county calls her the fairy-doctor. The Sídhe call her by a title in Irish that translates approximately as 'the inconvenient one.'
title: Brigid Ní Fhlaithbheartaigh

keys: Magister Albrecht Krone , Magister Krone , Magister Krone, , Magister Krone. ,
value:
Magister Albrecht Krone. [See also: The One-Eyed Lord; Frau Holle; Eiríkr the Walker.] He is a natural philosopher from Göttingen, fifty-three years old, who climbed the Brocken in the summer of 1816 to observe the atmospheric optical phenomenon called the Brocken Spectre — a shadow projected in rainbow rings by fog and sun — and discovered that what he was observing was not optical and had been watching him for some time before he noticed it. He has been at the summit shepherd's hut since. He has filled six notebooks with observations of Æsir Court Fae activity: identification notes for forty-seven distinct figures and behavioral catalogues constituting the most systematic non-magical documentation of a Fae court in European history. He has no magical ability whatsoever and has survived six months on the Brocken through iron charms, meticulous observational protocols, and what he suspects is the Fae's genuine amusement at being studied.¹
¹His notebooks are not yet published. He is unsure any journal would accept them. He is also unsure he can leave the mountain — not because anything is preventing him, but because he has not finished.
title: Magister Albrecht Krone

keys: Yannick le Braz , Yannick , Yannick, , Yannick. ,
value:
Yannick le Braz. [See also: Mélusine; The Chief Notary.] He is a fisherman's son from Brittany, born in a caul, which in local estimation established the terms of his life before he had any say in the matter. He is lean, dark-haired, perpetually damp from the coastal fog, and he loiters among the megaliths at Carnac with the comfortable authority of a man who knows the neighborhood considerably better than the visiting scholars who arrive with notebooks and measuring equipment. The korrigans — the Breton fairy folk who inhabit the megalithic landscape — will speak to him when they will not speak to anyone else. He does not know why this is. He suspects it is the caul, the sea-proximity, and several generations of Breton fishermen leaving the right offerings at the right stones. He serves as translator between the korrigans and the growing number of Romantic scholars arriving from Paris, and he charges each of them a fee he considers reasonable and they consider impertinent.¹
¹He charges the korrigans as well, in cream and honey. They find this fair. The korrigans' capacity to find things fair is closely connected to the specificity of the tribute offered, which is why Yannick is the one conducting the negotiations.
title: Yannick le Braz

keys: Elena Barbu , Elena Barbu, , Elena Barbu. ,
value:
Elena Barbu. [See also: The Schoolmaster; Andrei Stormwalker.] She is a descântătoare — a Romanian enchantress — of approximately fifty, broad-shouldered and weathered, who maintains through nightly descântece the boundary between the living and the dead at Lake Snagov. This is exacting work under ordinary circumstances. Circumstances at Lake Snagov in 1817 are not ordinary. The lake's proximity to Nav — the Slavic dead-world — means corpses have been surfacing that were committed to the water long ago, and the strigoi population in the surrounding forest has tripled since magic's return, considerably increasing the boundary's load-bearing requirements. She is exhausted. She is paid what the village can afford, which is not enough. She is also the only thing standing between the local population and a full-scale incursion from the dead side of the water, which gives her very little room to leave. She will accept assistance from almost any practitioner willing to offer it.¹
¹She will extend trust after a practitioner has demonstrated they can recite at least one descântec without mispronouncing it. She considers this the minimum available competence test, and it has already reduced the field considerably.
title: Elena Barbu

keys: Aitor Etxeberria , Aitor , Aitor, , Aitor. ,
value:
Aitor Etxeberria. [See also: Mari; Sugaar; The Lamia of Mundaka; Maddi.] He is twenty years old and has been hearing Mari's voice from the cave at Zugarramurdi since he was fifteen. He has not entered. His grandmother, a sorgin who taught him most of what he knows about Mari's protocols, forbade him from entering, saying that when She wanted him She would come and collect him. He lingers at the cave mouth instead, lighting candles in the stream called Infernuko Erreka and relaying messages between the Mari Court and the Basque villagers who still observe the old ways. He is nervous, thorough, and in the process of reconciling his grandmother's practical instructions about offerings and required etiquette with the rather larger scale of what is currently manifesting on the mountain. His grandmother's instructions did not cover the situation where the mountain changes the weather of a significant portion of Europe as a routine matter. He is improvising with care.¹
¹He prays somewhat ecumenically, both to Mari and to the Virgin Mary, having decided that the theological question of whether they are related is above his competence and that thoroughness is the safer approach.
title: Aitor Etxeberria

keys: Dalia Kazlauskienė , Dalia , Dalia, , Dalia. ,
value:
Dalia Kazlauskienė. [See also: Vytautas the Flame-Carrier; The Thunderer of the Sacred Oak; Laima; Juozas Vaitkevičius.] She is a grandmother of approximately seventy who was walking in the forest outside her village one morning last year when she found a fire burning in a clearing her grandmother had described in stories four generations old: the sacred clearing of the Romuva grove, not tended for four hundred years, suddenly lit again with a fire no one had set. She sat down. She fed it oak branches. She has been there since. She does not claim to be a priestess; the tradition has no living priesthood and she is not certain she would qualify if it did. She says only that the fire needed someone and she was there. Lithuanian scholars, Russian officials, and representatives of at least three Fae courts have arrived at the clearing since, and Dalia has greeted all of them with the same hospitality: sit, warm yourself by the fire, and show proper respect.¹
¹She was a schoolteacher's widow, previously. She has remarked that managing the Romuva revival is considerably more demanding than managing a schoolteacher's household, but that the skills transfer.
title: Dalia Kazlauskienė

keys: Kapitajn Søren Madsen , Captain Madsen , Captain Madsen, , Captain Madsen. ,
value:
Kapitajn Søren Madsen. [See also: The One-Eyed Lord; Frau Holle.] He is a Danish naval officer of forty-two stationed at Kronborg Castle in Elsinore, and he has been filing formally worded reports of maritime anomalies in the strait for approximately eighteen months with decreasing expectation that anyone is reading them. Ships have been becalmed in storms and driven by gales under clear skies in the same week. A vibration like a very slow bell has been felt through the castle's foundations on three separate occasions with no corresponding seismic event. Sonar-like frequencies have been detected in the water below the castle at intervals that correspond to no known shipping schedule. His superiors have suggested, with varying degrees of tactfulness, that he reduce his aquavit consumption. He is entirely sober, a condition he now maintains specifically because the logs need to be legible. He keeps a private logbook of supernatural maritime incidents separate from the official reports, in the event that a practitioner with relevant knowledge one day arrives at Kronborg.¹
¹He has been told that the power sleeping beneath Kronborg is not a metaphor. He had already concluded this. He is trying to determine what it is.
title: Kapitajn Søren Madsen

keys: Kyriakos Papadimitriou , Captain Papadimitriou , Captain Papadimitriou, , Captain Papadimitriou. ,
value:
Kyriakos Papadimitriou. [See also: The Grey-Eyed Counselor; The Huntress; The Lord of the Shining Mountain.] He is a klepht of forty — a Greek bandit-patriot operating in the Pindus Mountains against Ottoman administration — and he has been receiving supernatural assistance for approximately two years without having asked for it. The neraides of the mountain valleys have begun guiding his band through passes that Ottoman patrols cannot navigate, warning of ambushes, and teaching his men songs that affect the weather with a precision the mountain peasants recognize and find, to his private relief, entirely comprehensible. He is a freedom fighter by conviction, a magical practitioner by accident, and a captain by temperament. He is deeply suspicious of foreigners — an attitude the Pindus has historically endorsed — but he is a practical man, and practitioners with useful knowledge are arriving on the mainland with increasing frequency as European magical developments draw attention to Constantinople.¹
¹He is affiliated with the Filiki Eteria — the Greek nationalist society whose revolution will come in 1821. He does not know when. He suspects it is soon. The neraides will not tell him.
title: Kyriakos Papadimitriou

keys: The Pale Butler , the pale manservant of Lost-hope ,
value:
The Pale Butler. [See also: The Gentleman with the Thistle-down Hair; Stephen Black; Lady Pole; The Grey-Haired Dancer.] He presents as a tall figure whose skin has the faintly translucent quality of candle wax viewed in the light it was made to accompany — a quality one notices first as unusual pallor and understands only gradually as an absence: not paleness, but the translucency of a face that has had something removed from it. He manages the practical arrangements of Lost-hope's nightly balls: greeting arrivals at the threshold, directing them to the floor, ensuring the evening proceeds according to the Gentleman's wishes and that no one departs before the Gentleman has concluded his entertainment. He may once have been mortal — he retains a faint residual quality of servitude that suggests enchantment rather than birth. He can be induced to speak with stories from the mortal world, which he receives with an expression not quite hunger and not quite grief.¹
¹Those who have successfully bribed him with sufficient quality information have received, in return, intelligence of their own — the ball's schedule, the exits, which of the Gentleman's current distractions might be exploited. He does not refuse the Gentleman. He is, however, grateful.
title: The Pale Butler

keys: Cián of the Silver Tongue , the Silver-Tongued Emissary ,
value:
Cián of the Silver Tongue. [See also: Finvarra; Oonagh; Brigid Ní Fhlaithbheartaigh.] He presents as a young man with hair that resolves, depending on the light, as either red or gold or both simultaneously, dressed in clothing that shifts between periods — sometimes Tudor, sometimes medieval, sometimes a style for which the relevant era has not yet arrived. He is one of Finvarra's lesser courtiers, assigned to receive visitors, determine whether they merit an audience with the High King, and ensure the Hollow Hills' interests are assessed before any mortal concern is acknowledged as worthy of consideration. He is charming, apparently delighted to meet you, and absolutely untrustworthy — not because he lies (Fae cannot lie) but because every true thing he tells you has been selected specifically for the effect it will produce rather than the information it will convey. He speaks every language in the Celtic world and several that no living human uses.¹
¹He was given the epithet 'of the Silver Tongue' by someone who meant it as a warning. He received it as a compliment. This distinction describes him adequately.
title: Cián of the Silver Tongue

keys: Sigríður Kolsdóttir , the dead völva at the gate ,
value:
Sigríður Kolsdóttir. [See also: The One-Eyed Lord; Huginn, Muninn; Eiríkr the Walker; Frau Holle.] She is a völva — a seeress and practitioner of seiðr — who died several centuries ago and was subsequently taken into the Æsir Court's service in a capacity that is neither quite ghosthood nor quite resurrection, but something for which the older traditions have no formal category and the newer ones have no vocabulary. She guards the threshold between the mortal world and the Æsir's halls. She carries a seiðstafr of considerable age that hums at a frequency practitioners report as audible below comfortable hearing. She speaks Old Norse of her era, which modern Icelandic scholars find almost comprehensible on a good day. She tests visitors with riddles: those who answer well are admitted, those who answer poorly are sent away with a persistent headache, and those who answer with contempt are struck by lightning in a manner she appears to find regrettable rather than pleasurable.¹
¹She was mortal, once, and she knows it. She carries this knowledge the way a very old practitioner carries their first failure: precisely, without forgetting, and without letting it affect the current work.
title: Sigríður Kolsdóttir

keys: The Echo-Child , the Tuonela river child ,
value:
The Echo-Child. [See also: The Singing Void; Ahti; Vellamo; Tapio.] It stands on the far bank of the dark river separating the mortal world from Tuonela, and it looks like a child of approximately seven, except for its eyes, which are black from lid to lid, and its voice, which arrives a half-second before its lips move — a quality one notices early and does not thereafter stop noticing. It does not guide visitors. It does not set riddles or demand tribute or assess worthiness in any conventional sense. It reflects. It repeats your words back to you with slight alterations — word substitutions, emphasis shifts, small inversions of subject and object — that reveal, with unanticipated precision, the hidden intentions behind what you actually said. Those who hear what it shows them about themselves and continue anyway find the river shallow enough to wade. Those who find the reflection intolerable are free to leave; the return path remains open.¹
¹Whether the Echo-Child is aware it performs this function, or whether it simply repeats what it hears and the alteration is a property of acoustic conditions at that bank of the river, remains one of the Väki Court's more productive open questions.
title: The Echo-Child

keys: Dedushka Petr , the Buyan door-keeper ,
value:
Dedushka Petr. [See also: The Thunder-Voiced; The Weaver of Fate; The Cattle-Lord of the Underworld; The Wandering Domovoi.] He presents as a very small, very old man in a red shirt, without shoes, with an expression suggesting he has been waiting considerably longer than he finds appropriate and has formed opinions about everyone who has yet arrived. He is a domovoi — a household spirit — assigned, or having appointed himself, to receive visitors at the shores of Buyan, the Parliament of Domains' court seat. He is grumpy, opinionated, and obsessed with domestic etiquette in its Russian form, which is to say he wants to know whether you have left proper offerings for your own household domovoi before you have the presumption to stand at the Parliament's threshold. He can be mollified with bread and salt, and with genuine courtesy toward the spirits of one's own home — genuine being the operative term, as household spirits communicate with each other and will know.¹
¹His opinion of the One-Eyed Lord has not been documented. His expression when the subject arises has been.
title: Dedushka Petr

keys: Žaltvykslė , the sacred grove serpent ,
value:
Žaltvykslė. [See also: Dalia Kazlauskienė; The Thunderer of the Sacred Oak; Laima; Vytautas the Flame-Carrier.] She is a žaltys — a sacred grass snake of the Lithuanian old religion — of unusual size, approximately ten feet in length, and unmistakable intelligence. She coils at the base of the central oak of the sacred Romuva grove, a position she appears to have occupied since before the grove fell into disuse and resumed with apparent patience since the fire rekindled. She does not speak in words; her movements convey meaning with a precision that those with any supernatural sensitivity read without difficulty, and those without occasionally learn through proximity and attention. A raised head indicates welcome. A turned body indicates leave. A hiss indicates offense, for which the appropriate response is to leave more quickly than the turned body suggested. She has been offered milk by Dalia Kazlauskienė every morning since the fire returned; she accepts it as her due, which it is.¹
¹In the old religion, a family that killed a žaltys brought catastrophe upon itself. A family that fed one brought prosperity. The Romuva Court does not consider this principle to have lapsed.
title: Žaltvykslė

keys: The Librarian , the shifting Scholomance librarian ,
value:
The Librarian. [See also: The Schoolmaster; Gheorghe the Doorkeeper; Andrei Stormwalker.] It presents as whatever form seems locally appropriate: a stooped scholar with ink-stained fingers when the light is adequate, a bat-winged figure when it is not, and on occasion only a pair of points of light between the bookshelves in the deep stacks, which one takes on faith are eyes. It maintains the Scholomance's underground library — the largest collection of operational magical texts in Eastern Europe, catalogued according to a system either perfectly logical or completely opaque depending on whether one has read the Schoolmaster's organizational notes, which are themselves in the library, catalogued according to the same system they explain. It does not converse. It communicates through citations: presenting relevant volumes, indicating passages, directing visitors to texts with a precision implying it has read and retained every item in the collection. It is, by some considerable margin, the most cheerful entity the Scholomance produces.¹
¹It has never refused to answer a question. It has occasionally answered questions that were not yet asked, which some visitors find efficient and others find unsettling.
title: The Librarian

keys: Hermes-Who-Was , Hermes , Mercury ,
value:
Hermes-Who-Was. [See also: The Lord of the Shining Mountain; The Grey-Eyed Counselor; The Huntress; The Smith.] He calls himself the Threshold-Crosser, which is unusual: most Fae do not volunteer names of any kind, and the fact of volunteering is itself information, the interpretation of which is left as an exercise. He presents as a young man of athletic build, wearing winged sandals that may or may not be literal depending on what he requires them to do, and possessing a smile of such easy mischief that it functions as a warning to anyone who recognizes it as one. He is the Olympian Court's designated representative for mortal affairs — not because he is its most powerful member but because he is the only Olympian who genuinely enjoys mortal company rather than merely tolerating it as an occasional necessity. He is approachable, interested, and the least trustworthy presence in any room he enters: not because he lies (Fae cannot lie) but because he delights in leading mortals to truths they were not prepared for, by routes they did not expect.¹
¹He will guide you through the caves below Olympus. He has never claimed he would warn you about what is inside.
title: Hermes-Who-Was

keys: Maddi , Maddi, , Maddi. ,
value:
Maddi. [See also: Mari; Sugaar; The Lamia of Mundaka; Aitor Etxeberria.] She is a young Basque woman who has served as Mari's voice to the mortal world since the age of thirteen, when Mari appeared to her on the slopes of Anboto as a red cow, spoke at some length in a language Maddi has described as 'not Euskara but understandable,' and departed without further instruction. Maddi has been relaying Mari's judgments to the villages below and carrying offerings back up the mountain since. She is not Fae; she is human, sorgin-born — born of a sorgin mother — and entirely aware of her own limitations. She does not understand most of what Mari tells her. She delivers it anyway, word-perfect, because accuracy in the service of a geological Fae power who values honesty with lethal totality is not so much a virtue as a condition of continued existence.¹
¹She is twenty-one years old. She has not told her mother how frightened she is because her mother is more frightened. She is managing both of them.
title: Maddi

keys: Maître Corveau , the junior fairy notary ,
value:
Maître Corveau. [See also: The Chief Notary; Mélusine; Maître Lefebvre; Raymond of Poitou.] He presents as a thin man in sixteenth-century clerk's clothing — a period that evidently struck the Mélusine Court as the apogee of notarial aesthetic, as several of its Fae settled on it independently — with fingers permanently ink-stained and a manner calibrated with surgical precision to be equally helpful and condescending. He handles initial consultations with mortal visitors to the Grimoire Vault: explaining the court's organizational system, providing standard contract templates, and ensuring that visitors understand, before any document is introduced, the vault's rules. No documents to be removed. No documents to be damaged. No documents to be read aloud without prior authorization. Words have power here; the last item is the one that matters. He is the Mélusine Court's front office — the face mortals encounter before they have fully determined what they have walked into.¹
¹He is junior notary in title only. He has been at this desk since before the Treaty of Westphalia. His opinion of mortal legal systems is not sought, which is fortunate.
title: Maître Corveau

keys: Mrs. Amelia Carstairs , Mrs. Carstairs , Mrs. Carstairs, , Mrs. Carstairs. ,
value:
Mrs. Amelia Carstairs. [See also: Gilbert Norrell; John Childermass; Brother Ezekiel Thorne; Dr. Margaret Buchanan.] She is a wealthy widow of forty-five who maintains a fashionable salon in Mayfair — one of those drawing rooms where the topics of conversation are just slightly more interesting than the guests realize until they are already in the middle of discussing them. She practices what Norrell's establishment declines to acknowledge as real magic: the working of spells through needlework, garden-craft, and household management, in the tradition Clarke established in *The Ladies of Grace Adieu*. She knows everyone of consequence in London's magical society, hears everything of significance that passes through it, and operates a quiet network of female practitioners across southern England that has survived several sustained Norrellite attempts to ignore it out of existence. She is warm, perceptive, socially impeccable, and considerably more dangerous than she appears.¹
¹Norrell has declined to comment on her practices on four recorded occasions, each time in terms suggesting he has thought about them more carefully than he would prefer to admit. Childermass has remarked privately that if Norrell spent as much time with Mrs. Carstairs as he does avoiding her, English magic would be in a considerably better position.
title: Mrs. Amelia Carstairs

keys: Monsieur Delacroix , Monsieur Delacroix, , Monsieur Delacroix. ,
value:
Monsieur Delacroix. [See also: Mélusine; The Chief Notary; Maître Corveau; Germaine de Staël.] He is a bookseller of fifty on the Quai des Grands-Augustins in Paris, operating from a narrow shop that smells of paper, damp, and the recent passage of important decisions. Before magic's return he sold antique books to scholars and collectors. Since magic's return he sells operational grimoires to anyone who can demonstrate they know what an operational grimoire is, which he considers a sensible minimum qualification. Business has never been better. He is connected to both the legitimate magical scholarly community in Paris and the criminal underworld that trades in forged and stolen texts, and he moves between these circles with the ease of a man who has long since concluded they are the same circle at different distances from the light. He has read enough Mélusine Court contracts to identify their trap-clauses on sight.¹
¹He is not a practitioner. He is, however, a reader — and in a world where magical texts are operational, the distinction is becoming increasingly difficult to maintain with confidence.
title: Monsieur Delacroix

keys: Doktor Sigismund Hartmann , Doktor Hartmann , Doktor Hartmann, , Doktor Hartmann. ,
value:
Doktor Sigismund Hartmann. [See also: Klemens von Metternich; Ludwig van Beethoven; Franz Schubert; Florica Lăutaru.] He is forty years old, secretary to the Imperial Commission for Extraordinary Phenomena — the Habsburg Empire's institutional attempt to process magic through administrative channels — and he produces, for official purposes, reports that minimize magical events. For private purposes, he maintains a separate journal documenting everything the Commission suppresses, which is considerably more interesting than anything in the official record. He is meticulous by temperament, privately terrified by circumstance, and secretly fascinated by everything the circumstance is producing. His official function is to reduce; his private compulsion is to record. The tension between these has produced, over two years of the Commission's existence, a hidden journal representing the most comprehensive surviving account of Fae activity across the Habsburg domains in the modern period — none of which his superiors have read.¹
¹He is looking for someone he can trust with the information. The Commission's culture of suppression is not conducive to identifying trustworthy recipients, as the trustworthy have mostly been quietly transferred to less interesting assignments.
title: Doktor Sigismund Hartmann

keys: Father Alexei Volkonsky , Father Volkonsky , Father Volkonsky, , Father Volkonsky. ,
value:
Father Alexei Volkonsky. [See also: Tsar Alexander I; The Thunder-Voiced; The Weaver of Fate; The Wandering Domovoi.] He is a monk of the Alexander Nevsky Lavra in St. Petersburg, sixty years old, who practices the hesychast tradition of contemplative prayer — the Orthodox ascetic discipline of achieved inner stillness — and has discovered that deep contemplative states now produce, quite reliably, genuine supernatural perception. He can see the domovoi in households he visits, perceive the shape of the vodyanoy's presence in the Neva, and sense the structural arrangement of Fae power as an experienced physician senses the arrangement of a disease not yet named. The Church hierarchy is uncomfortable with him. His perceptions are too specific to dismiss as mystical metaphor, too practical to endorse as mystical experience, and too consistently accurate to classify as pathology. He operates at Russia's dvoeverie intersection — where Orthodox Christianity and awakened Slavic spirits coexist without formal reconciliation — with the patience of a man who has spent forty years learning to be still, and found that stillness shows you everything.¹
¹He has attempted to report his perceptions through official Church channels. The responses have been lengthy, polite, and evasive. He does not find this surprising.
title: Father Alexei Volkonsky

keys: Elif Hatun , Elif Hatun, , Elif Hatun. ,
value:
Elif Hatun. [See also: Sultan Mahmud II; Yusuf Efendi; Khalid ibn Rashid.] She is a Turkish woman of fifty who maintains an apothecary in the Grand Bazaar of Constantinople, in a corner where the ceiling is low and the smell of dried herbs requires several minutes to fully catalogue. She practises Ottoman herbal medicine and Sufi mystical theory in a combination that satisfies neither orthodox medicine nor orthodox theology entirely, which she has concluded is the position from which the most useful work gets done. She can identify fairy-touched goods by handling them, diagnose the Evil Eye across at least four cultural traditions simultaneously, and prepare protective amulets drawing from Greek, Turkish, Arabic, and Balkan sources in proportions suited to the specific complaint. No other city in Europe sits at the intersection of so many traditions, and no other practitioner in that city has been doing this work long enough to know which combinations help and which cancel each other out.¹
¹She does not advertise. She does not need to. The Grand Bazaar has functioned as Constantinople's information exchange for several centuries, and those who need her are directed there before they have fully formed the need.
title: Elif Hatun

keys: Padre Tommaso Sforza , Padre Sforza , Padre Sforza, , Padre Sforza. ,
value:
Padre Tommaso Sforza. [See also: Pope Pius VII; Cardinal Consalvi; Ferdinand VII.] He is a Jesuit priest of sixty assigned to the Vatican's Archivio Segreto, and he has spent the past year in a state of controlled moral anguish that has not diminished. He has been cross-referencing Inquisition trial transcripts against genuine supernatural incident reports from the same periods, and he has discovered, with the meticulous precision of a man trained in scholarly method, that the Inquisition knew. Certain cardinals, across three centuries of documented record, always knew that magic was real. They suppressed the evidence with the same institutional thoroughness they applied to theological heterodoxy, and for reasons the transcripts reveal only obliquely. He is devout, methodical, and sitting on the most extensive collection of operational magical intelligence in Europe — a collection no practitioner has ever accessed, because no practitioner has had cause to review sixteenth-century trial records for systematic evidence of institutional knowledge.¹
¹He will share what he has found with someone he believes will use it for good purposes. He is aware that this is a sentence that has been said before, by men in his position, with results he has read in those same records.
title: Padre Tommaso Sforza

keys: Professor Heinrich Brandt , Professor Brandt , Professor Brandt, , Professor Brandt. ,
value:
Professor Heinrich Brandt. [See also: Jacob Grimm; Wilhelm Grimm; E.T.A. Hoffmann; Klemens von Metternich.] He is thirty-two years old and the University of Berlin's first holder of the newly created Chair of Extraordinary Natural Philosophy — a position established at the Prussian War Ministry's suggestion and not, he has begun to suspect, with entirely academic objectives. He is brilliant, systematic, and deeply frustrated by magic's refusal to behave according to predictable principles. He has been corresponding with the Brothers Grimm in Kassel about folkloric records of Fae behavior, with Icelandic rune-scholars about Germanic magical theory, and with the editors of three scientific journals about whether any of them will accept a paper on the empirical basis of supernatural phenomena. He is attempting to build a unified theoretical framework for magical mechanics — a project that would require magic to be consistent, which it is not, and to be independent of the courts that govern it, which it is also not — and he pursues this with the admirable confidence of a man who has not yet fully grasped what he is dealing with.¹
¹The Fae courts' response to systematic academic study is, historically, amused condescension. In Prussia, it has also been bureaucratic. The combination is, practitioners agree, more dangerous than either alone.
title: Professor Heinrich Brandt

keys: Dr. Margaret Buchanan , Dr. Buchanan , Dr. Buchanan, , Dr. Buchanan. ,
value:
Dr. Margaret Buchanan. [See also: Walter Scott; James Hogg; Mrs. Amelia Carstairs.] She is a physician of forty at the Royal Infirmary of Edinburgh, possessed of second sight inherited from a grandmother in the Highland tradition and exercised with the clinical precision of an Edinburgh-trained physician. She diagnoses fairy-struck patients as she diagnoses any patients, with observation, differential analysis, and an awareness of what the evidence indicates regardless of how awkward the indication. She prescribes iron supplements and rowan-berry tinctures alongside conventional treatments, presenting these with the same matter-of-fact authority as any other medical recommendation. She is respected by her medical colleagues and closeted about her Sight among them — not from fear but from pragmatism, Edinburgh's medical establishment being the most accomplished in Britain and requiring considerable persuasion before acknowledging information that did not come from a laboratory. She is quietly desperate for colleagues who understand both halves of her working world.¹
¹She has been corresponding anonymously with Mrs. Carstairs in London for eighteen months. Mrs. Carstairs knows who she is. Dr. Buchanan knows Mrs. Carstairs knows. The correspondence continues anonymously by mutual, unspoken agreement.
title: Dr. Margaret Buchanan

keys: Khalid ibn Rashid , Khalid ibn Rashid, , Khalid ibn Rashid. ,
value:
Khalid ibn Rashid. [See also: The Ifrit of the Pillars; Zahra bint Yusuf; Elif Hatun; Yusuf Efendi.] He is a practitioner from the Levant in his sixties — a sahir, a sorcerer, though he prefers the neutral term 'negotiator' — who maintained Djinn relationships across three generations through the extraordinarily careful theological camouflage of weaving the old alliances into Sufi frameworks that satisfied both the orthodoxy and the Djinn, barely, simultaneously. He is based in Damascus but travels regularly to Constantinople, where Elif Hatun's apothecary serves as a discreet meeting point. He is the mortal world's most experienced Djinn negotiator — the only person in the eastern Mediterranean who can reliably conduct such a negotiation without getting someone killed. Since the Debts Come Due crisis, with families arriving daily carrying inherited pact-obligations from ancestors dead for three generations, he is also exhausted, and the exhaustion is audible beneath the patience.¹
¹He has sent word to Zahra bint Yusuf in Cairo requesting additional seal-capacity. She has replied that she is working as quickly as she can, which he knows, and which provides less reassurance than either of them would prefer.
title: Khalid ibn Rashid

keys: Zahra bint Yusuf , Zahra bint Yusuf, , Zahra bint Yusuf. ,
value:
Zahra bint Yusuf. [See also: The Ifrit of the Pillars; Khalid ibn Rashid.] She is twenty-three years old, working from a cramped room above a metalworker's shop in Cairo's Khan el-Khalili, surrounded by copper plates etched with geometric patterns that hum faintly when the hour is late and the air is still. She has inherited from her grandmother a collection of Solomonic seal-templates — inscriptions, sacred names, and material specifications for constraining a Djinn long enough to conduct a conversation without the conversation becoming something else. She is reconstructing them. Some are damaged. Some are deliberately fragmented, by ancestors who feared the knowledge they contained and divided it accordingly. She is the only person in North Africa who can reliably bind a Djinn — not permanently, those seals are lost — but long enough to establish terms. She is brilliant, thorough, and frightened in the specific way of someone who understands exactly how large the thing they are doing is while having no option not to do it.¹
¹The copper plates hum because the geometric patterns channel something she cannot yet fully characterize. She has recorded the frequency. She has not yet recorded what it is a response to, because determining that would require approaching the question more closely than she is yet prepared to do.
title: Zahra bint Yusuf

keys: Brother Ezekiel Thorne , Brother Thorne , Brother Thorne, , Brother Thorne. ,
value:
Brother Ezekiel Thorne. [See also: The Narcissist; Mrs. Amelia Carstairs.] He is a Dissenter minister of forty-five from Norwich, and he has been receiving visions since magic's return — clear, vivid, apparently divine communications of increasing frequency and specificity. He believes God is speaking to him. He is correct. The Narcissist is speaking to him, which amounts to the same thing from the Narcissist's perspective and constitutes a distinct category of situation from any other perspective available. The visions are genuine. The entity sending them is real, ancient, and completely convinced of its own creative sovereignty over all existence. Brother Thorne is frightened, earnest, and in possession of authentic prophetic experience that his Norwich congregation cannot contextualize, the Anglican establishment will not hear, and the London Dissenter community is politely suggesting he rest more. He does not yet know that what is happening to him has been happening to millions of people, across every Abrahamic tradition, for several thousand years.¹
¹He is staying in Shoreditch and attending Mrs. Carstairs's salon. He will receive there the first explanation that fits the evidence. It will not, in several respects, resemble the explanation he was hoping for.
title: Brother Ezekiel Thorne

keys: Florica Lăutaru , Florica , Florica, , Florica. ,
value:
Florica Lăutaru. [See also: Doktor Sigismund Hartmann; Zahra bint Yusuf.] She is a Romani drabardi — a seer — of approximately fifty who reads cards in the marketplaces of Bucharest, Budapest, and Vienna, traveling between them seasonally. Her sight is fractured: she perceives the present from multiple temporal angles simultaneously, seeing the same moment from its probable futures as well as its actual present, a quality that makes her card readings considerably more comprehensive than most clients expect. She has recently begun seeing something she has not seen in forty years of this work: all the temporal angles converging on a single point in spring 1817. She does not know what the convergence signifies. She knows, with the certainty of the craft, that it is consequential.¹
¹Doktor Hartmann's Commission attempted to register her as a practitioner of extraordinary arts. She gave them her grandmother's name, as per custom, and they have been unable to locate the appropriate form since. She considers this an efficient resolution. She is currently reading cards near the Naschmarkt and waiting for the convergence with the focused calm of someone who has learned that waiting well is not the same as waiting passively.
title: Florica Lăutaru

keys: Pétalo Heredia , Pétalo , Pétalo, , Pétalo. ,
value:
Pétalo Heredia. [See also: Ferdinand VII; Padre Diego Montero; Mari; Sugaar.] He is a Gitano of thirty-five in Andalusia, lean, dark-eyed, and possessed of the remarkable quality that every horse within a considerable radius of his person will cooperate with him to an extent the Inquisition's surveillance reports have been attempting to characterize for six months without finding adequate vocabulary. He has his mother's pact-making gift: small-alliance relationships with the horses, negotiated in a language of gesture and intent he cannot entirely articulate and has not needed to. The horses tell him things. They told him about the arrest of the bruja in Logroño before the news had reached Andalusia by road. They are currently telling him something about the Pyrenees — something involving Mari, weather patterns, and a sound they describe as 'the mountain breathing wrong' — that he cannot yet interpret. He operates under constant Inquisition scrutiny, which has identified him as something without being able to file him under any available prosecution category.¹
¹His horse-intelligence network covers Spain more comprehensively than any courier system the government operates. This has not been reported to the government, which he considers the sensible approach.
title: Pétalo Heredia

keys: Captain James Fairfax , Captain Fairfax , Captain Fairfax, , Captain Fairfax. ,
value:
Captain James Fairfax.
