[See also: Jonathan Strange; Lord Wellington; Gilbert Norrell.] He is a Waterloo veteran of thirty-six stationed at Dover, assigned to a 'customs inspection' service whose official mandate involves the monitoring of goods crossing the Channel and whose actual mandate involves the monitoring of everything else that crosses it. He carries iron-tipped ammunition, a rowan-wood baton, and a copy of Strange's *History and Practice of English Magic* with certain passages underlined in the manner of a man who has marked them because they describe things he has encountered. He is competent, deliberate, and profoundly uncomfortable with his assignment — not because he cannot execute it but because no one above him has yet been willing to explain what Britain's policy actually is toward the supernatural, making his discretionary judgments at the border considerably more consequential than he signed up for.¹
¹He expects honest answers. He can tell when he does not receive them, a capacity he attributes to Waterloo, where the consequences of believing optimistic reports taught him to read the information underneath the report.
title: Captain James Fairfax

keys: Sergeant Émile Garnier , Sergeant Garnier , Sergeant Garnier, , Sergeant Garnier. ,
value:
Sergeant Émile Garnier. [See also: Mélusine; The Chief Notary; Monsieur Delacroix.] He is a rural gendarme of forty-eight stationed at a border post — Bayonne on the Basque frontier, or Calais on the Channel, depending on the player's approach — a man of middle years, considerable experience of human nature, and a new set of standing orders regarding 'les phénomènes extraordinaires' that he has read three times and understood not at all. The pamphlet that accompanied them is, if anything, less clear. He is pragmatic in the manner of a man who has spent twenty years managing what people actually do rather than what they are supposed to do, and he has extended this pragmatism to the supernatural: he will wave you through if you appear manageable and detain you if you appear interesting. He is more afraid of the paperwork than of the phenomena, which is not an attitude he has arrived at casually.¹
¹He has been told that 'extraordinary phenomena' may include 'travelling practitioners of traditional arts.' He has filed this alongside his standing orders on smuggling and applied roughly equivalent judgment to both.
title: Sergeant Émile Garnier

keys: Herr Franz Gruber , Herr Gruber , Herr Gruber, , Herr Gruber. ,
value:
Herr Franz Gruber. [See also: Klemens von Metternich; Doktor Sigismund Hartmann; Ludwig van Beethoven.] He is a Habsburg customs inspector stationed at the Semmering Pass or the Brenner, depending on the player's approach, a man of forty-one with an obsequious manner, a suspicious mind, and a checklist of 'indicators of extraordinary activity' prepared by Metternich's Staatspolizei. He understands approximately a third of the indicators. The remainder contain terminology he has been unable to source in any dictionary available to him at his post, and his superiors, when consulted, have declined to clarify. He confiscates anything that looks magical and files a report that is forwarded to Vienna and, as far as he can determine, read by no one. He is secretly terrified of what he has been asked to monitor, which he addresses by monitoring it with extreme procedural thoroughness, on the theory that a man who follows the form correctly cannot be blamed for what the form fails to anticipate.¹
¹He has confiscated seventeen items in the past six months that he cannot identify, categorize, or safely return. They are in a locked box in the customs house that occasionally hums. He has not yet filed a report on the humming.
title: Herr Franz Gruber

keys: Marfa Ivanovna , Marfa Ivanovna, , Marfa Ivanovna. ,
value:
Marfa Ivanovna. [See also: The Thunder-Voiced; The Weaver of Fate; The Wandering Domovoi; Father Alexei Volkonsky.] She is approximately seventy years old, a znakharka — a folk healer — in a border village, and she is not an official of any government or institution of any kind. She is simply the person the village defers to, because she has been practicing zagovory — verbal healing charms — for fifty years, she maintains the village's ongoing relationship with its domovoi through daily offerings and respectful acknowledgment, and she can determine, within a few minutes of a traveler's arrival, whether that traveler carries fairy taint, an unreckoned pact-debt, or something worse. The actual customs officials at the nearby post defer to her judgment without admitting it publicly, a practice she finds sensible and they find easier than explaining. She will feed visitors, ask about their grandmothers' magical practices, and decide whether they are safe to let through.¹
¹She has released some travelers and turned others back. The criteria are not publicly documented. Those who have been welcomed report that warmth and a willingness to speak honestly about one's own grandmother's craft seem to help.
title: Marfa Ivanovna

keys: Leutnant Karl von Steinberg , Leutnant von Steinberg , Leutnant von Steinberg, , Leutnant von Steinberg. ,
value:
Leutnant Karl von Steinberg. [See also: Professor Heinrich Brandt; Klemens von Metternich; Jacob Grimm; Wilhelm Grimm.] He is twenty-four years old and has been assigned to the Abteilung für Außergewöhnliche Aufklärung — the Prussian Department for Extraordinary Reconnaissance — since its establishment six months ago. He is clean-shaven, precise, carrying a leather case of instruments designed by Professor Brandt's department to detect magical activity. None of the instruments work reliably. He has filed three reports to this effect. The department has instructed him to continue using them while improvements are developed. He will measure you. He will categorize you according to the department's classification system, which has twenty-four categories and leaves approximately forty percent of observed phenomena in a category labeled simply 'further study required.' He is not hostile to practitioners; he is relentlessly procedural, which practitioners occasionally find more difficult to manage than hostility.¹
¹He is privately convinced that the instruments are measuring something real and that the problem is the measurement units, not the phenomena. He has not yet put this in a report.
title: Leutnant Karl von Steinberg

keys: Padre Diego Montero , Padre Montero , Padre Montero, , Padre Montero. ,
value:
Padre Diego Montero. [See also: Ferdinand VII; Pétalo Heredia; Mari; Sugaar.] He is a lay officer of the Holy Office of the Inquisition, fifty years old, stationed at the Pyrenean border with the task of identifying practitioners of brujería entering or leaving Spain. The Inquisition was restored by Ferdinand VII in 1814 and is active; Montero's authority is real and his power to detain considerable. He is devout, thoroughgoing, and privately troubled: a man who believes in his faith, follows his orders, and is increasingly uncertain that the two are pointing in the same direction. He is genuinely afraid of magic — a fear that, since the Logroño incident, is somewhat better calibrated than it was — and he applies this fear systematically, which is the most dangerous kind.¹
¹He was present at one hearing since the Logroño incident in which the accused performed genuine magic before the tribunal. He has filed the transcript and not spoken of it since. Those who have seen the transcript report that the final exchange contains a question he asked the accused that was not in the standard form.
title: Padre Diego Montero

keys: Yusuf Efendi , Yusuf Efendi, , Yusuf Efendi. ,
value:
Yusuf Efendi. [See also: Sultan Mahmud II; Elif Hatun; Khalid ibn Rashid.] He is a dragoman — an interpreter-diplomat — stationed at the Ottoman frontier, the Danube crossing or the Adriatic coast depending on the player's approach, a man of forty whose linguistic repertoire covers Turkish, Greek, French, Italian, and Arabic with sufficient fluency to conduct negotiations in all of them simultaneously if the parties require it. His function is to facilitate passage for European travelers and to assess, as instructed by recent correspondence from Constantinople, visitors with 'unusual capabilities.' His instructions are admirably specific about the need to assess and admirably vague about what to do with the results beyond filing them. He is cosmopolitan, educated, simultaneously welcoming and evaluating — a combination calibrated to be virtually indistinguishable from pure hospitality.¹
¹He is the Ottoman Empire's approach to magic made personable: engagement rather than suppression, filtered through existing frameworks, assessed by a practical intelligence that has noted the European governments' varied failures to process this phenomenon adequately and is attempting a different method.
title: Yusuf Efendi

keys: Fru Astrid Lindqvist , Fru Lindqvist , Fru Lindqvist, , Fru Lindqvist. ,
value:
Fru Astrid Lindqvist. [See also: The One-Eyed Lord; Frau Holle; Sigríður Kolsdóttir; Bjarni Jónsson.] She is a wise woman of sixty in the border village of Svinesund on the Norwegian-Swedish frontier, a klok gumma who has been practicing folk magic from her svarteboken since her mother taught her at the age of twelve. The black book is worn, specific, and hers; she does not lend it. Her charms work now with a directness exceeding anything she experienced before magic's return, which she has accepted without visible surprise and applied with methodical competence to the growing demand. She reads runes with an accuracy generating word-of-mouth inquiry from considerably further than Svinesund. She will tell you what she sees bluntly, because she finds the alternatives — tact, vagueness, comforting imprecision — more trouble than the bluntness causes. She charges a fair price, in silver or in labor, and considers the fair price an important point of principle: knowledge has cost her forty-eight years of practice and ought not be given away.¹
¹Her opinion of the Æsir Court has been described as 'respectful without being reverential,' which the practitioners who have observed her work consider the correct calibration.
title: Fru Astrid Lindqvist

keys: Brother Matteo , Brother Matteo, , Brother Matteo. ,
value:
Brother Matteo. [See also: Pope Pius VII; Cardinal Consalvi; Padre Tommaso Sforza.] He is a young Franciscan friar of twenty-eight who operates a pilgrim hospice at the border of the Papal States — a small stone building that smells of bread, olive oil, and the honest effort of a young man trying to do something good in a complicated world. He welcomes all travelers with genuine Franciscan hospitality, which regards the care of the stranger as a religious obligation rather than a social nicety. He is gentle, earnest, and increasingly troubled by what he has been observing since magic's return. Pilgrims are arriving with stories. Some of the stories describe things he cannot square with his theology. Some of the things the stories describe have subsequently occurred in his immediate vicinity. He does not know how to frame a question to his superiors that would not result in reassignment to a house with no border traffic, and so he has not yet framed it.¹
¹He asks every traveler who arrives whether they have experienced anything 'unusual' on the road. He has been asking for eight months. He has filled two notebooks with the answers. He has shown them to no one.
title: Brother Matteo

keys: Bjarni Jónsson , Bjarni Jónsson, , Bjarni Jónsson. ,
value:
Bjarni Jónsson. [See also: The One-Eyed Lord; Sigríður Kolsdóttir; Fru Astrid Lindqvist; Eiríkr the Walker.] He is an Icelandic scholar and manuscript trader of thirty-five who frequents Copenhagen and can arrange passage to Iceland for those with legitimate scholarly interest and sufficient funds — the latter being a qualification he assesses generously if the interest is genuine. He deals in galdrabækur — Icelandic magical manuscripts, grimoires of stave-magic and charm-work preserved in the island's tradition — and galdrastafir templates, the carved protective symbols the Æsir Court's Icelandic tradition has preserved in unusually complete form. Business is excellent. Iceland occupies a peculiar position in magical geography: it is the repository of the Æsir's lore in its most complete surviving form, preserved through the medieval Eddic tradition and through a working folk-magical practice that maintained operative function through centuries when the continental Germanic tradition was suppressed. Bjarni knows this and has priced accordingly.¹
¹He is lively, opportunistic, and genuinely knowledgeable, a combination that has served him well. He is also careful not to sell anything to anyone he believes will use it badly, a principle he applies with a discretion that surprises clients who have judged his enthusiasm for commerce as the whole of him.
title: Bjarni Jónsson

keys: Juozas Vaitkevičius , Juozas , Juozas, , Juozas. ,
value:
Juozas Vaitkevičius. [See also: Dalia Kazlauskienė; Vytautas the Flame-Carrier; The Thunderer of the Sacred Oak; Laima.] He is a Lithuanian schoolteacher of thirty-two who abandoned his post three months ago and has been walking from grove-site to grove-site across Samogitia since, following the rekindling fires. His boots are mud-stained. His notebook contains sketches of fire-patterns, the movements of sacred snakes at grove entrances, and the expressions on elderly women's faces when they hear the old songs again for the first time in their lives — expressions he has been attempting to capture with an educator's precision and finding that precision inadequate to the task. He is not a practitioner. He is not a priest. He is a witness: a man with the training to observe accurately and the instinct to write it down, present at the beginning of something he does not yet have language for.¹
¹He sent a letter to his school board explaining his absence and has not yet received a reply. He is not, at this time, thinking about it.
title: Juozas Vaitkevičius

keys: Hurtfew Abbey — The Missing Chapter , the missing Hurtfew chapter ,
value:
Hurtfew Abbey — The Missing Chapter. [See also: Gilbert Norrell, The King's Letters, John Uskglass.]
A page has appeared in one of Norrell's most guarded books — written in the King's Letters and describing a spell Norrell has never encountered. It was not there yesterday. He is simultaneously terrified and fascinated, which is for Mr. Norrell an unusually productive emotional state. Someone — or something — is editing his library from the inside. The page is genuine; he has verified it three times and will not verify it a fourth, because a fourth verification would confirm what the first three already confirmed.
¹ Norrell has not told Childermass. Childermass already knows.
title: Hurtfew Abbey — The Missing Chapter

keys: Lost-hope — The Ball That Won't End , the eternal ball at Lost-hope ,
value:
Lost-hope — The Ball That Won't End. [See also: The Gentleman with the Thistle-Down Hair, Lady Pole, Stephen Black, The King's Roads.]
The nightly ball at Lost-hope has run for three consecutive days without stopping. The enchanted dancers are collapsing. The Gentleman is delighted — he has found that exhaustion produces a particular quality of movement he has not previously observed. The enchantment is feeding on itself, growing beyond its original design, and the boundary between Lost-hope and the mortal world is thinning. Mirrors across England are showing ballroom reflections that do not match the rooms they hang in.
¹ The Gentleman considers the expansion an improvement. His awareness of consequences mortals would call catastrophic remains, as always, entirely theoretical.
title: Lost-hope — The Ball That Won't End

keys: The King's Roads — The Crossroads Collapse , the road collapse ,
value:
The King's Roads — The Crossroads Collapse. [See also: The Sídhe Court, The Mélusine Court, Faerie's Geography, Inter-Court Politics.]
The Sídhe roads and the Mélusine roads have overlapped at a previously stable intersection. Travelers entering from England emerge in Brittany. Travelers entering from a Paris catacomb find themselves in an Irish fairy fort. The intersection is expanding. Both courts blame the other. The Chief Notary has filed a formal complaint. Cián of the Silver Tongue delivered the Sídhe's response — courteous and, upon careful reading, legally meaningless.
¹ This is the physical manifestation of The Brittany Question. See Lore: Inter-Court Politics.
title: The King's Roads — The Crossroads Collapse

keys: The Scholomance — The Tenth Student , the tenth student affair ,
value:
The Scholomance — The Tenth Student. [See also: The Schoolmaster, Andrei Stormwalker, Gheorghe the Doorkeeper, Solomonar.]
The Scholomance's current class is approaching graduation. Nine students will be released. The tenth will be retained as the new Weathermaker — the permanent faculty position the Schoolmaster considers the tuition's most elegant component. The tenth student has sent a message from within the mountain, begging for rescue, tucked inside a hailstone that struck a shepherd's cottage a hundred miles away. The Schoolmaster is aware the message was sent. He considers initiative a promising quality. It will not change his accounting.
¹ Gheorghe the Doorkeeper, asked about the Weathermaker position, describes it as perpetual employment in agreeable scholarly conditions. He has occupied it for decades. He did not elaborate.
title: The Scholomance — The Tenth Student

keys: The Fairy Mounds — The Open Door , the open mound ,
value:
The Fairy Mounds — The Open Door. [See also: The Sídhe Court, Finvarra, Oonagh, Brigid Ní Fhlaithbheartaigh.]
Brú na Bóinne — Newgrange — has opened. The passage tomb's entrance, which admits sunlight only at the winter solstice, is now permanently illuminated by cold light from within. People are walking in and not walking out. Brigid Ní Fhlaithbheartaigh stands at the entrance turning back everyone she can, but the light calls some people by name, and those people cannot resist it.
¹ The Sídhe Court has not sent a representative to the entrance. It does not consider an open door to require explanation.
title: The Fairy Mounds — The Open Door

keys: Brocken Peak — Walpurgisnacht in February , February's unseasonal Walpurgisnacht ,
value:
Brocken Peak — Walpurgisnacht in February. [See also: The Æsir Court, The One-Eyed Lord, Magister Albrecht Krone, Valhöll — A Rune Appears.]
A seasonal Fae gathering is occurring on the Brocken three months early. The Æsir Court is holding an unscheduled assembly, and the Brocken spectre has been visible for a week. Magister Krone's latest notebook entry is a single line: 'They are arguing about something. They are afraid.' Something has frightened a court that prizes courage above all qualities and considers fear aesthetically disqualifying.
¹ Cross-reference: Valhöll — A Rune Appears. The assembly and the rune are connected. The connection has not been explained to any mortal.
title: Brocken Peak — Walpurgisnacht in February

keys: The Stones of Carnac — The Humming , the Carnac humming ,
value:
The Stones of Carnac — The Humming. [See also: The Mélusine Court, The Sídhe Court, Yannick le Braz, The Brittany Question.]
The standing stones of Carnac have begun producing an audible harmonic tone — not quite music, not quite language, felt in the bones as much as heard. The frequency is rising. Yannick le Braz reports the korrigans are agitated, dancing more frequently and more violently, and refusing to explain why. The Mélusine Court has sent a notary to investigate, which reveals they did not expect this — the Court investigates surprises; it files everything else.
¹ The stones predate both courts' claims to Brittany. Whatever they respond to is older than either court's paperwork. The Chief Notary has opened a new file. Its title is not yet determined.
title: The Stones of Carnac — The Humming

keys: Lake Snagov — The Strigoi Procession , the Snagov walking dead ,
value:
Lake Snagov — The Strigoi Procession. [See also: Elena Barbu, Strigoi, Balkan Dragon-Scholar Magic, The Scholomance Court.]
A column of strigoi morți — the dead who walk — has emerged from the forest around Lake Snagov and is marching in formation toward an unknown destination. They are not attacking. They are not feeding. They carry objects that may be weapons or ritual implements. Elena Barbu's descântece cannot turn them — they are ignoring her entirely, which has never happened before. The procession is organised. Someone has arranged them.
¹ Elena Barbu has sent word to Andrei Stormwalker. She has not received a reply, which she records as 'also, it seems, a kind of answer.'
title: Lake Snagov — The Strigoi Procession

keys: Zugarramurdi Cave — Mari Speaks , the Basque cave pronouncement ,
value:
Zugarramurdi Cave — Mari Speaks. [See also: Mari, Maddi, Aitor Etxeberria, The Mari Court, The Weather War.]
Mari has spoken directly — not through Maddi, not through a sorgin, but from every cave mouth in the Pyrenees simultaneously. She said one sentence in Euskara and one in a language no living person recognises. The Basque sentence translates as: 'The old compact is broken. New terms are required.' No one has established what the old compact was or who the other party was.
¹ The Mari Court does not explain itself. This sentence is the explanation. It is being treated accordingly — with great care and considerable fear.
title: Zugarramurdi Cave — Mari Speaks

keys: Sacred Groves of Samogitia — The Aitvaras Plague , the Lithuanian aitvaras plague ,
value:
Sacred Groves of Samogitia — The Aitvaras Plague. [See also: The Romuva Court, The Thunderer of the Sacred Oak, Dalia Kazlauskienė, Vytautas the Flame-Carrier.]
Aitvaras — household spirits that bring stolen wealth — are manifesting across Lithuania at unprecedented rates. Households prosper briefly; then the aitvaras steals from neighbours, destroying community bonds. Dalia Kazlauskienė believes the Romuva Court is testing whether the population can manage the obligations of a pact relationship rather than simply accepting the benefits. The test is not proceeding well.
¹ When the fire was asked, it produced thunder and what Dalia describes as 'the quality of silence that follows thunder when the thunder was an answer.'
title: Sacred Groves of Samogitia — The Aitvaras Plague

keys: Elsinore — Something Wakes , the Kronborg awakening ,
value:
Elsinore — Something Wakes. [See also: Kapitajn Søren Madsen, The Æsir Court, Eiríkr the Walker.]
The vibrations beneath Kronborg Castle have become strong enough to crack masonry. Fish in the strait are dying. Ships report the water turned a deep, unnatural blue. Kapitajn Madsen's latest logbook: 'Whatever sleeps beneath us turned over in its sleep last night. Two sentries fainted. The castle dog will not stop howling.' The Danish government is sending an investigation. They do not know what they are investigating.
¹ The present author earmarks: Lore entry needed — What Sleeps at Elsinore. Eiríkr the Walker has been observed near the castle twice this month. He has not spoken to anyone.
title: Elsinore — Something Wakes

keys: The Pindus Mountains — The Neraides' Price , the mountain fairy's price ,
value:
The Pindus Mountains — The Neraides' Price. [See also: Kyriakos Papadimitriou, The Olympian Court, The Huntress.]
The neraides supporting Kyriakos's klepht band have named their price for continued assistance: a mortal man must marry into their community permanently. Kyriakos has a volunteer — a young fighter who has fallen in love with a neraida's voice. The Filiki Eteria leadership is divided. The neraides say this is not a request. The term they used for what it is translates, approximately, as 'a statement of terms already agreed to at the first cup of water shared, which you have perhaps forgotten, and which we have not.'
¹ Kyriakos has since reviewed his band's water supply with new attention.
title: The Pindus Mountains — The Neraides' Price

keys: Venice — Strange's Mirror Experiments , Strange's Venetian mirror-work ,
value:
Venice — Strange's Mirror Experiments. [See also: Jonathan Strange, The Gentleman with the Thistle-Down Hair, Flora Greysteel, Lord Byron.]
Strange's work with Venetian mirrors is producing effects beyond his lodgings. Canal reflections move independently of what they reflect. The Gentleman's face has been glimpsed in a dozen reflective surfaces — watching, and gone before certainty. Italian practitioners report interference between Strange's English mirror-magic and Mediterranean protective frameworks. Flora Greysteel's reflection occasionally moves a half-second ahead of her. Byron reports the masked balls feel 'haunted by a music no one can quite hear.'
¹ The Gentleman has not visited Venice. His face in the mirrors travels along a road that Strange has inadvertently widened.
title: Venice — Strange's Mirror Experiments

keys: Starecross Hall — The Captive Speaks in Patterns , Lady Pole's coded speech ,
value:
Starecross Hall — The Captive Speaks in Patterns. [See also: Lady Pole, John Segundus, Lost-Hope, The Gentleman with the Thistle-Down Hair.]
Lady Pole's fairy-tale eruptions — the nonsensical stories that replace her speech when she attempts to describe her captivity — have begun to exhibit structure. Segundus has found they follow a sequence: the same tales recur in a specific order, and mapped together, they describe Lost-hope's interior — its ballrooms, corridors, and the location of the Raven King's throne. She is smuggling intelligence through the enchantment's aesthetic restrictions, using the tales it forces her to tell as a vehicle for information it was not designed to suppress.
¹ The enchantment gags its captives in the court's aesthetic register. It did not account for a captive with nine years to study that register.
title: Starecross Hall — The Captive Speaks in Patterns

keys: Ashfair — The Grave That Grows , the growing grave at Ashfair ,
value:
Ashfair — The Grave That Grows. [See also: Arabella Strange, Jonathan Strange, Fetch, The Raven King's Court, Genius Loci.]
The churchyard plot at Ashfair where the moss-oak fetch was buried is producing anomalous growth — moss in concentric rings, a yew's roots bending toward the plot, grass moving in a direction unrelated to the wind. The vicar has written to the Bishop. The Bishop has not replied. A piece of Faerie — ancient wood animated by fairy magic — is buried in English soil, and the genius loci of Ashfair are debating whether to absorb it or expel it.
¹ Strange is in Venice and does not know. The grave is making a decision about itself that no living magician has authorised.
title: Ashfair — The Grave That Grows

keys: The Singing Stone of Karelia — The Origin Deepens , the Karelian origin deepening ,
value:
The Singing Stone of Karelia — The Origin Deepens. [See also: The Väki Court, Old Matti Karjalainen, The Singing Void, Tietäjä.]
The glacial erratic in eastern Finland that has been producing origin-charms is intensifying. The sound has shifted to the origin of origins — a recursive incantation describing the moment before the first thing existed. Runo-singers who listen too long lose the ability to perceive individual objects: everything dissolves into its component origins. Old Matti Karjalainen has been sitting beside it for three weeks and has stopped eating, though he does not appear to be losing weight.
¹ The Väki Court is producing something through the stone. No tietäjä alive knows what. The question presupposes the Väki Court distinguishes production from the natural state of things, which it may not.
title: The Singing Stone of Karelia — The Origin Deepens

keys: Lost-hope — The Empty Throne Glows , the glowing throne of the Raven King ,
value:
Lost-hope — The Empty Throne Glows. [See also: John Uskglass, The Gentleman with the Thistle-Down Hair, Vinculus, Jonathan Strange.]
Deep inside Lost-hope, past the ballrooms, a chamber the Gentleman never enters contains a throne of black stone carved with ravens. The throne has begun to glow — cold light the Gentleman has noticed and is pretending is beneath his notice. The throne belongs to the Raven King. Its activation indicates the spell being worked through Strange and Norrell is approaching completion. The Gentleman's nightly balls have become erratic. His captives report he is distracted in a way he has not previously been.
¹ He considers the throne's former owner 'a rather excellent predecessor, from whom one has naturally learned a great deal.' He has not sat in the chair. He is aware it would not receive him.
title: Lost-hope — The Empty Throne Glows

keys: The Hollow Hills — The Tithe Approaches , the approaching Sídhe tithe ,
value:
The Hollow Hills — The Tithe Approaches. [See also: The Sídhe Court, Finvarra, Oonagh, Changeling Returned, Fairy Debt.]
It is nearly seven years since the last fairy tithe — the mortal sacrifice the old ballads record as the Sídhe's payment. Finvarra is selecting which captive will be given. Rumour within the court suggests the chosen is not a random stolen child but someone whose sacrifice will have political consequences in the mortal world. The court is uneasy in the way the Sídhe are uneasy — with perfect composure and a quality of protocol-observance that increases precisely as the underlying situation worsens.
¹ The tithe is presented here on the authority of tradition, as Clarke treats it — as established court practice reported by ballad rather than as mechanically confirmed fact.
title: The Hollow Hills — The Tithe Approaches

keys: Valhöll — A Rune Appears , the unknown rune on Yggdrasil ,
value:
Valhöll — A Rune Appears. [See also: The Æsir Court, The One-Eyed Lord, Brocken Peak — Walpurgisnacht in February.]
A new rune has appeared carved into the trunk of Yggdrasil — one not in the original set. The One-Eyed Lord did not carve it. He does not recognise it. This is, for the Æsir Court, equivalent to finding a new letter in the alphabet overnight — except the alphabet in question is the structural mechanism of reality. He has sent the ravens to observe it from every angle. They reported accurately. He remains uncertain what he is looking at, which is a condition he has not experienced in several centuries and finds genuinely interesting.
¹ Cross-reference: Brocken Peak — Walpurgisnacht in February. The unscheduled assembly and the rune are connected.
title: Valhöll — A Rune Appears

keys: Tuonela — The River Thins , the thinning of the Finnish river ,
value:
Tuonela — The River Thins. [See also: The Väki Court, The Echo-Child, The Singing Void, Finno-Ugric Origin-Song Magic.]
The dark river separating the mortal world from Tuonela is running shallow — now wadeable at certain points by ordinary mortals. The dead are occasionally visible on the near bank. The Echo-Child is agitated, repeating visitors' words with increasing distortion. Tietäjät who visit report the Echo-Child's reflections are now showing futures rather than intentions — a shift from revealing hidden motives to revealing hidden outcomes.
¹ The Singing Void is audible at the near bank. It has never previously been audible above ground.
title: Tuonela — The River Thins

keys: Buyan — The Parliament Deadlocks , the Białowieża parliamentary deadlock ,
value:
Buyan — The Parliament Deadlocks. [See also: The Parliament of Domains, The Thunder-Voiced, The Cattle-Lord, The Weaver of Fate, Dedushka Petr.]
The Parliament has been in continuous session for three weeks over a territorial dispute between the leshy of Białowieża and the vodyanoy of the Bug River. The real issue is precedent: whoever wins establishes how domain boundaries are redrawn now that old territories are reasserting themselves. The Thunder-Voiced and the Cattle-Lord are on opposite sides. The Weaver of Fate is mediating. Nothing is resolving. Dedushka Petr has stopped offering bread and salt to visitors, which he only does when the situation is beneath the dignity of hospitality.
¹ See Lore: Inter-Court Politics — the Parliament-Romuva border conflict is contributing to the deadlock.
title: Buyan — The Parliament Deadlocks

keys: Romuva Grove — The Fire Changes Color , the colour-changed Lithuanian fire ,
value:
Romuva Grove — The Fire Changes Color. [See also: The Romuva Court, The Thunderer of the Sacred Oak, Dalia Kazlauskienė, Laima.]
The sacred fire at Romuva, burning amber since its rekindling, has turned blue. No tradition Dalia Kazlauskienė has inherited mentions a blue fire. The Thunderer of the Sacred Oak is present at the grove for the first time since magic's return, standing before the blue flame and saying nothing. In the Romuva tradition, when the thunder-being says nothing, this is itself a statement.
¹ Vytautas the Flame-Carrier has arrived and cannot determine whether the lantern he carries from the daughter-fires should be relit from the blue flame or kept separate. He is waiting for guidance. The fire has not provided it.
title: Romuva Grove — The Fire Changes Color

keys: The Scholomance — A Graduate Returns , the returned Scholomance graduate ,
value:
The Scholomance — A Graduate Returns. [See also: The Schoolmaster, Andrei Stormwalker, The Librarian, Balkan Dragon-Scholar Magic.]
A solomonar who graduated decades ago has returned to the Scholomance — raving, his dragon dead, his talisman shattered. He describes something encountered in the Carpathian passes: neither mortal, nor Fae, nor dead, nor alive. The Schoolmaster has locked the gates. No students in; no students out. The Scholomance is, for the first time anyone recalls, frightened. The Librarian is pulling texts that predate the Scholomance's founding by centuries.
¹ The solomonar's specific words before incoherence are earmarked to Lore: What the Graduate Found in the Carpathians.
title: The Scholomance — A Graduate Returns

keys: Cave Beneath Olympus — The Lord of the Shining Mountain Weeps , the Olympian lord's inexplicable weeping ,
value:
Cave Beneath Olympus — The Lord of the Shining Mountain Weeps. [See also: The Olympian Court, The Lord of the Shining Mountain, The Grey-Eyed Counselor.]
The being called Zeus is weeping. Thunderstorms have wracked Greece for a week. He does not weep. He rages, celebrates, pursues and abandons. He does not weep. Something — a message, a vision, a memory — has broken his composure in a way none of his courtiers have previously observed. The Grey-Eyed Counselor is investigating. She has not shared her findings. Her expression, rarely unguarded, is.
¹ He weeps not from grief in any mortal sense, but because the situation is, to him, a considerable aesthetic disappointment. The nature of the disappointment is not yet established.
title: Cave Beneath Olympus — The Lord of the Shining Mountain Weeps

keys: Cave of Mari — The Seasonal Migration Fails , Mari's failed seasonal migration ,
value:
Cave of Mari — The Seasonal Migration Fails. [See also: Mari, The Mari Court, Andrei Stormwalker, The Weather War, Maddi.]
Mari's seasonal migration between peaks has stalled. The Pyrenean weather patterns are in disarray. Andrei Stormwalker reports his storm-commands meeting resistance from a geological direction — the Scholomance's storm-craft and Mari's weather-being are interfering across southern Europe. Maddi says Mari is waiting for something. The tradition has no name for this condition, because Mari does not wait. She is the mountain. Maddi says this one does, now.
¹ See: The Weather War. See: Zugarramurdi Cave — Mari Speaks. The migration failure and the broken compact are related.
title: Cave of Mari — The Seasonal Migration Fails

keys: The Grimoire Vault — A Contract Is Contested , the contested Mélusine contract of 1347 ,
value:
The Grimoire Vault — A Contract Is Contested. [See also: The Mélusine Court, The Chief Notary, Maître Lefebvre, Fairy Debt.]
A family in Poitou has discovered their ancestor signed a Mélusine Court contract in 1347 requiring the delivery of the seventh-generation firstborn daughter 'into the court's service.' The seventh generation is now. The daughter is twelve. The Chief Notary says the contract is valid. The terms are clear. Five centuries of forgetting do not constitute grounds for renegotiation. Maître Lefebvre has found three procedural obstacles. The Chief Notary has already filed the counter-filings.
¹ A lawyer who can identify what the Chief Notary has not anticipated may be more useful here than any magician.
title: The Grimoire Vault — A Contract Is Contested

keys: The King's Roads — The Light Grows Brighter , the Narcissist's expanding bright zone ,
value:
The King's Roads — The Light Grows Brighter. [See also: The Narcissist, Brother Ezekiel Thorne, The Gentleman with the Thistle-Down Hair, The Raven King's Court.]
The Narcissist's section of the King's Roads is expanding. The bright zone — once a curiosity — is now visible from intersections it was never visible from before. The Narcissist is not expanding deliberately. Tambora's famine has driven millions across Europe to prayer, and prayer is its sustenance. The other courts have noticed. In London, Brother Ezekiel Thorne's visions are intensifying in direct proportion to the light's expansion, the content becoming more specific, more demanding, and harder to distinguish from his own thoughts.
¹ The Roads' original maker is, as far as anyone can determine, not involved in the light's expansion. He is occupied with other matters. These are converging.
title: The King's Roads — The Light Grows Brighter

keys: London — The Thames Speaks , the speaking Thames ,
value:
London — The Thames Speaks. [See also: Jonathan Strange, Genius Loci, English Practical Magic, Mrs. Amelia Carstairs.]
The River Thames has spoken — a single deep grinding syllable heard simultaneously within a quarter-mile, shattering windows in Westminster and stopping every clock in Southwark. It has not repeated. No one knows what it said. Mrs. Carstairs's network is analysing it. Strange was seen at the riverbank the following morning, pale and shaking, writing furiously — in the King's Letters.
¹ The Thames is among the oldest genius loci in England, bound by the Raven King's ancient alliances. When it speaks, it is worth attempting to determine to whom.
title: London — The Thames Speaks

keys: London — The Publishing War , the magical publishing war ,
value:
London — The Publishing War. [See also: Gilbert Norrell, Jonathan Strange, Henry Lascelles, John Childermass, The Friends of English Magic.]
Norrell's periodical and Strange's published History are a live conflict in February 1817. Lascelles has planted a hoax spellbook attributed to Strange — portions of which are disturbingly partially functional. A Strangite underground press in Cheapside produces unauthorised copies of suppressed texts. Drawlight is attempting to locate it. The player may be courier, investigator, or participant. Genuine suppressed texts are valuable resources. Helping Lascelles plant false information is morally compromised and practically effective. Neither option will feel clean.
¹ Information is the resource most worth controlling in February 1817. Norrell understands this. Strange did not, until he published.
title: London — The Publishing War

keys: Paris — The Catacomb Breach , the Paris catacomb breach ,
value:
Paris — The Catacomb Breach. [See also: The Mélusine Court, The Chief Notary, Monsieur Delacroix.]
A section of the Paris catacombs has collapsed inward, opening a passage into the Mélusine Court's Grimoire Vault at street level — beneath Monsieur Delacroix's bookshop. He has barricaded the entrance and is demanding hazard pay from parties he declines to specify. The Mélusine Court is filing formal protests. Parisian urchins are selling 'genuine fairy documents' pilfered from the opening. At least two are genuine. At least one creates obligations for whoever touches it.
¹ Delacroix has read enough Mélusine Court contracts to know his own employment arrangement does not cover this. He is negotiating a rider. The Chief Notary is interested in his approach.
title: Paris — The Catacomb Breach

keys: Vienna — The Musical Phenomenon , the Viennese musical phenomenon ,
value:
Vienna — The Musical Phenomenon. [See also: Doktor Sigismund Hartmann, Florica Lăutaru, Ludwig van Beethoven, Franz Schubert.]
A performance of Mozart's Don Giovanni at the Burgtheater produced a supernatural event: during the Commendatore scene, the stone guest actually appeared — living marble, speaking in a voice that cracked the plaster. The audience fled. The singer playing Don Giovanni has not spoken since. Hartmann's Commission classified the event as 'mass hysteria.' His private journal does not. Florica Lăutaru says something used Mozart's score as a summoning formula, and what it summoned has not left the theatre. It is waiting for the next performance.
¹ Beethoven was informed. He asked for the full account, listened without comment, and returned to his notebooks.
title: Vienna — The Musical Phenomenon

keys: St. Petersburg — The Neva Freezes Wrong , the wrong freezing of the Neva ,
value:
St. Petersburg — The Neva Freezes Wrong. [See also: Tsar Alexander I, Father Alexei Volkonsky, The Parliament of Domains.]
The Neva River has frozen in an unusual pattern — the ice formed a shape visible from the Winter Palace's upper windows. Father Volkonsky says it is a word in a Slavic script he does not recognise. The Tsar's advisors argue about whether to read it, destroy it, or pretend it is not there. Father Volkonsky, whose forty years of contemplative discipline now produces reliable supernatural perception, recommends reading it. A thing that writes itself in the capital's principal river is unlikely to be satisfied with being ignored. His recommendation has not been adopted. The pattern has not melted.
title: St. Petersburg — The Neva Freezes Wrong

keys: Constantinople — The Hagia Sophia Resonance , the Hagia Sophia's resonance ,
value:
Constantinople — The Hagia Sophia Resonance. [See also: Sultan Mahmud II, Elif Hatun, Yusuf Efendi, The Djinn Courts.]
The Hagia Sophia has begun producing a continuous harmonic tone — deeper and older than Carnac's humming, in a register that makes listeners feel simultaneously devout and terrified. Muslim, Christian, and Jewish worshippers all report the same experience: vast, ancient attention focused on them personally. Elif Hatun says the building is 'remembering what it was' — all its sacred purposes activating simultaneously. A building that has been Orthodox basilica, Latin cathedral, and mosque is remembering each at once, without hierarchy.
¹ Every denomination present has separately determined that the theological implications are not ones they wish to be the first to articulate.
title: Constantinople — The Hagia Sophia Resonance

keys: Rome — The Archive Speaks , the speaking Roman archive ,
value:
Rome — The Archive Speaks. [See also: Padre Tommaso Sforza, John Uskglass, Vinculus, The Raven King's Court.]
A grimoire in the Vatican's suppressed collection has opened itself. Sealed with wax, iron, and a papal blessing in 1493 — the seal is intact, but the book is open, pages turning slowly as if read by an invisible scholar. The text is in the King's Letters — the same script appearing on the street prophet Vinculus in northern England. The Vatican has a copy of the Raven King's book, and it is reading itself. Padre Sforza has been watching for three days and has not yet submitted his report, which he restarts each morning.
¹ The Vatican's copy appears to be reading further ahead than the copy currently written on Vinculus.
title: Rome — The Archive Speaks

keys: Berlin — The Rune-Stone Activates , the Berlin rune-stone activation ,
value:
Berlin — The Rune-Stone Activates. [See also: Professor Heinrich Brandt, Jacob Grimm, Wilhelm Grimm, The Æsir Court.]
A Viking-era runestone in the Berlin Antiquarian Society's collection has begun glowing. Its activation produces localised effects within a ten-foot radius: compulsive truthfulness, inability to carry iron, and a persistent sensation of being watched. Professor Brandt is professionally ecstatic. Jacob Grimm, summoned for an unrelated matter, passed through the room, stopped, read the inscription, and spent seventeen minutes writing in a notebook he has since declined to show anyone.
¹ The rune producing compulsive truthfulness was carved by the One-Eyed Lord. The present author considers its activation at this moment worth noting.
title: Berlin — The Rune-Stone Activates

keys: Edinburgh — Arthur's Seat Opens , the opening of Arthur's Seat ,
value:
Edinburgh — Arthur's Seat Opens. [See also: Dr. Margaret Buchanan, Walter Scott, James Hogg, The Sídhe Court.]
A fissure has appeared on Arthur's Seat, Edinburgh's extinct volcano, revealing a passage into the hill's interior. Warm air carries the faint sound of music. Dr. Buchanan has examined the opening with her Sight and reports a 'substantial fairy presence' within. The Edinburgh city council is debating whether to post guards, seal the entrance, or charge admission. Walter Scott has visited twice and taken notes. The fairy court within has not expressed a preference regarding any of these approaches, which James Hogg, from his knowledge of the tradition, considers more alarming than any direct communication would be.
title: Edinburgh — Arthur's Seat Opens

keys: United Kingdom — The Raven King's Roads Reopen , the reopened English fairy roads ,
value:
United Kingdom — The Raven King's Roads Reopen. [See also: The King's Roads, John Uskglass, Jonathan Strange, Captain James Fairfax.]
Fairy roads are opening across Northern England — through standing stones, crossroads, and certain doors that now lead somewhere they shouldn't. The roads connect locations across the Raven King's old territory, and people are wandering into them accidentally. Captain Fairfax's border patrol is overwhelmed. Strange says the roads cannot be sealed. Norrell says they should not have opened. The roads predate both of them and have no view on either position.
¹ The opening Roads are the physical manifestation of the Raven King's spell entering its final stages. The people wandering into them are part of the spell's mechanism.
title: United Kingdom — The Raven King's Roads Reopen

keys: United Kingdom — The Peninsular Legacy , the battlefield magic question ,
value:
United Kingdom — The Peninsular Legacy. [See also: Jonathan Strange, Lord Wellington, Gilbert Norrell, Military Officer.]
Strange served with Wellington during the Peninsular Campaign — raising a road from marsh, moving a river, creating diversions that preserved Wellington's flank. Wellington asked the famous question: 'Can you not simply magic away the French army?' Strange's answer — no — illuminates both the scope and the limits of practical magic as nothing else can. In February 1817, Waterloo veterans carrying this knowledge are everywhere across England. Officers at the Horse Guards are compiling classified reports. A memorandum arguing for a permanent magical corps is circulating.
¹ Strange's answer was 'no.' His working notes from the same period suggest he continued thinking about the question. He has not published those notes.
title: United Kingdom — The Peninsular Legacy

keys: Windsor — The King's Madness , the King's mysterious affliction ,
value:
Windsor — The King's Madness. [See also: George III, Jonathan Strange, Gilbert Norrell, Sir Walter Pole.]
George III is confined at Windsor. Is his madness fairy in origin? Circumstantial evidence: it began in 1788, during the last period of active practitioners before Norrell. His lucid episodes involve descriptions of things no one else perceives. Physicians have suppressed observations that his agitation increases during certain lunar phases and decreases when cold iron is placed near his bed. Accessing Windsor requires Sir Walter Pole's political connections and Norrell's reluctant cooperation — he investigated this himself and sealed the report. What a player finds may be the most consequential evidence of covert fairy involvement in English public life, or a genuinely unrelated medical condition. The ambiguity is the point.
¹ Childermass knows where Norrell's sealed report is.
title: Windsor — The King's Madness

keys: England — The Ladies Who Know , the ladies' magical network ,
value:
England — The Ladies Who Know. [See also: Mrs. Amelia Carstairs, Dr. Margaret Buchanan, English Practical Magic, The Strangite School.]
Women's magic in England operates entirely outside the Strange-Norrell debate. It was never institutional — transmitted mother to daughter, neighbour to neighbour, through household and garden and sickbed. The return of magic has made the question of visibility urgent: the Norrellites want to suppress or absorb it, the Strangites want to claim it as validation, and the women themselves have views on neither proposal that they have been asked. A network exists across England, Wales, and Scotland — quietly more accomplished than most practitioners the Learned Society has acknowledged. Access runs through Miss Absalom of Bath, who operates under the fiction that she is a folklore scholar. She is in truth one of the most accomplished practical magicians in England after Strange.
¹ Miss Absalom finds the fiction both useful and accurate, in the sense that all fictions about real things are accurate — they describe one aspect of the truth while declining to describe several others.
title: England — The Ladies Who Know

keys: York — The Learned Society in Crisis , the York magicians' crisis ,
value:
York — The Learned Society in Crisis. [See also: Gilbert Norrell, John Childermass, Jonathan Strange, John Segundus, Mr. Honeyfoot, Theoretical Magician.]
York — The Learned Society in Crisis is in acute crisis. Norrell's monopoly is fraying. Strange's publication has given dissidents a rallying point. John Segundus — the Society's most persistent and quietly stubborn member, who asked the question that began Norrell's public career and has regretted the answer ever since — is attempting to establish a school of magic at Starecross Hall, an undertaking Norrell's allies are working to prevent. Childermass's increasing independence suggests the entire arrangement is structurally compromised. The Society's members each hold individual positions on the great debate; several have genuine minor magical abilities; all have access to institutional apparatus and embarrassing commitments in their published theoretical work. Between February 1817 and the Childermass defiance scene — which is coming — the Society is a live political institution whose members' positions can be moved.
¹ Childermass has already decided what he will do at the end. He is waiting for the right moment. The Society does not know this. Segundus suspects it.
title: York — The Learned Society in Crisis

keys: Austria — The Táltos War , the Hungarian spirit war ,
value:
Austria — The Táltos War. [See also: Táltos, Táltos-Born, Hungarian, Finno-Ugric Origin-Song Magic.]
Two Hungarian táltos have entered combat — projecting their spirits as a white bull and a black bull over the Hungarian plain. The storms generated by their battle are devastating farmland across three counties. Habsburg authorities are attempting to arrest both parties but cannot locate their physical bodies, which lie in trance elsewhere. Doktor Hartmann has been dispatched and is documenting what he cannot explain, with the methodical honesty that makes his private journal considerably more useful than his official reports.
¹ Hartmann's private journal entry for this assignment begins: 'The instruments do not work here. Neither does my methodology. I am nonetheless recording what I observe, on the grounds that a record of what does not work is also a kind of knowledge.'
title: Austria — The Táltos War

keys: Russia — The Leshy Territorial Expansion , the expanding Russian forests ,
value:
Russia — The Leshy Territorial Expansion. [See also: The Parliament of Domains, The Old Man of Białowieża, Marfa Ivanovna, Slavic Whisper Magic.]
The leshy of the Białowieża Forest has extended his territorial claim to encompass settlements previously outside his domain. Farmers wake to find their fields bordered by trees that weren't there the night before. Marfa Ivanovna reports this is happening across Russia — every leshy is expanding, and the forests are remembering how large they used to be before human settlement reduced them. The Parliament of Domains is arguing about it. The Russian Imperial administration has no mechanism for responding to a territorial claim by an entity that does not recognise administrative frameworks.
¹ The Old Man of Białowieża considers this progress. He does not consider it fast.
title: Russia — The Leshy Territorial Expansion

keys: Prussia — The Grimm Emergency , the Prussian fairy tale emergency ,
value:
Prussia — The Grimm Emergency. [See also: Jacob Grimm, Wilhelm Grimm, The Æsir Court, Leutnant Karl von Steinberg.]
Jacob Grimm has sent an urgent letter to the Prussian War Ministry: a tale collected from a Hessian grandmother has activated. A Napoleonic veteran in the village where the story was collected is now living its plot, step by step, unable to deviate. Grimm believes the return of magic has made collected folk tales performative — stories become spells. If he is right, every fairy tale ever collected is a potential magical event. The Brothers Grimm have been summoned to Berlin.
¹ Some of the Grimm brothers' informants were not recalling old stories but reporting recent events they had no other vocabulary for. The distinction is now less theoretical.
title: Prussia — The Grimm Emergency

keys: Spain — The Inquisition's Dilemma , the Spanish ecclesiastical difficulty ,
value:
Spain — The Inquisition's Dilemma. [See also: Ferdinand VII, Padre Diego Montero, The Mari Court, Pétalo Heredia.]
The Holy Office has arrested a bruja in Logroño. During her trial, she performed genuine magic: she called fire from an empty hearth, and the fire obeyed. The Inquisition's entire framework assumes magic is demonic fraud. This was neither fraud nor, apparently, demonic. Padre Montero has sent a confidential report to Madrid. Ferdinand VII has not responded. The bruja remains in custody. The fire she called still burns in the tribunal's hearth, refusing to be extinguished. Three theological memoranda on its classification do not agree. The fire has not been informed of any of them.
title: Spain — The Inquisition's Dilemma

keys: Ottoman Empire — The Jinn Question , the Ottoman djinn difficulty ,
value:
Ottoman Empire — The Jinn Question. [See also: The Djinn Courts, The Olympian Court, Yusuf Efendi, Elif Hatun.]
Ottoman scholars propose the European 'Fae courts' are simply jinn — already accounted for in Islamic theology. The Olympian Court is offended. The Djinn are offended by both sides — the scholars for reducing them to a category, the Olympians for denying kinship with beings older than their own court. The Djinn have sent word through Khalid ibn Rashid that they predate both the Olympians and the theological framework being used to classify them, and that anyone who calls a Djinn 'simply' anything has not been paying attention for several thousand years.
¹ Yusuf Efendi is attempting to mediate. He has found that the parties' inability to agree on what they are arguing about is itself a form of argument, which he is documenting for the Sultan.
title: Ottoman Empire — The Jinn Question

keys: Sweden-Norway — The Draugr Rising , the Scandinavian walking dead ,
value:
Sweden-Norway — The Draugr Rising. [See also: The Æsir Court, Eiríkr the Walker, Fru Astrid Lindqvist, Veteran.]
Draugar — the corporeal Norse undead — are emerging from grave-mounds across Norway and Sweden. They are not attacking. They stand at barrow entrances, looking out at a world they do not recognise. Some have been dead for centuries. They appear to be waiting for orders from a commander who has not yet arrived. Fru Lindqvist's charms can hold them at bay but not banish them. Several draugar have looked at passing Napoleonic veterans with an expression she describes as 'a professional recognition that crosses several centuries.'
¹ See: Valhöll — A Rune Appears. The draugar's emergence and the unknown rune are likely connected.
title: Sweden-Norway — The Draugr Rising

keys: Papal States — The Benandanti Return , the Italian spirit-fighters' return ,
value:
Papal States — The Benandanti Return. [See also: Benandante, Caul-Born, Mediterranean Eye-Ward Magic, Italian.]
In Friuli, hundreds of caul-born Italians are reporting spirit-battle experiences on Ember Days — far more than historical accounts describe, and the malandanti they fight appear, for the first time, to be organised. The Vatican cannot classify the reports: not demonic possessions, not saintly visions, not heresy in any recognisable form. An organised supernatural militia fighting an organised supernatural enemy, and the Church has no doctrine for either side. Padre Sforza's cross-referencing of Inquisition records has found accounts of previous intensifications that were suppressed rather than investigated.
¹ Who or what has organised the malandanti is not answered in any source the present author has found. The benandanti do not know. They report only that the opposition has changed.
title: Papal States — The Benandanti Return

keys: Denmark-Iceland — The Galdrabók Plague , the Icelandic stave plague ,
value:
Denmark-Iceland — The Galdrabók Plague. [See also: The Æsir Court, Bjarni Jónsson, Norse-Germanic Rune Craft, Sigríður Kolsdóttir.]
Icelandic galdrastafir — protective staves — are appearing spontaneously across Iceland: carved into doorposts, scratched into windowsills, etched into the ice on ponds. No one is carving them. Bjarni Jónsson has identified them as protective staves from the Galdrabók tradition — as if Iceland itself is warding its population against something anticipated. The Æsir Court, when Bjarni inquired, responded with a silence he interprets as: 'your inquiry is correct,' and a second silence he interprets as: 'do not ask what is coming.'
¹ The staves' handwriting is consistent across all instances. Bjarni has compared them and has not yet shared his conclusion about whose hand it resembles.
title: Denmark-Iceland — The Galdrabók Plague

keys: Lithuania — The Fire Spreads , the spreading Lithuanian fires ,
value:
Lithuania — The Fire Spreads. [See also: The Romuva Court, Vytautas the Flame-Carrier, Dalia Kazlauskienė, Juozas Vaitkevičius.]
The sacred fire at Romuva has spawned daughter-fires at six additional grove sites across Samogitia, each burning without fuel. Vytautas the Flame-Carrier moves between the sites with his sacred lantern. Russian authorities have dispatched a military detachment. Juozas Vaitkevičius is attempting to reach the groves before the soldiers, carrying notebooks documenting that the fires are benevolent. He and Vytautas have not yet met, but they are converging on the same clearings from opposite directions — one carrying scholarship, the other carrying fire.
¹ The military detachment's instructions do not specify what to do upon encountering a fire that burns without fuel and cannot be extinguished. This was an oversight in the drafting.
title: Lithuania — The Fire Spreads

keys: The Levant — The Debts Come Due , the Levantine djinn debts ,
value:
The Levant — The Debts Come Due. [See also: The Djinn Courts, The Ifrit of the Pillars, Khalid ibn Rashid, Zahra bint Yusuf.]
Across the eastern Mediterranean, families are receiving nighttime visitors — columns of heat in cold rooms, whispered demands in languages their great-grandparents spoke. The Djinn are collecting. A Damascus family promised the firstborn of every seventh generation. An Aleppo merchant house pledged trade route profits. A Lebanese village swore seasonal offerings at a spring paved over in 1650. Khalid ibn Rashid is overwhelmed with petitioners. Zahra bint Yusuf works without sleep reconstructing the Solomonic seals. The Djinn are not unreasonable. They are exact.
¹ The Djinn's memory of a compact predates the conversion offered as grounds for its cancellation. They find this argument unpersuasive. They have made this clear.
title: The Levant — The Debts Come Due

keys: Greece — The Olympian Price , the Greek Olympian bargain ,
value:
Greece — The Olympian Price. [See also: The Olympian Court, The Grey-Eyed Counselor, Kyriakos Papadimitriou, Hermes-Who-Was.]
The Filiki Eteria has discovered that the Olympian Court's support comes with terms the independence movement did not anticipate. The Grey-Eyed Counselor has presented them: when independence is achieved, the new Greek state must formally acknowledge the Olympian Court's existence — not worship, but diplomatic recognition. The Filiki Eteria leadership is horrified. The mountain fighters need the neraides to survive. Every day the rebellion grows more dependent on supernatural assistance it cannot afford to lose, and the terms of that assistance are becoming more specific.
¹ The Grey-Eyed Counselor presented these terms because she calculated the Filiki Eteria would accept rather than lose the military advantage. She calculated correctly.
title: Greece — The Olympian Price

keys: Poland — The Language of the Spirits , the Polish spirit language ,
value:
Poland — The Language of the Spirits. [See also: The Parliament of Domains, Slavic Whisper Magic, Polish.]
Poland does not exist on any 1817 map. But Polish practitioners have established that the Slavic spirits recognise Polish. In former Polish territory, a Polish zagovor outperforms a Russian one because the spirits' contracts are language-specific — made in Polish, before partition. The land still answers to Polish. Polish intellectuals in exile are spreading the word quietly.
¹ The Russian Imperial administration has begun documenting unusual charm efficacy rates in Polish-speaking communities. What they intend to do with this information has not been established. What the communities intend to do with the advantage has been more clearly articulated, in whispers.
title: Poland — The Language of the Spirits

keys: Ireland — The Fairy Doctors' Power , the Irish fairy doctors' resurgence ,
value:
Ireland — The Fairy Doctors' Power. [See also: Celtic Otherworld Magic, Brigid Ní Fhlaithbheartaigh, The Sídhe Court, Irish.]
Irish fairy doctors have established that their magic is stronger than English practical magic in the domain that matters most: direct Sídhe negotiation. The Sídhe respond to courtesy, the old forms, the language and offerings — maintained in Ireland where the English abandoned them four centuries ago. Norrell's institutional approach produces nothing at the Hollow Hills. A Galway fairy doctor's hazel wand and spoken charm produces immediate results. Brigid Ní Fhlaithbheartaigh has been invited to address the Learned Society of York Magicians. She has not yet decided whether to accept.
¹ The Learned Society's invitation specified 'a demonstration of Irish folk practices.' Brigid's reply specified what she would and would not demonstrate, and why. The exchange has not been published.
title: Ireland — The Fairy Doctors' Power

keys: Portugal — The Mouras Encantadas Stir , the Portuguese enchanted ladies ,
value:
Portugal — The Mouras Encantadas Stir. [See also: Iberian Blood-and-Stone Magic, The Mari Court, Iberian.]
Across Portugal's megalithic landscape, the mouras encantadas — enchanted women associated with dolmens and underground treasure — are stirring. They have been seen combing golden hair at river-mouths, offering travelers golden balls that turn to coal at sunrise — or do not, depending on the traveler. Their awakening suggests a Fae population predating all known courts, answering to none. The Mélusine Court has opened a filing: 'Pre-Documented Entities, Classification Pending.'
¹ The present author earmarks: if the mouras represent a genuinely independent Fae population, the number of courts in Europe has been undercounted. He considers this possibility with the measured calm of a man who has already revised his assumptions several times this year.
title: Portugal — The Mouras Encantadas Stir

keys: The Changeling Discovery , the changeling child affair ,
value:
The Changeling Discovery. [See also: The Sídhe Court, Oonagh, Fairy Doctors, Caul-Born, Fetch.]
A local family discovers their child is a changeling — the real child was taken, a fairy substitute left in its place. The changeling is sickly, strange-eyed, and growing visibly older. The family wants their real child back. The local Fae court wants the changeling returned before it expires, which would constitute a breach of their own protocol. Traditional methods for forcing a changeling to reveal itself carry genuine risk if the diagnosis is wrong. A fairy doctor or practitioner with the Sight can diagnose correctly. Without one, the family is working from folklore with regional variants, not all of which are accurate.
¹ The Sídhe do not always take children for malicious purposes. They take them because they want them. This does not comfort the parents but is operationally relevant.
title: The Changeling Discovery

keys: The Accidental Magician , the accidental practitioner ,
value:
The Accidental Magician. [See also: Street Performer or Conjurer, Genius Loci, English Practical Magic, The Strangite School.]
A completely untrained person — a baker, a cobbler, a washerwoman — has accidentally performed a genuine spell and cannot stop. The bread rises impossibly, the shoes walk by themselves, the laundry forms legible text. They are terrified. The local magical establishment is either dismissive or acquisitive. The person needs explanation, not exploitation. In England, the Norrellite network will attempt to suppress them; the Strangites will attempt to claim them. Neither response addresses what the person actually requires.
¹ This event is happening in variations across every country in this catalogue. The untrained practitioner is, in the Raven King's model, exactly what the spell is designed to produce.
title: The Accidental Magician

keys: The Dead Letter , the impossible letter ,
value:
The Dead Letter. [See also: John Uskglass, The King's Letters, The King's Roads, Fairy Debt.]
A letter arrives addressed to someone dead for decades — or centuries. Written in the King's Letters or the script of a local Fae court, its contents concern events that have not yet happened. The local postmaster remembers accepting it from a 'tall gentleman' he cannot otherwise describe. The letter is addressed to a name meaning something to a living person — a family name, a title, a role — and its contents are legible to anyone who can read the appropriate script. They are not comfortable reading.
¹ Letters on the King's Roads occasionally travel in both directions through time. This is not their designed function. It is a consequence of their age.
title: The Dead Letter

keys: The Iron Shortage , the iron problem ,
value:
The Iron Shortage. [See also: Blacksmith or Ironworker, Iron Vulnerability, Fae Courts Universal Principles.]
The local blacksmith's iron has stopped working magically — not mechanically, but as protection. Horseshoes don't ward. Iron nails don't seal. Something has neutralised iron's anti-Fae properties in this area, and the Fae know it. The community is unprotected, and whatever neutralised the iron did so deliberately — it planned to be there without iron's opposition. The blacksmith is anxious. His profession has become the most respected in any community for exactly this reason, and discovering that his core material is compromised is both professionally and personally alarming.
¹ No tradition records a mechanism for neutralising iron across a broad area without exceptional power and specific intent. The list of entities capable of it is short. The list who would do it is shorter.
title: The Iron Shortage

keys: The Fairy Market , the fairy market encounter ,
value:
The Fairy Market. [See also: Fairy Debt, The Mélusine Court, Merchant or Tradesman, The Bargain.]
A market appears overnight at a crossroads — stalls of impossible goods, vendors beautiful, strange, and evasive about prices. The goods are real. The cloth is finer than silk. The food is delicious. The prices are not monetary. The market will be gone by morning. Anyone who purchased something will discover within three days what the true price was. The vendors do not lie. They describe the goods accurately and the prices accurately — in terms the buyer will not understand until after the transaction is complete.
¹ The relevant question is not whether the goods are genuine but which court is running the market, what it wants, and whether what it wants is something the purchaser has already parted with without knowing it.
title: The Fairy Market

keys: The Bardic Duel , the inter-tradition contest ,
value:
The Bardic Duel. [See also: Inter-Court Politics, The Great Game, Fae Courts Universal Principles.]
Two magical practitioners from different traditions disagree about whose magic is real and whose is superstition. The disagreement escalates into a magical contest. Bystanders are caught in the crossfire. The deeper question: when two incompatible traditions clash, which court's rules apply? The answer varies by location — specifically by which court's territory the location sits in — and by whether either practitioner determined this before beginning.
¹ The most dangerous inter-tradition duels are not between practitioners who disrespect each other but between practitioners who genuinely respect each other and cannot agree on first principles. Contempt produces a shorter fight. Respect produces a longer one.
title: The Bardic Duel

keys: The Prophetic Child , the prophetic child affair ,
value:
The Prophetic Child. [See also: Vinculus, John Uskglass, The Raven King's Court, Seventh Son or Seventh Daughter.]
A child under seven begins speaking prophecy — in a language they do not know, describing events they cannot understand, with a voice not quite their own. The prophecy may come from a Fae court, a genius loci, the Raven King's spell reaching for instruments in the mortal world, or something else. The child is unharmed. The parents are frightened. The prophecy, once deciphered, concerns the player directly. The child will not remember any of this by morning.
¹ The Raven King's spell uses available instruments. Children under seven have not yet learned to resist unusual things, which makes them reliable channels. The spell is not cruel. It is thorough.
title: The Prophetic Child

keys: The Year Without a Summer's Toll , the famine bargain ,
value:
The Year Without a Summer's Toll. [See also: The Reawakening of Magic, Fairy Debt, The Bargain, Farmer or Rural Laborer.]
Famine, cold, despair. A community devastated by Tambora's aftermath is considering a bargain with the local Fae. The Fae are willing to help. Their price is reasonable. Their terms are precise. Someone needs to read those terms before the village agrees, because the court's price looks smaller than what the village is already losing — and this is correct — while also containing something the village cannot afford to lose — which is also correct. Both things are true simultaneously. This is exactly how Fae contracts work.
¹ The worst time to negotiate with any Fae court is under duress. This is also, in 1817, the most common time. The courts are aware of this correlation.
title: The Year Without a Summer's Toll

keys: The Wild Hunt Rides , the Wild Hunt encounter ,
value:
The Wild Hunt Rides. [See also: The Æsir Court, The One-Eyed Lord, Frau Holle, Norse-Germanic Rune Craft.]
The Wild Hunt tears across the sky — spectral riders, ghostly hounds, hooves on air. Those present must decide: hide (safest), stand and observe (risky — the Hunt may notice you), or attempt communication (extremely dangerous and potentially revelatory). Anyone caught in the Hunt's path risks being swept into its service. Anyone who successfully interacts with its leader may learn something no mortal is supposed to know, because he will share it if you interest him, and you will not know whether the information was a gift or a price until much later.
¹ The correct response is to bar your door and extinguish your lights. This response eliminates all possibility of communication, which has its own costs. Neither choice is without consequence.
title: The Wild Hunt Rides

keys: The Duel of Courts , the fairy court trespass ,
value:
The Duel of Courts. [See also: Inter-Court Politics, The Great Game, Diplomat or Government Official, Lawyer or Solicitor.]
A Fae from one court has trespassed on another's territory. The offended court demands satisfaction. The trespasser's court refuses to acknowledge the violation. Both courts are seeking mortal intermediaries — because Fae cannot lie, they require mortals to conduct certain kinds of diplomacy on their behalf. The player may be recruited by either side. Neutrality is not available: accepting an approach and declining to help is itself a position, and both courts will record it accordingly.
¹ Mortal intermediaries in inter-court disputes have a historically poor survival rate — not because courts harm them, but because resolution tends to alter the landscape in ways difficult to anticipate from one's current position.
title: The Duel of Courts

keys: The Frankenstein Moment , the reanimation affair ,
value:
The Frankenstein Moment. [See also: Gilbert Norrell, Lady Pole, Surgeon or Physician, Natural Philosopher.]
A natural philosopher or surgeon attempts to use magic to reanimate a corpse or create life from non-living matter. In a world where Norrell genuinely raised Lady Pole from death, the theoretical possibility is established. The practical results are unpredictable: what comes back may not be what died; what is created may not be what was intended. The cost of Norrell's successful resurrection is known — the bargain, what it produced, what it continues to require. A second attempt by a different practitioner, without Norrell's resources or the Fae's involvement, will produce different results. Mary Shelley's novel, published next year, will be fiction. This is not.
¹ The Gentleman's contract for Lady Pole contained terms Norrell did not read carefully. Any practitioner attempting independent reanimation works without a net, a contract, or adequate precedent.
title: The Frankenstein Moment

keys: The Returning Soldier , the soldier who came back wrong ,
value:
The Returning Soldier. [See also: Veteran, Changeling Returned, The King's Roads, Fetch.]
A veteran of the Napoleonic Wars returns to his village years after he was confirmed dead. He walks with a slight limp, speaks with a familiar accent not quite right, and doesn't remember things the way his family does. Is he a changeling? A revenant? A man who took a wrong turn on a fairy road and spent what felt like weeks in a place where years passed? The village is divided between joy, suspicion, and fear, and all three responses are reasonable given the evidence. The correct diagnosis requires someone who has encountered all three possibilities before.
¹ The roads recognise those who have been close to dying. Some veterans have walked the King's Roads without mirrors or intention, guided by something that recognised them.
title: The Returning Soldier

keys: The Book That Reads Itself , the self-reading book ,
value:
The Book That Reads Itself. [See also: Books of Magic, Books as Objects of Power, John Uskglass, Scholar or Archivist.]
A book in a private library, monastery, archive, or bookshop has begun turning its own pages. The text is changing — words rearranging into sentences that may be spells, prophecies, or messages. The book is trying to communicate. The question is not whether this is real — it demonstrably is — but with whom it communicates, whether the owner is the intended recipient, and whether what it is saying should be read aloud, which is the question a scholar will ask last and a practical magician will ask first.
¹ The Vatican holds a book currently reading itself in the King's Letters. It is not the only one. The present author knows of three others and is not certain those three are all of them.
title: The Book That Reads Itself

keys: The Roads Remember , the roads' long memory ,
value:
The Roads Remember. [See also: Romani Road Magic, Drabardi or Drabarni, The Fractured Sight, Small Alliances.]
A Romani drabardi sets up her table at a crossroads — three cards, a candle, a tin cup of tea. She offers a reading. It is genuine: her fractured sight shows the player's situation from temporal angles that reveal what is not yet known — a warning about a bargain's hidden clause, a glimpse of what the Raven King's spell is doing at this moment. The reading also enters the player into the oldest intelligence network in Europe — the small spirits with pacts to her bloodline are now aware the player exists. The drabardi finishes her tea, packs her cards, and is gone by morning. The sparrows remain.
¹ The drabardi does not explain what she shares. She delivers what the fractured sight shows. The player's interpretation is their own.
title: The Roads Remember

keys: Template: The King's Roads Encounter , the road encounter template ,
value:
Template: The King's Roads Encounter. [See also: The King's Roads, The Crossroads Collapse, Faerie's Geography, Changeling Returned.]
Three encounter categories for Roads travel. The Lost: a person who went wrong — lost for days (frightened, coherent); decades (adapted, speaking archaic language); or unwilling to leave (found something worth staying for). Guiding them out carries implications about how long they have been there and what they find when they return. The Things at the Margins: they do not approach directly; they call in familiar voices; the correct response varies by tradition and no tradition's response is fully reliable. The Court Intersections: two Fae road-networks overlapping — contradictory geography; a Fae from one court pursuing a Fae from another with the player between; or a junction into an unfamiliar court whose Fae have not yet decided whether the player is interesting or a problem.
¹ The Roads predate all current courts. Their rules are older than any court's rules. No authoritative source on what those older rules are has been found.
title: Template: The King's Roads Encounter

keys: Template: The Fae Aesthetic of Cruelty-as-Beauty , the fairy cruelty template ,
value:
Template: The Fae Aesthetic of Cruelty-as-Beauty. [See also: The Gentleman with the Thistle-Down Hair, Fae Courts Universal Principles, The One-Eyed Lord, The Schoolmaster.]
The player witnesses something a Fae finds beautiful that the player finds monstrous. The Fae is not doing this to the player. The player is simply present. The Fae is not evil — it is alien. Its indifference to suffering is genuine: it does not understand that suffering is happening, because understanding would require identification with the sufferer, which it does not possess. Intervention carries significant cost. The Fae's response will always be curiosity, amusement, or genuine bafflement that the player considers the matter relevant. Never guilt. Never shame. The concepts do not apply.
¹ This template is the most important in this document. Every Fae encounter should be legible through it. The Gentleman finds exhausted dancers beautiful. The One-Eyed Lord finds dead men at crossroads beautiful. The Schoolmaster finds retained students pedagogically satisfying. None are performing for the player's reaction.
title: Template: The Fae Aesthetic of Cruelty-as-Beauty

keys: Template: The Fairy Captive Discovery , the captive discovery template ,
value:
Template: The Fairy Captive Discovery. [See also: Lady Pole, Stephen Black, Starecross Hall — The Captive Speaks in Patterns, Fairy Doctor.]
The player encounters someone held by a Fae court who cannot communicate directly. Diagnostic approaches by tradition: English practitioners note the rose at the lips on attempted direct speech; Celtic fairy doctors check the shadow's slight wrongness; Romani practitioners use fractured sight to see the enchantment as an event already in the past. Each court's enchantment produces speech-substitution in its aesthetic register: the Gentleman produces fairy tales; the Mélusine Court produces contractual language with the captive's situation in the sub-clauses; the Scholomance produces academic discourse that, decoded, describes exactly what is happening. Rescue options scale to accumulated resources. Every rescue is incomplete. Every enchantment leaves a residue.
¹ Clarke's world does not produce clean retrievals. The player who breaks an enchantment finds a person who has been somewhere they cannot entirely leave.
title: Template: The Fairy Captive Discovery

keys: France — The Ankou's Census , the Ankou's Breton census
value:
France — The Ankou's Census. [See also: French Grimoire Magic, The Mélusine Court, Sergeant Émile Garnier, The Sídhe Court.]
In Brittany, the Ankou — Death's servant, replaced annually by the last to die in each parish — has begun visiting parishes ahead of schedule. He is not collecting the dead. He is counting the living, moving from house to house with his creaking cart and noting names in a ledger. This has never happened before. The Mélusine Court's notaries claim no knowledge of a census. The Ankou is associated with both Celtic Breton tradition and the Mélusine Court's administrative apparatus. Neither court has authorised a census. Neither denies knowledge of it.
¹ Sergeant Garnier's reports are being ignored in Paris, which he has come to expect.
title: France — The Ankou's Census

keys: Ferdinand VII , King Ferdinand , King Ferdinand, , King Ferdinand.
value:
Ferdinand VII. [See also: Spain, The Spanish Inquisition, Padre Diego Montero, The Mari Court.]
Heavy, unprepossessing build; dark eyes with the quality of a man who distrusts everyone by instinct; olive Spanish complexion; black hair; the formal dress of a Bourbon king who considers ceremony the first line of defence against a world he does not trust.
Restored Bourbon King of Spain. Restored the Inquisition in 1814. His suppression of liberal reform extends to magical practice — the Holy Office actively prosecutes practitioners. A bruja in Logroño who performed genuine magic before an Inquisition tribunal has created a crisis Ferdinand has not yet addressed. His silence on the matter is a political statement his officials are interpreting in several incompatible directions.
¹ The compiler notes that Ferdinand VII's theological advisors have produced three separate memoranda on whether genuine magic constitutes witchcraft, diabolism, or a third category requiring new canonical definition. The tribunal is waiting for guidance. The bruja remains in custody. No definition has been forthcoming.
title: Ferdinand VII
description: The King of Spain — suspicious, reactionary, and the man who restored the Spanish Inquisition. Magic is officially persecuted in Spain, and his Inquisition is arresting people for it. A current case — an actual witch who performed actual magic before the tribunal — has created a religious crisis no one at court knows how to resolve.
